- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f70aj0duk2tf71m22i3l4ivmv6dc9vbgoa@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<g6ksi0hkic10pjfv266lk70hmv22kmq4sl@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408262313.5c72daa5@posting.google.com> <6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <6dd8322.0408270924.40561400@posting.google.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408271341.5eab5a39@posting.google.com> <8vj1j0pccmom3kc3acrlaecdurco4e3bf0@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408282134.70db1ae9@posting.google.com> <9mr4j0lium86r4frssfb05t1o8odp0p424@4ax.com> <cgvvhl$1d50$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Mon, 30 Aug 2004 12:35:17 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>You've spent a lot of energy definding your position by describing all the
>ways in which you think roundING is broken, but not a lot of ways describing
>the ways in which you think its DESCRIPTION is broken in the standard.  How
>would you improve it?

By using Bankers' Rounding, which Jordan Wouk calls Nearest-unbiased.

I would NOT define a function. That defeats the convenience of using
pictures and COMPUTE. I'd make it a compiler option, like Standard
Math.

>And if it's not the *definition* of ROUNDED but rather its *use* (or, for
>that matter, the use of roundING through explicit calculations) that sticks
>in your craw, what are you suggesting that, for example, the banking
>industry do to correct this error that nearly a half-century of auditors
>have failed to catch?

They haven't failed to catch it, the problem is well known. Other
languages and databases that use Bankers' Rounding are Visual Basic,
hardly an obscure lanaguage, C# and all of .NET, JavaScript, Delphi
and Sybase.

It's called Bankers' Rounding because it is already standard practice
in the banking industry. No correction needed in that Unisys
stronghold.



>
>Personally, I think "ROUNDED" had better do exactly what the standard says
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T08:57:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch4rhp$1m4g$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<g6ksi0hkic10pjfv266lk70hmv22kmq4sl@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408262313.5c72daa5@posting.google.com> <6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <6dd8322.0408270924.40561400@posting.google.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408271341.5eab5a39@posting.google.com> <8vj1j0pccmom3kc3acrlaecdurco4e3bf0@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408282134.70db1ae9@posting.google.com> <9mr4j0lium86r4frssfb05t1o8odp0p424@4ax.com> <cgvvhl$1d50$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <f70aj0duk2tf71m22i3l4ivmv6dc9vbgoa@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:f70aj0duk2tf71m22i3l4ivmv6dc9vbgoa@4ax.com...

> >You've spent a lot of energy definding your position by describing all
the
> >ways in which you think roundING is broken, but not a lot of ways
describing
> >the ways in which you think its DESCRIPTION is broken in the standard.
How
> >would you improve it?
>
> By using Bankers' Rounding, which Jordan Wouk calls Nearest-unbiased.

I don't believe existing programs should suddenly exhibit different
behavior.

I have already gone on record as believing that it may be appropriate to ADD
an automated method of "banker's rounding" to the language, but I believe if
such is added to the standard it should be handled by explicit syntax
extending or analogous to the current ROUNDED phrase.

> I would NOT define a function.

I might, but I think I'd rather enhance ROUNDED.

> That defeats the convenience of using
> pictures and COMPUTE.

How so?  How is
    COMPUTE X ROUNDED-UNBIASED = <arithmetic expression>
"convenient" whereas
    COMPUTE X = ROUNDED-UNBIASED (<arithmetic-expression>)
"inconvenient"?

I can see the other arithmetics might be a little less convenient, but
COMPUTE?

> I'd make it a compiler option, like Standard Math.

No, I don't think that's a good idea.

If the behavior of the ROUNDED phrase is to be disparaged officially, the
right way to do this is to provide syntax for *all* the desired ROUNDED
options (including truncation!), and to make the absence of the *qualifier*
obsolescent.  Even that's unlikely to fly.

> They haven't failed to catch it, the problem is well known. Other
> languages and databases that use Bankers' Rounding are Visual Basic,
> hardly an obscure lanaguage, C# and all of .NET, JavaScript, Delphi
> and Sybase.

Ah.  But it's you that wrote that roundING is broken in COBOL and that
everybody was doing it wrong.

> It's called Bankers' Rounding because it is already standard practice
> in the banking industry. No correction needed in that Unisys
> stronghold.

Unless some programmer didn't do it that way.

What correction to an existing program is needed if the programmer, the
auditors, the CIO, the CFO, the CEO, the shareholders and Wall Street are
perfectly content with the company's financial reports (and financial
health)?  I contend "none".

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T18:22:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfubj05le0hlf4k5o5pmnftd1t3m9bj16u@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <6dd8322.0408270924.40561400@posting.google.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408271341.5eab5a39@posting.google.com> <8vj1j0pccmom3kc3acrlaecdurco4e3bf0@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408282134.70db1ae9@posting.google.com> <9mr4j0lium86r4frssfb05t1o8odp0p424@4ax.com> <cgvvhl$1d50$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <f70aj0duk2tf71m22i3l4ivmv6dc9vbgoa@4ax.com> <ch4rhp$1m4g$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Wed, 1 Sep 2004 08:57:45 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:f70aj0duk2tf71m22i3l4ivmv6dc9vbgoa@4ax.com...
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>behavior.

That happens every time an error is corrected.

>I have already gone on record as believing that it may be appropriate to ADD
>an automated method of "banker's rounding" to the language, but I believe if
>such is added to the standard it should be handled by explicit syntax
>extending or analogous to the current ROUNDED phrase.

I have no objection.

>> I would NOT define a function.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>"inconvenient"?

A function would not know the number of digits desired. Either the
programmer or compiler would have to pass that as a parameter.
Alternatively,  it could be defined as an integer function, which
would require the programmer to do scaling -- multiply and divide by
powers of 10.

I like the first sample. One could use the compiler's substitution
feature to equate ROUNDED with ROUNDED-UNBIASED.

One could make a stronger argument for
  COMPUTE X = <arithmetic expression> WITH STANDARD-ARITHMETIC

Perhaps I don't want ALL arithmetic standard because it's too slow. I
want only one hairy COMPUTE to be standard.

>What correction to an existing program is needed if the programmer, the
>auditors, the CIO, the CFO, the CEO, the shareholders and Wall Street are
>perfectly content with the company's financial reports (and financial
>health)?  I contend "none".

Statements like that remind me of a chemical manufacturing company
where I used to work. We got caught by one State for being underweight
on consumer products -- things like car wax -- and I was asked to look
into it. I went to the Production Manager and asked how he knew a 16oz
can really contained 16 ounces. He looked at me like I was from Mars
and replied "What do you mean? Use your common sense. Just look at it.
Doesn't it look full to you?" Being the diplomat that I am, I asked
whether he had a scale. He produced a $2 postal scale accurate, at
best, to plus or minus an ounce. Our 'violation' was a few hundredths
of an ounce. 

The CEO proposed filling cans the same, but changing the label to read
15oz. They we'd always be one ounce over (more or less). Who could
complain about that. Err, California Weights and Measures, for one.
Being over is as bad as being under. In fact, being dead on might not
be good enough. Deviation between samples has to be within a
prescribed limit. 

I helped set up a real QA operation and wrote software to evaluate
sample readings. The way to do it right, on a multi-head filling
operation, is to take samples when the line STARTS and adjust heads to
minimize deviation between then. By doing that, you don't have to
overfill as much to hit the target mean. If you wait 'till after a
batch is made, all you can do is throw it out if it's underfilled.

Production people thought we were meddling in their turf. Sometimes
they'd make a batch without telling us. We'd sample it a posteriori
and sometimes report it was underweight. Management's solution was
ship it to a State that didn't care or another country. That was
error-prone. We didn't track inventory by batch. Sometimes a bad batch
would go to a State that cared. In the end, we would up with a whole
warehouse of underweight products in California, private-labelled for
high-prestige car companies. Management's solution was to fire my
co-worker. He should have prevented that from happening. 

So my answer is NO, I don't think asking the CIO, the CFO, the CEO,
the shareholders and perhaps a few customers whether they're happy is
adequate QA.

As for Wall Street, the majority of financial numbers available on
your computer screen (say via Yahoo) come from one of four data
warehouses: Reuters, Thomson, Bloomberg and Morningstar. They each
have databases with billions of rows. I used to work at one of them,
processing reports from publically-owned companies and mutual funds. I
know they're not "perfectly content" with the quality of data they
recieve. Granted, 5/4 rounding is way down on the list of complaints,
but it's the only Cobol issue that came to mind. Higher up are column
headings that say 'in thousands' when the numbers aren't, or vice
versa, or some rounded (to avoid left-truncation) and some not, or
'rounded' to thousands and formatted as zzzz.999 (duh).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T12:24:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch57m8$1uge$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <6dd8322.0408270924.40561400@posting.google.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408271341.5eab5a39@posting.google.com> <8vj1j0pccmom3kc3acrlaecdurco4e3bf0@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408282134.70db1ae9@posting.google.com> <9mr4j0lium86r4frssfb05t1o8odp0p424@4ax.com> <cgvvhl$1d50$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <f70aj0duk2tf71m22i3l4ivmv6dc9vbgoa@4ax.com> <ch4rhp$1m4g$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <bfubj05le0hlf4k5o5pmnftd1t3m9bj16u@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:bfubj05le0hlf4k5o5pmnftd1t3m9bj16u@4ax.com...

> >I don't believe existing programs should suddenly exhibit different
> >behavior.
>
> That happens every time an error is corrected.

And maybe, just maybe, there is a body of users out there who use ROUNDED in
such a way as to satisfy themselves and their employers.  To change the
current behavior would be about as welcome as suddenly having the content of
A be 3 immediately after "COMPUTE A = 1 + 1"!

The current behavior of ROUNDED is documented, in the standards and in every
COBOL reference manual out there.  It isn't that it is *broken*, it's that
what it describes isn't the *sort* of rounding you think it should describe.

The standards committee may *archaize* a usage that is prone to producing
ambiguous or unexpected results -- as for example NEXT SENTENCE and MOVE of
alphanumeric figurative constants to numeric items in ISO/IEC 1989:2002, but
such elements are classified as archaic rather than obsolete because their
use in existing programs might be too extensive to warrant removal from the
standard.

What we are talking about here is having a *given* program compiled by a
compiler compliant with current standards suddenly producing *different*
results when compiled by a compiler compliant with a future standard.  That
happened early on -- I think it was with the '74 standard in some area -- 
and I was told that the standards committee members got *sued* by a company
who wasn't expecting the change.  Ain't gonna happen again.

Unless, of course, you are personally willing to guarantee that the results
after recompilation of all existing COBOL programs in the universe will be
correct *as the user defines it* after your recommended change, and are
furthermore willing *and able* to reimburse the users for any costs
associated with verifying those results, as well as any costs they might
incur if the results are not what they expected or desired.


> I have no objection.

Goody.

> >How so?  How is
> >    COMPUTE X ROUNDED-UNBIASED = <arithmetic expression>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> A function would not know the number of digits desired.

Might, depending on context.  Certainly could in the above example.


> I like the first sample. One could use the compiler's substitution
> feature to equate ROUNDED with ROUNDED-UNBIASED.

> One could make a stronger argument for
>   COMPUTE X = <arithmetic expression> WITH STANDARD-ARITHMETIC

Nope.  Standard arithmetic is already standard, and would be *broken*.  The
argument is not stronger, it's a disaster.

> Perhaps I don't want ALL arithmetic standard because it's too slow.

Today, on the machines you have available to you, maybe.  It's not clear
that that's the case forever.  I would hope to see some sort of hardware
implementations that would support SIDI's, and thus Standard Arithmetic, in
the not-distant future, though I have no knowledge of *specific* plans to
provide it.  I do know of at least one vendor who has promised a full 2002
COBOL implementation, and standard arithmetic is *not* a
"processor-dependent language element" in 2002 COBOL, a compliant
implementation *must* support it.

> I want only one hairy COMPUTE to be standard.

No, from what I can tell, it's the *rounding associated with the final
store* to allow an option *in addition* to the two options currently
provided.  That option is *not* standard today, in "standard arithmetic" or
otherwise.  What standard arithmetic does today, with or without the ROUNDED
clause, *is* standard.

> >What correction to an existing program is needed if the programmer, the
> >auditors, the CIO, the CFO, the CEO, the shareholders and Wall Street are
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> where I used to work. ....

Red herring.  If, in the view of everyone *but you* they have used (or NOT
used) ROUNDED correctly, why should they incur costs they might incur for
investigating the problem and revising the programs when the results *they*
obtain with *their* programs aren't what *they* want them to be?


> Granted, 5/4 rounding is way down on the list of complaints,
> but it's the only Cobol issue that came to mind.

For those analysts that object, the various programmers involved can
certainly implement the appropriate rounding in application code.  That the
specifications for ROUNDED don't happen to provide it is immaterial.   The
reason I say that is ...

> Higher up are column
> headings that say 'in thousands' when the numbers aren't, or vice
> versa, or some rounded (to avoid left-truncation) and some not, or
> 'rounded' to thousands and formatted as zzzz.999 (duh).

All of which are obviously *program errors*, not failures on the part of the
COBOL standards committee to meet the express wishes of the COBOL user
community.

Just as these are program errors, so is failing to recognize that there are
two, and only two, rounding options provided by *standard COBOL syntax*, and
they are well defined.  If the user wants to use a different rounding option
it is a trivial matter of application coding to do so.   As you point out,
applications programmers have been doing so for quite some time now.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T16:50:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409011550.54b529d7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408271341.5eab5a39@posting.google.com> <8vj1j0pccmom3kc3acrlaecdurco4e3bf0@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408282134.70db1ae9@posting.google.com> <9mr4j0lium86r4frssfb05t1o8odp0p424@4ax.com> <cgvvhl$1d50$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <f70aj0duk2tf71m22i3l4ivmv6dc9vbgoa@4ax.com> <ch4rhp$1m4g$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <bfubj05le0hlf4k5o5pmnftd1t3m9bj16u@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> >I don't believe existing programs should suddenly exhibit different
> >behavior.
> 
> That happens every time an error is corrected.

But there is no 'error' in Cobol.  The error was entirely in your
expectations that Cobol should do it differently for different sets of
data.

'Banker's rounding' is more accurate for rounding $.c to $ or to
1000$s, but is less accurate for MULTIPLY and DIVIDE.

It seems to be that you don't want a 'solution', what you want is to
_blame_ Cobol.  Perhaps you have been confronted that your report
doesn't work.  You reject all solutions that have it that the
programmer used the wrong mechanism and the code gives the wrong
results.

You want an answer that 'Cobol has a bug' or 'Cobol uses the wrong, or
inaccurate, rounding'.

You don't want to have to say: 'I (RW) don't understand rounding', you
want 'Cobol doesn't understand rounding'.

> Statements like that remind me of a chemical manufacturing company
> where I used to work. We got caught by one State for being underweight
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> of an ounce. 
 
Isn't that weird, you based your first of 'Cobol has a bug' on 'all
v99, v999, v999999' add up to the same total and 'looked at me as if I
was from Mars' for two or three times.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T14:05:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409011305.4c732d8b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<g6ksi0hkic10pjfv266lk70hmv22kmq4sl@4ax.com> <6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <6dd8322.0408270924.40561400@posting.google.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408271341.5eab5a39@posting.google.com> <8vj1j0pccmom3kc3acrlaecdurco4e3bf0@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408282134.70db1ae9@posting.google.com> <9mr4j0lium86r4frssfb05t1o8odp0p424@4ax.com> <cgvvhl$1d50$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <f70aj0duk2tf71m22i3l4ivmv6dc9vbgoa@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> >You've spent a lot of energy definding your position by describing all the
> >ways in which you think roundING is broken, but not a lot of ways describing
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> By using Bankers' Rounding, which Jordan Wouk calls Nearest-unbiased.

There are several different algorithms for transforming sets of
numbers from one range of precisions into another, with varying
results.

No single algoritm gives the best results in all cases.  It is the
programmer that should make an _informed_  choice depending on his
analysis of _each_individual_ problem.

Using 'bankers rounding' or some other will improve some calculations
(as does round-forward or pre-subtract) while causing errors in
others.
 
> I would NOT define a function. That defeats the convenience of using
> pictures and COMPUTE. I'd make it a compiler option, like Standard
> Math.

You seem to want some 'universal truth' solution that works for
everything.  There isn't one.  You have to apply different solutions
to different problems.

You didn't previously know this.  You thought that your use of the
wrong algorithm was a 'bug in Cobol'.

> They haven't failed to catch it, the problem is well known. Other
> languages and databases that use Bankers' Rounding are Visual Basic,
> hardly an obscure lanaguage, C# and all of .NET, JavaScript, Delphi
> and Sybase.

You should note that most languages have an array of techniques.  They
also have ceil(), floor(), truncate() and may have separate ones for
round() and unbiased().

Cobol uses 'nearest'.  This is not a 'bug', nor does it have a 'bug'.
 

> It's called Bankers' Rounding because it is already standard practice
> in the banking industry. No correction needed in that Unisys
> stronghold.

It is used for $.c to dollars, yes.  But should not be used for rate *
hours to payment (banks don't do that).
```

- **Message-ID:** `<ch5feq$23ig$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<g6ksi0hkic10pjfv266lk70hmv22kmq4sl@4ax.com> <6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <6dd8322.0408270924.40561400@posting.google.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408271341.5eab5a39@posting.google.com> <8vj1j0pccmom3kc3acrlaecdurco4e3bf0@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408282134.70db1ae9@posting.google.com> <9mr4j0lium86r4frssfb05t1o8odp0p424@4ax.com> <cgvvhl$1d50$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <f70aj0duk2tf71m22i3l4ivmv6dc9vbgoa@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409011305.4c732d8b@posting.google.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0409011305.4c732d8b@posting.google.com...

> You didn't previously know this.  You thought that your use of the
> wrong algorithm was a 'bug in Cobol'.

And not just a "bug in COBOL", but a "bug in the standard for COBOL".

> Cobol uses 'nearest'.  This is not a 'bug', nor does it have a 'bug'.

Actually, COBOL uses two:  "nearest" (when ROUNDED is specified) and "round
to zero" (aka "truncate") (when ROUNDED is omitted).  Both fall under the
description "rounding techniques", and both are included in lists of such
techniques.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-02T03:59:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sj5dj019de8iouq5v6rskft97btj8dj4q7@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <6dd8322.0408270924.40561400@posting.google.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408271341.5eab5a39@posting.google.com> <8vj1j0pccmom3kc3acrlaecdurco4e3bf0@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408282134.70db1ae9@posting.google.com> <9mr4j0lium86r4frssfb05t1o8odp0p424@4ax.com> <cgvvhl$1d50$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <f70aj0duk2tf71m22i3l4ivmv6dc9vbgoa@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409011305.4c732d8b@posting.google.com>`

```
On 1 Sep 2004 14:05:17 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

>There are several different algorithms for transforming sets of
>numbers from one range of precisions into another, with varying
>results.

You made a good point about Cobol rounding correcting truncation from
infinite precision. For example, readings of physical phenomena and
calculations.

Bankers' Rounding is best for integers that were not truncated. For
example, inventory counts and $.c. Also for numbers that were rounded
once before.

>No single algoritm gives the best results in all cases.  It is the
>programmer that should make an _informed_  choice depending on his
>analysis of _each_individual_ problem.

Ok, I'll concede that. 

>Using 'bankers rounding' or some other will improve some calculations
>(as does round-forward or pre-subtract) while causing errors in
>others.

Pre-subtract can cause serious errors when numbers end with zeros. For
instance, whole dollar amounts stored as $.cc, then rounded to $
(general ledger), or numbers in multiples of 100 rounded to thousands
(stock shares).

>It is used for $.c to dollars, yes.  But should not be used for rate *
>hours to payment (banks don't do that).

Banks do interest calculations on $.c, which is similar. 

The best technique for rate * hours depends on whether hours are
truncated or rounded.
```

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>

- **In-Reply-To:** `<cgvvhl$1d50$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<8a2qi094vn5ht6pfq3sa3rmvnpkihui5aq@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408260035.112a12a0@posting.google.com> <g6ksi0hkic10pjfv266lk70hmv22kmq4sl@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408262313.5c72daa5@posting.google.com> <6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <6dd8322.0408270924.40561400@posting.google.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408271341.5eab5a39@posting.google.com> <8vj1j0pccmom3kc3acrlaecdurco4e3bf0@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408282134.70db1ae9@posting.google.com> <9mr4j0lium86r4frssfb05t1o8odp0p424@4ax.com> <cgvvhl$1d50$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:

> "Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
> news:9mr4j0lium86r4frssfb05t1o8odp0p424@4ax.com...
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> as to rectify that error?

Given that there are several types of rounding, if COBOL is going to be
used for new development, a worthwhile addition would be qualification
of ROUNDED so that COMPUTE A ROUNDED HALF-EVEN, COMPUTE A ROUNDED
TOWARD-ZERO, etc.. could be coded.  This would accommodate the various 
rounding rules in a clear manner rather than forcing rather interesting 
coding techniques.  This might be one of those things that would have 
been very useful in 1970 or earlier but would be ignored because people 
have accommodated the need.  What is interesting is that I didn't learn 
about the multiple rounding rules until about 10 years ago so I have to 
wonder how many programs are improperly coded because IT assumed 
different rules for rounding that did those for whom the systems were 
designed.  I realize that I may just have missed something basic but I 
don't recall it from my course in Computer Programming and Numerical 
Analysis taken in 1960 - 1961 nor do I recall having seen anything about 
it in any of the various trade periodicals.  The only non-COBOL rounding 
rules I had to deal with were at Westinghouse when they had a savings 
plan where participation meant that hourly employees would have their 
pay rates adjusted to the nearest half cent.  Salaried employees had 
weekly/monthly rates adjusted to the nearest cent.

> 
>     -Chuck Stevens
> 
>
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<g6ksi0hkic10pjfv266lk70hmv22kmq4sl@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408262313.5c72daa5@posting.google.com> <6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <6dd8322.0408270924.40561400@posting.google.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408271341.5eab5a39@posting.google.com> <8vj1j0pccmom3kc3acrlaecdurco4e3bf0@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408282134.70db1ae9@posting.google.com> <9mr4j0lium86r4frssfb05t1o8odp0p424@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408301139.13a7858a@posting.google.com>`

```
On 30 Aug 2004 12:39:58 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>somehow added up to the same total.

Right. You win that point.

>> Rounding is supposed to maintain the average. 
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>not the result of valid experiment.

What if the numbers were not truncated, computed or rounded? I posted
a demo of that case to which you had no reasonable answer.

>> but it does in Cobol due to a bug.
> 
>No. Wrong.  The rounding mechanism is well defined and works
>identically in primary school textbooks, mechanical adding machines,
>C, Java, and Cobol.  

You're incorrect. It doesn't work that way in C# nor in JavaScript (I
don't know about compiled Java).

This looks like a Brooklyn Bridge defense. Everyone else is doing
wrong.

>> You try to explain away the bug by linking the two, by claiming that
>> rounding somehow recalls and corrects truncation error. 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>affecting the result of rounding, which is entirely predictable.

You've said that over and over. What's the point of truncating to
three digits followed by rounding to two? Why not just round in the
first place?


>You continue to say 'rounding error' when applied to an average. there
>is no _the_ rounding error. _A_ 'rounding error' is the difference
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>distributions.

Yes, and an infinite number of random errors average to zero. The
problem is Cobol rounding is not random. It pushes the average upward
always, unless the numbers happen to end with zero.


>Now here's the thing: read this 3 times, then come back tomorrow and
>read it again:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>     are _identical_      (in ideal conditions)

It didn't take that long. That's true only in the case where rounding
removes one digit. If we take the more common case of dollars.cents
rounded to dollars, there is an upward bias of half a cent.
>
>If the set of infinite precision reals adds up to the same as the set
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>How hard is that ?

It's based on a false premise -- that the truncation error is offset
by the rounding error. They are two separate issues that don't
necessarily cancel out.

>> When I present cases where there was no truncation to correct, you
>> brush them aside by saying the rules are different for integers. 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>technique to cater for the differences that you _should_ expect.

What does that mean .. in English? There is no reason rounding should
work differently on dollars rounded to thousands than it does on cents
rounded to dollars. As i pointed out in another thread, they're both
scaled integers at machine level.

>> Your argument is an appeal to ignorance. 
>
>I really don't know what to say without it seeming like an ad hominem
>attack ;-)

Just say I'm an idiot. You've done it before. :)

>Perhaps all this is a troll on your part to get me to throw insults at
>you.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>answers than Cobol does you can then present that here.

I have found a better definition. So have others who are serious about
the subject. It's called Nearest-unbiased.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Classic RW 2 (was: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T13:08:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409011208.4d5bc161@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408271341.5eab5a39@posting.google.com> <8vj1j0pccmom3kc3acrlaecdurco4e3bf0@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408282134.70db1ae9@posting.google.com> <9mr4j0lium86r4frssfb05t1o8odp0p424@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408301139.13a7858a@posting.google.com> <052aj0lmkgdi0r22g75jskjrqg8vv4se50@4ax.com> <qaudnWZHTdNPoKjcRVn-vg@comcast.com> <ce8bj0hlk042kkbs5c843cnrn2n2qosf0b@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> Because C# is an extension of C++ and JavaScript is a dialect of Java.

Geez, Robert.  It may be that you are not _deliberately_ trolling, but
you just have a natural flair for stating the things that they would
use as bait.

C# is _not_ 'an extension' of C++'.

C++ includes all of C and is derived from cflow.  C# is a new language
that uses much of the statement syntax of C but does not include any
of the things that would be C.

JavaScript is _not_ a 'dialect of Java' at all.  It was developed by
netscape and has some vague similarities, just as all C syntax,
vaguely OO languages would have.

It's marketing worked though.  The name was changed to JavaScript with
Sun's permission to fool people into think that they were related.

> For JavaScript:   well, oops, according to at least one reference,
> JavaScript does NOT use"bankers rounding" for its "normal" rounding. 
> 
> The Web site you posted above shows JScript under .NET doing Bankers'
> Rounding. 

JScript is a different language to JavaScript.  They may be similar
but JScript is MS's attempt to displace JavaScript.

> I thought it rounded to even because this site says so. 

It seems (depending on which 'this site' you meant) that you thought
something about Netscape's JavaScript because of what it said about
MS's JScript ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T02:56:21-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409010156.45f80e0e@posting.google.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> >It does. It maintains the average of the infinite precision original
> >real numbers.  With enough digits the average is close enough to
> >0.500.  Rounding maintains this with an average of 0.500.
 
> What if the numbers were not truncated, computed or rounded? I posted
> a demo of that case to which you had no reasonable answer.

Rounding, as used in Cobol, and in many other languages and in
mathematics works on numbers that are assumed to have an infinite
number of random digits following the point of rounding, as is usually
the case when doing calculations.

If you use this mechanism on numbers that are other than this, for
example if the digits (or implicit digits) are all zeroes or all 9s,
then the answer will not be a good representation of the original, and
simple rounding is not the correct tool.  Programmers should be
skilled enough to know what their tools can and cannot do and how to
choose appropriate tools.

> to which you had no reasonable answer.

I am not here to solve your programming problems.  However, I did
offer two mechanisms: round and forward, and pre-adjusting by
subtracting .499..5 to the rounding point.

If you don't find these 'reasonable' then I suggest that you look for
other alternatives, or perhaps attend a tutorial where they may teach
you how to solve such problems.

What you seem to require is a mechanism that simultaneously:

 *  rounds a set of dollar amounts to thousands based on 5/4 rounding
 *  maintains the exact total and average of the original
 *  adds up to the rounded exact total

You seem to not want to use bankers rounding or round and forward or
pre-subtract  0.49999..5 because the number must be the exact
thousands based on 5/4 rounding.

In other words you want something that the number system cannot
deliver.

Just who is being unreasonable ? 

> You're incorrect. It doesn't work that way in C# nor in JavaScript (I
> don't know about compiled Java).

A quick ferret on Google dragged up on JavaScript:

""" ---------------------------------
""Rounding a Number to a Number
""For a positive number X, Math.round(X) rounds to the nearest
integer,

That seems to be how Cobol does it.

""In my MSIE 4, for X positive or negative :-
""* Math.round(X) for half-integer X rounds towards plus infinity;
...
""Banker's rounding, for 0.5 to the nearest even number, is not used.

There you go, this doesn't use bankers.

""Most real numbers, in particular those with finite base-10
representations, ""cannot be held exactly in a Double. This is why,
for instance, ""Math.round(1.035*100) gives 1.03, but
Math.round(2.035*100) gives 2.04 and ""Math.round(1.045*100) gives
1.05; moreover, RoundToNdp(1.0035, 3) gives 1.004, ""but
RoundToNdp(1.035, 2) gives 1.03.

JavaScript only doesn't give the same result as Cobol because, for
example, 1.035 is represented in floating point by 1.034999... and not
because it works differently.

Certainly, many languages provide floor() and ceiling() as well.  Some
also use othertypes of rounding.


> This looks like a Brooklyn Bridge defense. Everyone else is doing
> wrong.

If rounding (as is used in Cobol) is consistenly defined by 'everyone'
to do the same job and the implementations meet that definition, then
where is the 'error' ?

I would suggest that it is with the programmer who used it without
knowing what it was defined to do.

> >This means that you _can_ truncate beyond the third digit without
> >affecting the result of rounding, which is entirely predictable.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> first place?

The point is that _YOU_ introduced the v999 truncation. _YOU_ brought
up the issue by first of all claiming that all v999, v99, v999999 add
up to average 0.500..

I had to go into detail to explain why these did not addup to the same
and why this was irrelevant to the result of the rounding.

Now you are confused about why it was being discussed.

The fact is that you can truncate illustrates that rounding neither
knows nor cares what is beyond the single digit that is used for
rounding.


> Yes, and an infinite number of random errors average to zero. The
> problem is Cobol rounding is not random. It pushes the average upward
> always, unless the numbers happen to end with zero.

No. Once again you fail to understand.  Once again you completely
ignore, willfully, what has been said to you over and over.

LISTEN TO ME:

Rounding is defined to operate on numbers with an infinite number of
random digits following the digit that it rounds on.

When used on a set of these numbers it DOES NOT change the average. 
This is provable.


> >Now here's the thing: read this 3 times, then come back tomorrow and
> >read it again:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> It didn't take that long.

Read it again. And continue to read it because you obviously don't
understand it yet.  You just m,ade a statement above that shows you
completely fail to understand it.

> That's true only in the case where rounding removes one digit. 

No that is not true.  That is wrong. Read it again.  This time
readwhat it says not what you thought it was going to say before
youread it.

> If we take the more common case of dollars.cents
> rounded to dollars, there is an upward bias of half a cent.

Dollar.cent values do not have an infinite number of random digits
after the rounding point.  Rounding neither knows nor cares about
this, it works as if they are there.

This is why it is called 'double rounding' and is a beginners error, a
programmers error. Your error.

I have already explained in detail exactly what happens when you do
this and how a _competent_ programmer copes with this.  For example by
using round and forward or pre-subtracting 0.0049999..5

What I am astounded by is that after 30 years of programming you are
still unaware of how numbers work and how to cope with this.  I would
expect this shortfall in skill and knowledge in a newbie with no
formal training.


> >If the set of infinite precision reals adds up to the same as the set
> >of rounded numbers (which it does under ideal conditions).  Then any
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> necessarily cancel out.

THEY DON'T 'CANCEL OUT' AT ALL.  You still need to go back and read
what I told you to read 3 times.

1. The rounded set (R) has an identical average to the infinite
precision set
   (P). (provable for ideal conditions)

2. Truncation discards digits and thus has a lesser value for set (T)
   (provable)

3.  ( R - T )  ==  ( P - T )   (bacause R == P )

4.  Rounding only needs n + 1 digits to give the rounded set (R)
    (definition)

5.  (P) -> (T n+1) -> (R n)   gives (R) == (P)

How hard _is_ this ?
 
> >> When I present cases where there was no truncation to correct, you
> >> brush them aside by saying the rules are different for integers. 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> What does that mean .. in English? 

If you cannot follow that then either you are trolling or I would
serious doubt your ability to follow such things are the Cobol
standard or a program spec.

> There is no reason rounding should
> work differently on dollars rounded to thousands than it does on cents
> rounded to dollars. As i pointed out in another thread, they're both
> scaled integers at machine level.

There is no difference in these two.  Neither have an infinite number
of random digits following the rounding point.  They have 3 and 2
non-zero digits respectively, then they have an infinite number of
implicit zeroes.


> >> Your argument is an appeal to ignorance. 
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Just say I'm an idiot. You've done it before. :)

It may yet come to that.

> I have found a better definition. So have others who are serious about
> the subject. It's called Nearest-unbiased.

Good. As you should notice it is a _different_ definition and is used
for different requirements.  Do not use it when wanting a rounded
result of a multiply or divide - it will give the wrong answer.

'Nearest-unbiassed' requires more digits than rounding.  It can be
implemented by subtracting 0.004999..5 from the dollar cent amounts
prior to using (standardCobol) rounding.

Perhaps you may recognise my 'reasonable' suggestion.
```

```
On 1 Sep 2004 02:56:21 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>the case when doing calculations.

Only if the calculation is division. Multiplication does not produce
an infinite number of digits right of decimal. For example, an
interest calculation multiplies nv99 (balance) by nv9 (rate). The
answer has precision nv999 and an average, as you point out, of
n.4995. If we round to cents with Cobol, the average creeps up to
.5000; if we use Bankers' Rounding, it remains n.4995.

If the account started with an even dollar amount, say $100.00, all
digits right of decimal are the result of interest calculation. This
causes a cascade effect. When we compute interest next month, THIS
month's interest will round up to .5005. 

>If you use this mechanism on numbers that are other than this, for
>example if the digits (or implicit digits) are all zeroes or all 9s,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>choose appropriate tools.

Realistically, how many are? I didn't know the was lower than .500
until you explained it.

>What you seem to require is a mechanism that simultaneously:
>
> *  rounds a set of dollar amounts to thousands based on 5/4 rounding
No.      
> *  maintains the exact total and average of the original
Yes, over an infinite set of samples. As close as possible with a
finite set.
> *  adds up to the rounded exact total
No. I can live with numbers that don't sum to the printed total. The
only method that fixes this is round and forward.

>You seem to not want to use bankers rounding or round and forward or
>pre-subtract  0.49999..5 because the number must be the exact
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Just who is being unreasonable ? 

People who argue 'that's what I learned in elementary school,
therefore it must be right'. Alternatively,

ARGUMENTUM AD NUMERUM

This fallacy is closely related to the argumentum ad populum.  It
consists of asserting that the more people who support or believe a
proposition, the more likely it is that that proposition is correct.

>That seems to be how Cobol does it.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>There you go, this doesn't use bankers.

I found the same by experiment. 

>""Most real numbers, in particular those with finite base-10
>representations, ""cannot be held exactly in a Double. This is why,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>because it works differently.

Java fixes that with a BigDecimal class. JavaScript doesn't have such
a class.

>LISTEN TO ME:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>This is provable.

I acknowledge that you are correct. 

>> >Now here's the thing: read this 3 times, then come back tomorrow and
>> >read it again:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >     are _identical_      (in ideal conditions)

>Read it again. And continue to read it because you obviously don't
>understand it yet.  You just made a statement above that shows you
>completely fail to understand it.

Says the same thing. You're correct again.

>Dollar.cent values do not have an infinite number of random digits
>after the rounding point.  Rounding neither knows nor cares about
>this, it works as if they are there.

There's the rub. Cobol programmers routinely round numbers that do not
have infinite decimals .. things like financial numbers. Cobol is said
to be a business language. Why is it optimized for scientific use?
```

- **Date:** 2004-09-02T11:21:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch7oc5$jri$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <6dd8322.0408270924.40561400@posting.google.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408271341.5eab5a39@posting.google.com> <8vj1j0pccmom3kc3acrlaecdurco4e3bf0@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408282134.70db1ae9@posting.google.com> <9mr4j0lium86r4frssfb05t1o8odp0p424@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408301139.13a7858a@posting.google.com> <052aj0lmkgdi0r22g75jskjrqg8vv4se50@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409010156.45f80e0e@posting.google.com> <87qdj0hrsit5ge3v9rqcbms4guqeh4lnkm@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:87qdj0hrsit5ge3v9rqcbms4guqeh4lnkm@4ax.com...
>  For example, an
> interest calculation multiplies nv99 (balance) by nv9 (rate). The
> answer has precision nv999 ...

If "n" in both instances is singular, I would contend that the precision of
the answer is nnv999, not nv999, for a total of *five* digits.  A balance of
"9.99" multiplied by a rate of "9.9" ought to result in the intermediate
product "98.901", the way I see it, not "8.901".   The final result, and
whether digits are truncated or rounded, is dependent on both the verb and
the destination's description.

> >Dollar.cent values do not have an infinite number of random digits
> >after the rounding point.  Rounding neither knows nor cares about
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> to be a business language. Why is it optimized for scientific use?

Sigh.  Those who use ROUNDED in COBOL do so to obtain the results that have
been defined for the ROUNDED clause for forty-odd years.  Those who do not
want the behavior of the ROUNDED clause would be well-advised to use
something else.

I fail to see how COBOL had anything that could be described as "optimized
for scientific use" when it was utterly devoid of any sort of
floating-decimal-point representation until 2002 (and yes, I know, there
were some intrinsic functions introduced in 1989, but devoid of
floating-point data types they weren't and aren't going to give Fortran or
even Excel a run for its money).

The COBOL programmer who wants a form of "rounding" that differs from the
two methods provided in COBOL -- that which obtains when the ROUNDED clause
is present, and that which obtains when the ROUNDED clause is absent -- can
certainly code his programs accordingly.   If his expectations of the COBOL
ROUNDED clause differ from those delineated in the COBOL standards and/or
the reference materials for the particular implementation he is using, I do
not see that the fault is with the implementation, with the documentation,
or with the standard, but rather with his expectation that the world must
conform to HIS expectations.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> >Rounding, as used in Cobol, and in many other languages and in
> >mathematics works on numbers that are assumed to have an infinite
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> an infinite number of digits right of decimal. 

Rounding neither knows nor cares whether it is used for division or
multiplication nor what the number of digits in the calculations was. 
It is completely irrelevant whether there were actually an infinity of
digits or just the one used for rounding.

> For example, an
> interest calculation multiplies nv99 (balance) by nv9 (rate). The
> answer has precision nv999 and an average, as you point out, of
> n.4995. If we round to cents with Cobol, the average creeps up to
> .5000; if we use Bankers' Rounding, it remains n.4995.

That is not the fault of rounding which neither knows nor cares
whether there are only 3 digits or an infinity of them.  Getting the
'right' answer is the responsibility of an informed programmer who
_does_ know and should care how many digits there are of significance.

Whether one method of rounding (or truncation) is used or another is
actually the responibility of the _business rules_.

Regardless of the digits it may be that a Payroll deliberately uses
ceil() or that an interest calculation uses floor() to calculate the
cents.

 
> If the account started with an even dollar amount, say $100.00, all
> digits right of decimal are the result of interest calculation. This
> causes a cascade effect. When we compute interest next month, THIS
> month's interest will round up to .5005. 

It is the responsibity of the programmer to ensure that the program
gives the right answer.

Instead of just plucking '.5005' out of ^H^H^H the air, why don't you
show some calculations on your $100.00 that illustrates this
'cascading' that magically changes this months figures next month ?

> > Programmers should be
> >skilled enough to know what their tools can and cannot do and how to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> until you explained it.

It seemed common enough knowledge to me.  I've never done any formal
courses on the subject, no degrees.

> This fallacy is closely related to the argumentum ad populum.  It
> consists of asserting that the more people who support or believe a
> proposition, the more likely it is that that proposition is correct.

That was not my argument.  


> There's the rub. Cobol programmers routinely round numbers that do not
> have infinite decimals .. things like financial numbers. Cobol is said
> to be a business language. Why is it optimized for scientific use?

It seems to me that you are using the exact 'argument by population'
and the 'Brooklyn Bridge'. '[all] Cobol programmers' do it that way.

   They all do it wrong, so its OK that you did it wrong too.

In fact most businesses use nearest rounding for calculating cents
values and have _always_ done so, with ledgers, with tabulators, with
computers, so the 'routine way' is correct.  An alternative is called
'Bankers Rounding' because only the banks did it.
```

```
On Thu, 2 Sep 2004 20:47:27 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>You really don't need to know how CoBOL does its rounding.   Because it is
>simple enough to code in exactly the type of rounding that meets the
>requirements at my workplace.   My accountants tell me to round one way, I can
>always code in exactly the way they want.    It's not hard.

If you were using OO, you could overload rounding and it would be
changed everywhere. 

If you were  using IEEE 754, you could do it by changing one bit in a
hardware register. 

If you're good with parsers (they're not hard), you could write a
pre-compiler.

If you knew how to write microcode (it's not hard), you could create
an instruction to do it .. REALLY FAST.
```

```
to have three or four.

>> Those seem more valuable contributions than answers to beginners'
>> questions.
>
>I have a different view on those issues. Reverting to 'what C did in
>the 80s' is  not necessarily useful.

Professional programmers should know basics, like how to manage a
list.

>What you failed to present, probably because you never did much C, was
>why C++ was developed to solve the problems that existed in C, such as
>the problems inherent in using pointers.  And then Java solved the
>problems inherent in C++.

Java syntax was copied from C++. Java solved the problem of
distributing software to multiple platforms.

>When you introduce the use of raw address pointers, and also of other
>things such as ENTRY, you completely fail to take into account the 20
>or 30 years of experience that C programmers have had with the
>problems that these introduce. Most likely because you weren't doing C
>in enough depth to care.

Namespace pollution isn't as bad as the mess created by mangling.
Cobol can have the same problems. I've worked on Cobol load modules
that had 100 callable programs.

>What would be more valuble is if you brought in these 30 year old
>techniques and explained why _not_ to use them.  Then people would see
>why C++ was developed and would understand why OO is a valuble tool
>(but pointers are not).

I can't dispute that.

>I suppose that you will demand examples.  Well just as a starter:
>"ANS84 does not define a standard file status code for 'file not
>found'".

In 74, it was a 9x. I forgot they standardized it in New Cobol.
Besides, I just write:
IF FILE-STATUS NOT EQUAL TO ZERO
    DISPLAY 'Something is wrong ' FILE-STATUS
   GO TO ABEND.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Classic RW (was: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T14:27:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch4m95$je3$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <g4l6j05mdps70k5thbo78fjhmukrdbu2h9@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408301554.62260b11@posting.google.com> <68o9j0hjmld6k3jurc9jps4or8onm6hpnq@4ax.com> <ch3aok$i3u$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

On 31-Aug-2004, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> >IF FILE-STATUS NOT EQUAL TO ZERO
> >    DISPLAY 'Something is wrong ' FILE-STATUS
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> where none need occur.

Certainly.   We had a program which we were supposed to use when doing IO
commands with a KSDS file.   That program returned a "7" when it found a file
status not equal to zero.   The calling program didn't know what to do with that
7 so it normally abended.   The abend didn't show the real error.

I found out that often times, the *real* error was "97", which means "For VSAM
only:  OPEN statement execution successful:  File integrity verified".

Now our shop doesn't work by standards of OO shops.   When we make a change all
programs effected need to be extensively unit tested, systems tested, and user
tested before implementing.    Because of this, we never fixed the program to
accept "97" as valid.

The sin the original programmer did wasn't overlooking "97".   His sin was in
hiding the return code, leaving the calling program in the dark.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Classic RW (was: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T00:18:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0408312318.1f3cfe29@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408281150.124ce258@posting.google.com> <pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <j933j0ta4sk90q0ina59riu0qkmueau38s@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408291233.2b484f66@posting.google.com> <g4l6j05mdps70k5thbo78fjhmukrdbu2h9@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408301554.62260b11@posting.google.com> <68o9j0hjmld6k3jurc9jps4or8onm6hpnq@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> We all were. Some of us left the culture when it became practical to
> do so.

I never found that it was a 'culture'.  It was just programming. In
the late 70s I moved between hangers full of mainframes to the new
8080 CP/M things and Unix in between.

> Hopefully, decision makers will peek in and learn how to save money,
> make systems more reliable and programmers less cynical. 

Here ?  Don't be silly.

> Professional programmers should know basics, like how to manage a
> list.
 
> Java syntax was copied from C++. Java solved the problem of
> distributing software to multiple platforms.

Java does more than that.  For a start it dumps pointers to move to a
higher level language.

> Namespace pollution isn't as bad as the mess created by mangling.
> Cobol can have the same problems. I've worked on Cobol load modules
> that had 100 callable programs.

Actually ENTRY names are _in_addition_ to the program names, so there
is the _same_ problem, plus more problem.
 
> In 74, it was a 9x. I forgot they standardized it in New Cobol.

That's OK. No one can remember everything.  The problem was that when
it was pointed out that there was one you brought out your 'superior
tactical firepower' and continually denied that it existed, even
[mis-]quoted Fujitsu to 'prove' you were right.

Instead of accepting that the correction may be right and checking in
the manual, you simply continue to argue the misinformation.  You
mentioned the look of fear when you walked into a site, ....

> Besides, I just write:
> IF FILE-STATUS NOT EQUAL TO ZERO
>     DISPLAY 'Something is wrong ' FILE-STATUS
>    GO TO ABEND.

When testing a File Status code for success you should only check the
first character for being zero.  There are success codes that are not
'00'.

Your code also appears to be something from a 'mainframe culture' of
the 70s. This may be suitable where someone will be called in at 2am
to decipher what '9[' means. Modern systems require a more
sophisticated approach.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Classic RW (was: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T10:45:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch51sa$1qk9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408281150.124ce258@posting.google.com> <pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <j933j0ta4sk90q0ina59riu0qkmueau38s@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408291233.2b484f66@posting.google.com> <g4l6j05mdps70k5thbo78fjhmukrdbu2h9@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408301554.62260b11@posting.google.com> <68o9j0hjmld6k3jurc9jps4or8onm6hpnq@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408312318.1f3cfe29@posting.google.com> <ch4mc2$jtt$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:ch4mc2$jtt$1@peabody.colorado.edu...

> > When testing a File Status code for success you should only check the
> > first character for being zero.  There are success codes that are not
> > '00'.
>
> Such as the IBM mainframe code of "97".

No, I don't think so.

While the meaning of any "9x" I-O status code is indeed defined by the
implementor, ANSI X3.23-1974 clearly states that the I-O operation was
*unsuccessful* (paragraph 1.3.4.1, Status Key 1, in three places:  page IV-2
for Sequential I/O, page V-2 for Relative I-O; and VI-3 for Indexed I-O).

In standards-compliant COBOL, "97" may not be a "success code".

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Classic RW

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T19:23:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NkpZc.292683$J06.108267@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<ch52qg$dn0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408281150.124ce258@posting.google.com> <pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <j933j0ta4sk90q0ina59riu0qkmueau38s@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408291233.2b484f66@posting.google.com> <g4l6j05mdps70k5thbo78fjhmukrdbu2h9@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408301554.62260b11@posting.google.com> <68o9j0hjmld6k3jurc9jps4or8onm6hpnq@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408312318.1f3cfe29@posting.google.com> <ch4mc2$jtt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ch51sa$1qk9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ch52qg$dn0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

>On  1-Sep-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
But according to the J4 Book it ain't . Your favourite bugaboo this 
'97'. Accept Howard, "IBM done it wrong !", and it is THEY who are 
responsible for putting you through all your aggro on testing etc.

Now for me it isn't a problem. Using Procedural I'm guessing your 
file-statuses are popping up all over the place, while mine are getting 
tested in only one of five File tempaltes. (I've recently added a 
template for an ISAM with One Alternate Key).
This approach still follows Donald's original suggestion some two years 
back or more  - a fixed length PrimeKey up front, followed by a fixed 
length Alternate Key - even if you don't *actually* use all character 
positions.

Now as part of my set-up for a particular file I have to pass parameters 
to the File type Class, external name and actual-record length. Within 
the File class I check those incoming parameters, because the class is 
controlled by :-

       FD  Data-File
           record varying from 20 to 1500 characters        *> +++      
<--- notations to remind me
              depending on ws-RecordSize.
       01  Data-record.
           05 Data-PrimeKey       pic x(20).
           05 Data-info           pic x(1480).              *> +++

Note I have hard-coded a max of 1,500 - I don't think there's any other 
way of doing it. At some stage I forget my top limit of 1,500 'sending' 
a  record-size of 1,700 - BOINK ! On checking the parameters if incoming 
size greater than MaxRecordSize(1500), I move "RS" = RecordSize, to  
file-status code . I can now invoke my FilerError class for the 
file-status codes, and I have a text for "RS" above to use my standartd 
File Error  message box telling me I goofed on record size.

Increase the 1,500 to say 2,000 in the five template classes - quick 
compile - less than 1 minute for all five - requires no testing of the 
programs which will access the features in the file template classes - 
and I'm away to the races.

Even if M/F had given me a weirdo like your '97' in the 9x category , I 
could still check in my "OpenFile" methods and return an '00' or 
OK-status back to the invoking class.  Again, check the five File 
templates for their "openfile" methods - and allow for the '97' - again 
quick compile and away to the races..

Now when I've illustrated the above in the past you have indicated, 
"Interesting approach", and then followed that up with, "But file 
handling in the Business program is the easy part". But it bloody-well 
isn't is it ? You are still stuck with your stinking 97s.

Suggestions - (1) from memory I think you may have access to IBM 
Enterprise and the OO module - if that's correct, dip your feet into OO 
and emulate what I'm doing. (2) If # 1 is not viable, and again assuming 
you have 'entry-points' (for the life of me can't remember whether or 
not that is/is-not now in the 'Standard'), go Three-Tier and emulate my 
invoking with a file program that uses entry-points instead of method names.

Whichever way, kill those 97's off for good.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Classic RW

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T19:45:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch58sr$7j6$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408281150.124ce258@posting.google.com> <pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <j933j0ta4sk90q0ina59riu0qkmueau38s@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408291233.2b484f66@posting.google.com> <g4l6j05mdps70k5thbo78fjhmukrdbu2h9@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408301554.62260b11@posting.google.com> <68o9j0hjmld6k3jurc9jps4or8onm6hpnq@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408312318.1f3cfe29@posting.google.com> <ch4mc2$jtt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ch51sa$1qk9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ch52qg$dn0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <NkpZc.292683$J06.108267@pd7tw2no>`

```

On  1-Sep-2004, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> Whichever way, kill those 97's off for good.

We're killing them off by moving away from VSAM files.

When I write a program that accesses them, if I am not required to go thru that
generic program, I use:
 01  PRTFILE-STATUS          PIC X(02)   VALUE '00'.
     88 PRTFILE-STATUS-OK                VALUE '00' '97'.

Sure we can code around the fact that '97' is a "successful open" code when by
the standards it shouldn't be.   I never proposed that we can't.   I was just
pointing out that despite the standards, in my environment, it IS a successful
return code.

When the standards don't agree with reality, I have to go with reality.   And
document.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Classic RW

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T17:32:26-04:00

- **In-Reply-To:** `<NkpZc.292683$J06.108267@pd7tw2no>`
- **References:** `<6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408281150.124ce258@posting.google.com> <pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <j933j0ta4sk90q0ina59riu0qkmueau38s@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408291233.2b484f66@posting.google.com> <g4l6j05mdps70k5thbo78fjhmukrdbu2h9@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408301554.62260b11@posting.google.com> <68o9j0hjmld6k3jurc9jps4or8onm6hpnq@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408312318.1f3cfe29@posting.google.com> <ch4mc2$jtt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ch51sa$1qk9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ch52qg$dn0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <NkpZc.292683$J06.108267@pd7tw2no>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> Now for me it isn't a problem. Using Procedural I'm guessing your 
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> way of doing it. At some stage I forget my top limit of 1,500 'sending' 

What I am playing with Jimmy, is code generation.  In other words, I 
have a generic class of objects called "COBOL-FILES" that exists in my 
software developmet area.  It looks like the old railroad track cobol 
specs ... it generates the appropriate records and file clauses into the 
copybook.  The idea I am trying to warp my head arround is that a 
cobol-object (of which a file is a subclass) should be able to generate 
and compile its own code for *any* given platform.  Having great fun 
playing with the idea.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Classic RW

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-09-02T03:53:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cPwZc.297844$J06.99152@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<xjrZc.20853$7i2.757405@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408281150.124ce258@posting.google.com> <pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <j933j0ta4sk90q0ina59riu0qkmueau38s@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408291233.2b484f66@posting.google.com> <g4l6j05mdps70k5thbo78fjhmukrdbu2h9@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408301554.62260b11@posting.google.com> <68o9j0hjmld6k3jurc9jps4or8onm6hpnq@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408312318.1f3cfe29@posting.google.com> <ch4mc2$jtt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ch51sa$1qk9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ch52qg$dn0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <NkpZc.292683$J06.108267@pd7tw2no> <xjrZc.20853$7i2.757405@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:

> James J. Gavan wrote:
>
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I think I understand what you are saying - it essentially starts at the 
copybook stage. Now let's make some comparisons.

Pete Dashwood produced DECLGEN which he offered free here to those 
interested initially. A a good little application. Take MS Access. As 
designer I specify the tables within Access. Pete then reads the Access 
format and generates a copybook of the Variables required for the DB 
pre-processor, plus 'translation' to COBOL pic types - the COBOL 
copybook if you like. Now Micro Focus has ESQL Assistant - a truly great 
tool, (and bear in mind they have the resources - manpower). They went 
one step further than Pete, also generating a bunch of NULLs for each 
column in a row. Now even better, go into the ESQL Tool, bring up the 
particular table and it generates your SQL query - copy and paste into 
your program - clean compile - you are home and dry. (And of course 
within that tool, you can do a dummy run on your SQL statement - no 
programming required).

Now your approach - you want to generate the copybook in effect. They, 
(M/F) are one jump ahead of you again. From the IDE for V 3.0 you may 
have under Tools, something like "Data files". I'm guessing you've only 
got reformatting, changes etc. but V 3.1 onwards, (and I don't use the 
feature), you can build the copybook you are talking about. I could be 
wrong - but once you have that copybook - then you follow through on my 
approach. A whole series of copybooks generated by M/F or in your case - 
yourself. Then "feed' into my five classes - bearing in mind my file 
classes NEVER see the contents of the copybooks.

And oh so much thank you for the throwaway comment, "Put your Primekey 
up front with a fixed size". Your piece, coupled with Bill's suggestion 
use "occurs varying"  when specifying the Data-record - the combination 
did the trick !

Have fun.

SQL - to you many who have been using it a long time - I think I'm a bit 
out in space trying to figure this one - is there any possible way you 
could finish up with a vanilla flavoured or generic Table reference. I'm 
guessing not because it is the DB software that DEMANDS to know what the 
row format is.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
wrote:

>Howard Brazee wrote:
>
>>On  1-Sep-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>Increase the 1,500 to say 2,000 in the five template classes - quick 
>compile - less than 1 minute for all five - requires no testing of the 
>programs which will access the features in the file template classes - 
>and I'm away to the races.

You just said the secret word: testing. <duck descends> If he had
single method for all files, a change to it would require retesting
every program in the shop. In that environment, one is punished for
reusable code; cut-and-paste redundant code is cheaper.
```

- **References:** `<217e491a.0408291233.2b484f66@posting.google.com> <g4l6j05mdps70k5thbo78fjhmukrdbu2h9@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408301554.62260b11@posting.google.com> <68o9j0hjmld6k3jurc9jps4or8onm6hpnq@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408312318.1f3cfe29@posting.google.com> <ch4mc2$jtt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ch51sa$1qk9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ch52qg$dn0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <NkpZc.292683$J06.108267@pd7tw2no> <2vicj05fb3jqut2j4hpv042eren1gdsnkm@4ax.com> <VRwZc.297857$J06.292727@pd7tw2no>`

```
On Thu, 02 Sep 2004 03:56:37 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>Don't  quite get that one Robert. Care to expand a little ?

In some mainframe shops, a change to a called program requires
re-testing every program that calls it. It's not adequate to test the
method with a testbed or, perhaps, a few applications.

Testing thousands of programs, which all call a utility file method,
would be prohibitively expensive.
```

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>On Thu, 02 Sep 2004 03:56:37 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
>wrote:
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Thanks for that Robert, and I can't disagree in the slightest with your 
second paragraph.

 However as to the first - the problem arises because we know that there 
are still programs out there which have been running successfully for 
perhaps two or three decades.  That's *existing* programs.. But what 
about new projects ?  Perhaps some re-thinking to overcome logjams.

Example and it is not really OO - I have a separate class for the Date 
functions. My use is limited, I'm only interested in the time span 
between ccyymm (1) and ccyymm(2) - the 'dd' is irrelevant. So I have any 
of the date functions I use wrapped in that separate Class/Program. If I 
now want to calculate a date difference using ccyymmdd, then it could be 
added in there. It is using the Three-Tier approach.

Now back when I started with the old RM/COBOL application - yes I had 
*exactly* the same problem as para. 1 - make a change to a file format - 
now test the whole damn suite of programs before you could be assured.

Jimmy
```

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T13:19:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m-ednUTWv_MGj6vcRVn-ig@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408281150.124ce258@posting.google.com> <pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <j933j0ta4sk90q0ina59riu0qkmueau38s@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408291233.2b484f66@posting.google.com> <g4l6j05mdps70k5thbo78fjhmukrdbu2h9@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408301554.62260b11@posting.google.com> <68o9j0hjmld6k3jurc9jps4or8onm6hpnq@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408312318.1f3cfe29@posting.google.com> <ch4mc2$jtt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ch51sa$1qk9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck,
  The issue of "97" being treated as successful has been discussed with IBM. 
(You might want to personally ask Ann Bennett about it some time).  There is an 
existing (IBM user group) requirement to provide a (new with the 2002 Standard) 
"0x" implementor-defined status code as a (programmer selected) option to 
replace it.   IBM did (I can't find the citation at the moment) document that 
although the status code indicates an unsuccessful I/O statement that (as an 
extension?) IBM continues action after this FS "as if" it were a successful File 
status code.

Actually, before the 2002 Standard, IBM was in a "bit of a jam" in that there 
was no way to define an implementor-defined SUCCESSFUL file status code.
```

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T21:10:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oidcj0d5g8ihnufrb8q9tnhmmltip2af6f@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <j933j0ta4sk90q0ina59riu0qkmueau38s@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408291233.2b484f66@posting.google.com> <g4l6j05mdps70k5thbo78fjhmukrdbu2h9@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408301554.62260b11@posting.google.com> <68o9j0hjmld6k3jurc9jps4or8onm6hpnq@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408312318.1f3cfe29@posting.google.com> <ch4mc2$jtt$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ch51sa$1qk9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Wed, 1 Sep 2004 10:45:45 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>In standards-compliant COBOL, "97" may not be a "success code".

This is a good example of reluctance to change existing behavior, even
though you're fixing a problem.

In the good old days, the open did fail with 97, which meant another
program abended and left the file in an 'unstable' state. The fix was
to run a utility (IDCAMS) that 'verified' the file and removed the
'left open' indicator. 

Later, IBM improved VSAM to verify automatically when an open failed
for this reason. What to do about existing programs that were doing
IMPORTANT THINGS when they saw a 97 .. like abend? Yesterday it did
one thing; today it does something different. Maybe they don't WANT to
keep running. Maybe they want to make a grilled cheese sandwich. Who
knows? 

That's why they retained 97, even though the problem had been fixed.
Blame it on FUD.
```

```
Strawberry isn't a fruit, it's a stem. The achenes on its surface,

>Mr Wagner, any Marine commander worth their commission should know that 
>some situations require 'Superior Tactical Firepower' and enough lead so 
>you can walk on the air... and some situations require a barefoot assassin 
>with a knife.  An attempt to treat either situation as the other may

Because I was in Recon,  I knew that. The remark about firepower was a
generality.

>>In the context of
>>this forum, that means Truth eventually wins.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>enough crap against the wall and some of it is sure to stick.'

Our technique was go to full auto and hose the area down. We didn't
care whether we hit anything. We just wanted them to duck long enough
to make our getaway.
```

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T01:38:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ots9j05fcab0ao37sat1sg014601rfhuhf@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <6dd8322.0408270924.40561400@posting.google.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408281150.124ce258@posting.google.com> <pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <NnvYc.34591$5s3.5528@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cgvkae$15bu$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v2h7j0dqgqga79u7ufe1nlrtnrfoav4944@4ax.com> <-MOdnZod8e0BZK7cRVn-iA@comcast.com>`

```
On Mon, 30 Aug 2004 23:07:54 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>"In computing, a fixed-point number representation is a real data type for a
>number that has a fixed number of digits after the decimal (or binary or
>hexadecimal) point. For example, a fixed-point number with 4 digits after the
>decimal point could be used to store numbers such as 1.3467, 281243.3234 and
>0.1000, but would round 1.0301789 to 1.0302 and 0.0000654 to 0.0001."

 How do you indicate a decimal point in a register? How do you
represent one in memory? Except for floating-point, computer
instructions don't know about decimal points; they operate on
integers.
```

- **References:** `<01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408281150.124ce258@posting.google.com> <pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <NnvYc.34591$5s3.5528@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cgvkae$15bu$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v2h7j0dqgqga79u7ufe1nlrtnrfoav4944@4ax.com> <-MOdnZod8e0BZK7cRVn-iA@comcast.com> <ots9j05fcab0ao37sat1sg014601rfhuhf@4ax.com> <s76dnXjXxubmpajcRVn-ig@comcast.com>`

```
On Tue, 31 Aug 2004 21:43:25 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Although you have quoted my post out of context, remember it was you who asked 
>for "citations" and once you received them, you seem to "go off" on another 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>is wrong.

I didn't ask for citations; I asked for "evidence to the contrary." I
had in mind things that are real .. things you can put your hands on. 

>Admit that (and admit your original error or misstating of what you meant) and 
>then you can postulate something that may (or may not) be true, e.g.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>such stored values.

Yes, of course. The numerical point I was trying to make is that
fixed-point, like integers, are exact representations. Calculations
with them don't suffer from the rounding problems inherent to
floating-point. I thought saying "fixed-point ARE integers" was more
succinct and dramatic than carefully chosen words.

----------------
ASCII silly question, get a silly ANSI
```

```
I literally translated the word 'tangible', should have said
'perceptible'.
```
