###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Classic RW

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T10:10:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch4vr0$1p9q$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408281150.124ce258@posting.google.com> <pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <NnvYc.34591$5s3.5528@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cgvkae$15bu$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v2h7j0dqgqga79u7ufe1nlrtnrfoav4944@4ax.com> <-MOdnZod8e0BZK7cRVn-iA@comcast.com> <ots9j05fcab0ao37sat1sg014601rfhuhf@4ax.com> <s76dnXjXxubmpajcRVn-ig@comcast.com> <6v0bj094ccakm3peue6tprnfp38oa60i9k@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:6v0bj094ccakm3peue6tprnfp38oa60i9k@4ax.com...

> Yes, of course. The numerical point I was trying to make is that
> fixed-point, like integers, are exact representations. Calculations
> with them don't suffer from the rounding problems inherent to
> floating-point.

Depends on the values.  There are thirteen different *exact* representations
of the *value* 1 in single-precision format on Unisys MCP systems; only one
of them is technically an integer representation, and normalization or
integerization of the values has to be done explicitly.

> I thought saying "fixed-point ARE integers" was more
> succinct and dramatic than carefully chosen words.

I don't know how to tell you this, but "succinct and dramatic" don't carry a
whole lot of weight when the statement is "wrong".

Over the decades, I have fixed more than one compiler bug in which customers
were *really* pleased with the *speed* with which an answer was produced,
less than pleased with the *accuracy* of the answer itself.  "Fast", like
"succinct and dramatic", is a virtue only in the context of "correct".

     -Chuck Stevens
```

- **References:** `<01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408281150.124ce258@posting.google.com> <pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <NnvYc.34591$5s3.5528@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cgvkae$15bu$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v2h7j0dqgqga79u7ufe1nlrtnrfoav4944@4ax.com> <-MOdnZod8e0BZK7cRVn-iA@comcast.com> <ots9j05fcab0ao37sat1sg014601rfhuhf@4ax.com> <s76dnXjXxubmpajcRVn-ig@comcast.com> <6v0bj094ccakm3peue6tprnfp38oa60i9k@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> Yes, of course. The numerical point I was trying to make is that
> fixed-point, like integers, are exact representations. Calculations
> with them don't suffer from the rounding problems inherent to
> floating-point. 

   10 / 3

Explain how using fixed point doesn't result in a 'rounding problem'.

  Hourly rate = 10.45
   Time worked = 3.75
  payment $.c  =

Explain how this doesn't result in a 'rounding problem' 

There are no 'rounding problems' inherent in floating point, there are
problem of inexact representation which have nothing to do with
rounding.  In fact rounding is the solution not the problem.

> I thought saying "fixed-point ARE integers" was more
> succinct and dramatic than carefully chosen words.

Dramatic, yes, correct, no.

You are just using rationalization and deflection to claim you weren't
_really_ wrong (as usual).

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Classic RW

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-02T00:49:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f4qcj0l8mbpqkeb891uhuabnfi262b2qqv@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <NnvYc.34591$5s3.5528@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cgvkae$15bu$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v2h7j0dqgqga79u7ufe1nlrtnrfoav4944@4ax.com> <-MOdnZod8e0BZK7cRVn-iA@comcast.com> <ots9j05fcab0ao37sat1sg014601rfhuhf@4ax.com> <s76dnXjXxubmpajcRVn-ig@comcast.com> <6v0bj094ccakm3peue6tprnfp38oa60i9k@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409011142.1f6e4c4@posting.google.com>`

```
On 1 Sep 2004 12:42:43 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>   10 / 3

Point of Sale systems handle this like your 'forward'. In this case
the price is 3 for $10. The computer rings up the first and second at
3.34, the third at 3.32

>Explain how using fixed point doesn't result in a 'rounding problem'.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Explain how this doesn't result in a 'rounding problem' 

That's more difficult than it looks. Is 3.75 a truncated or rounded
number? If it came from a time clock, it is truncated from an
infinitely long analog measurement. The employee did not work exactly
3.75, he worked between 3.75 and 3.7599999. In that case, Cobol
rounding restores the truncated digits, as you so capably pointed out.

However, if the employee is a programmer who writes down 8.0 every
day, that's a rounded time interval. He doesn't work exactly 8 hours
on any day. Over a long period of time, he works an average of 8 hours
per day. In that case, Bankers' Rounding is the appropriate algorithm.

How does the payroll program know which method to use for the
individual paycheck?

>There are no 'rounding problems' inherent in floating point, there are
>problem of inexact representation which have nothing to do with
>rounding.  In fact rounding is the solution not the problem.

You're right. I view all unintentional changes in value as rounding,
as in 'round to nearest binary value'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Classic RW

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-02T00:37:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409012337.4304ee5e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <NnvYc.34591$5s3.5528@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cgvkae$15bu$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v2h7j0dqgqga79u7ufe1nlrtnrfoav4944@4ax.com> <-MOdnZod8e0BZK7cRVn-iA@comcast.com> <ots9j05fcab0ao37sat1sg014601rfhuhf@4ax.com> <s76dnXjXxubmpajcRVn-ig@comcast.com> <6v0bj094ccakm3peue6tprnfp38oa60i9k@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409011142.1f6e4c4@posting.google.com> <f4qcj0l8mbpqkeb891uhuabnfi262b2qqv@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> >> Yes, of course. The numerical point I was trying to make is that
> >> fixed-point, like integers, are exact representations. Calculations
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 3.34, the third at 3.32

So, you do agree that 'calculations _DO_ suffer from rounding
problems' and that the programmer copes with this using an appropriate
mechanism.

You also seem to be using ceil() and then a static adjustment against
the total which is completely different from 'round and forward' which
may have given 3.33, 3.34, 3.33.

> >  Hourly rate = 10.45
> >   Time worked = 3.75
> >  payment $.c  =
> >
> >Explain how this doesn't result in a 'rounding problem' 

> That's more difficult than it looks. 

So you do agree that there is a 'rounding problem'.

> Is 3.75 a truncated or rounded
> number? If it came from a time clock, it is truncated from an
> infinitely long analog measurement. 

Geez when did you last see an analog time clock that printed the hours
worked ?

All the ones that I saw just printed the time.

> The employee did not work exactly
> 3.75, he worked between 3.75 and 3.7599999. In that case, 

More likely the hours were calculated in some formal way from the
start and end time such that the worked hours can only one from a set
of fixed intervals.

> Cobol
> rounding restores the truncated digits, as you so capably pointed out.

Cobol rounding (nor any other mechanism) does nothing to 'restore
truncated digits' on an individual number.  It gives a value _as_if_
the original had an infinite number of random digits.  It neither
knows nor cares whether there actually are any or what they are.

> However, if the employee is a programmer who writes down 8.0 every
> day, that's a rounded time interval. He doesn't work exactly 8 hours
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> individual paycheck?

It uses the one specified in the program spec.  This may depend on
labour laws, business rules, employee (or union) agreements, or a
range of other things.

Regardless of whichever one is used there is no $65,000.00 to put in
your pocket.
```

```
On 2 Sep 2004 00:37:23 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

>> Point of Sale systems handle this like your 'forward'. In this case
>> the price is 3 for $10. The computer rings up the first and second at
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>may have given 3.33, 3.34, 3.33.

Right. The price is 3.34 and there is a volume discount of .02. The
effect is the same as rounding.

>> Is 3.75 a truncated or rounded
>> number? If it came from a time clock, it is truncated from an
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>All the ones that I saw just printed the time.

I haven't seen an analog clock in 15 years. They're  electronic and
there's usually no paper card. You just wave an RFID at it.

>> The employee did not work exactly
>> 3.75, he worked between 3.75 and 3.7599999. In that case, 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>of fixed intervals.

In the US, it is determined by State law. This typical one says the
employer MAY round to the NEAREST quarter-hour, but is not required to
do so. Electronic clocks usually return hundredths of an hour.

Moreover, if an employer utilizes time clocks, early or late clock
punching, which results in minor discrepancies, can be disregarded.
Furthermore, generally, the practice of rounding the employeesï¿½
starting and finishing time to the nearest five minutes, nearest
one-tenth or quarter of an hour will be accepted, unless such
practice, over time, results in the failure to compensate employees
properly for all the time they have actually worked

http://www.state.vt.us/labind/Wagehour/sumlawsbrochure.htm 

>> How does the payroll program know which method to use for the
>> individual paycheck?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>range of other things.

Someone writes the program spec.
```

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0409022250.770fb739@posting.google.com...
> Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> In what way ?

As Mr Wagner stated in a prior post, "Point of Sale systems
handle this like your 'forward'. In this case the price is 3 for $10.
The computer rings up the first and second at 3.34, the third
at 3.32."

I know of no POS systems where the distribution would not
be 3.34, 3.33, and 3.33. I would speculate that Mr Wagner
does not yet grasp certain methods for distributing residual
amounts to provide accurate totals; thus the explanation for
a difference of >0.01 is "volume discount ... same as rounding".

I suspect Mr Wagner's own explanation might provide a more
interesting discussion.
```

Robert Wagner  <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:

[snip]

>Explain how an apocryphal system you "know" would handle this: Coke
>sells for .35 per can, 1.99 for a 6-pack. Keep in mind the 6-pack
>doesn't have a distinct UPC. No, you can't assign a PLU. If the
>customer buys 5 cans, management wants to charge 1.75.

Hmmmmm... after totalling the cans purchased how about..

Divide all-cans-bought by 6 giving six-packs-bought 
                            remainder single-cans-bought
End-Divide
Compute coke-total-cost = ((six-packs-bought * six-pack-price) +
                           (single-cans-bought * single-can-price))
End-Compute

... or a reasonable facsimile thereof?

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:8opgj059i8dero0vk74hfo7kph121obf20@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 3 Sep 2004 05:31:38 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> a consulting contract.

It is true that my 'first-hand' experience consists only of looking
at receipts printed by POS terminals and, since my experience
does not conflict with my expectations, it is also true that I have
nothing to complain about but the prices themselves. Damn
inflation!

> Explain how an apocryphal system you "know" would handle this: Coke
> sells for .35 per can, 1.99 for a 6-pack. Keep in mind the 6-pack
> doesn't have a distinct UPC. No, you can't assign a PLU. If the
> customer buys 5 cans, management wants to charge 1.75.

First, Mr Wagner, I flunked 'apocryphal' (and 'travesty') on an
IQ test, had I known those words I might be smarter than I am.

For a six-pack, I might have the checker enter 6 for the quantity;
for five, I might have the checker enter 5 for the quantity. The
receipt might then state 'Coke 12oz 6-pack 1.99' or '5@0.35'
'Coke 12oz 1.75'. if the 5 cans were read individually, the
reciept might, five times, state 'Coke 12oz 0.35'.

Mr Wagner what you requested seems unrelated to the original,
which was merely the fraction '10/3'. You supplied the context
of POS and did not provide a condition for individual pricing,
at that time.
```

```
On Fri, 3 Sep 2004 11:16:24 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:8opgj059i8dero0vk74hfo7kph121obf20@4ax.com...

>> Explain how an apocryphal system you "know" would handle this: Coke
>> sells for .35 per can, 1.99 for a 6-pack. Keep in mind the 6-pack
>> doesn't have a distinct UPC. No, you can't assign a PLU. If the
>> customer buys 5 cans, management wants to charge 1.75.

>For a six-pack, I might have the checker enter 6 for the quantity;
>for five, I might have the checker enter 5 for the quantity. 

That's exactly what they do. They scan one can and enter Qty x.
They could scan each can, but that's discouraged for two reasons.
It takes longer and the scanner might drop one or two. Why? Watch what
a checker does when an item won't scan. He or she waves it back and
forth until something registers. It's probably read by the scanner two
or three times. To prevent overcharging the customer, software _in the
scanner_, not the POS, discards duplicates seen too close together,
about one second, I think.

>The
>receipt might then state 'Coke 12oz 6-pack 1.99' or '5@0.35'
>'Coke 12oz 1.75'. if the 5 cans were read individually, the
>reciept might, five times, state 'Coke 12oz 0.35'.

How does the POS do it? What does the price file look like?

It says UnitPrice .35, DiscountThreshold 6, DiscountAmount .11

>Mr Wagner what you requested seems unrelated to the original,
>which was merely the fraction '10/3'. You supplied the context
>of POS and did not provide a condition for individual pricing,
>at that time.

True, I used POS in the context of rounding, but you wanted to talk
about POS.
```

Then might I assume the following to be true?

Buy 1, charge, 3.34, minus price 3.333, equal premium, .007.
Buy 2, charge, 6.68, minus price 6.666, equal premium, .014.
Buy 3, charge, 10.00, minus price, 9.999, equal premium, .001.
And a premium of .001 is the same as a discount of .02.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Classic RW

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T09:58:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch4v2s$1ok0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<6ltti0p51k8ddoav0hlin5tjb9l75nbjk8@4ax.com> <6dd8322.0408270924.40561400@posting.google.com> <01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408281150.124ce258@posting.google.com> <pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <NnvYc.34591$5s3.5528@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cgvkae$15bu$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v2h7j0dqgqga79u7ufe1nlrtnrfoav4944@4ax.com> <-MOdnZod8e0BZK7cRVn-iA@comcast.com> <ots9j05fcab0ao37sat1sg014601rfhuhf@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:ots9j05fcab0ao37sat1sg014601rfhuhf@4ax.com...

>  How do you indicate a decimal point in a register? How do you
> represent one in memory? Except for floating-point, computer
> instructions don't know about decimal points; they operate on
> integers.

Maybe your systems require that you distinguish among them.  Our
instructions (we call them "operators") operate on *operands*.  None of the
mathematical operators require integers, or make any particular
differentiation between integers and floating-point values.  Even the
operators that convert an arithmetic result to packed-decimal accept a
floating-point value.   If you add two operands that happen to be
single-precision integers together, the result will be a single-integer only
if the magnitude of the sum is less than 8**13, otherwise it's
floating-point.

Once again, an implementor *may* treat fixed-point values as integers and
keep track of the scale factor in the generated program, although the
standard doesn't require this.  That does not mean fixed-point items *are*
integers, only that they are sometimes *handled* as integers.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Classic RW

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T22:13:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sihcj0ta3l9umv4kg09c25rave0qg7b7jb@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408281150.124ce258@posting.google.com> <pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <NnvYc.34591$5s3.5528@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cgvkae$15bu$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v2h7j0dqgqga79u7ufe1nlrtnrfoav4944@4ax.com> <-MOdnZod8e0BZK7cRVn-iA@comcast.com> <ots9j05fcab0ao37sat1sg014601rfhuhf@4ax.com> <ch4v2s$1ok0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Wed, 1 Sep 2004 09:58:04 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:ots9j05fcab0ao37sat1sg014601rfhuhf@4ax.com...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>floating-point.

I said "except for floating-point". You are saying all numbers are
floating-point on your A machine. I'm curious how you store a value
like 1.23. Seems to me there are four ways:

1. Make the exponent base-10. You don't have that; it's base-8.
2. Store the value like an integer and keep track of point location in
software. You say you don't do that.
3. Convert to binary. In that case, you don't have exact values. 
4. Do the math in packed-decimal. In that case some of the
mathematical operators do require integers (mathematical definition).

>Once again, an implementor *may* treat fixed-point values as integers and
>keep track of the scale factor in the generated program, although the
>standard doesn't require this.  That does not mean fixed-point items *are*
>integers, only that they are sometimes *handled* as integers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Classic RW

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T16:04:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch5kho$272l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408281150.124ce258@posting.google.com> <pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <NnvYc.34591$5s3.5528@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cgvkae$15bu$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v2h7j0dqgqga79u7ufe1nlrtnrfoav4944@4ax.com> <-MOdnZod8e0BZK7cRVn-iA@comcast.com> <ots9j05fcab0ao37sat1sg014601rfhuhf@4ax.com> <ch4v2s$1ok0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <sihcj0ta3l9umv4kg09c25rave0qg7b7jb@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:sihcj0ta3l9umv4kg09c25rave0qg7b7jb@4ax.com...

> I said "except for floating-point". You are saying all numbers are
> floating-point on your A machine.

I'm saying all *operands* used in arithmetic operations are indeed *in
floating-point format* precisely because, in that implementation, the
internal integer representation is a subset of the floating-point
representation.

> I'm curious how you store a value
> like 1.23.

That depends on how it's *used*, how the item used to contain it is
*declared*, and exactly what "a value like 1.23" *means* in those contexts.
Is this a numeric literal?  The interpretation of a value in a data item
with a fixed implied decimal point?  The value retrieved from a REAL or
DOUBLE item?  What?

> Seems to me there are four ways:
>
> 1. Make the exponent base-10. You don't have that; it's base-8.

No, we don't do that.

> 2. Stre the value like an integer and keep track of point location in
> software. You say you don't do that.

Sometimes we do that.  Depends on USAGE and context.   That doesn't mean
that 1.23 *is an integer*, nor does it mean that the value stored is stored
*as an integer*.

If it's in a USAGE BINARY item declared with two implied decimal points,
it's *likely* that it will have been integerized  before being stored -- 
thus the value stored would be @00000000007B@ --but @251EC0000000@ could
reasonably occur and be handled correctly, as could anything in between
those two extremes like @23800FC00000@.  Only the first is *strictly
speaking* an integer *representation*.  The others are floating-point; they
happen to correspond exactly to the same integer values.

>> In that case, you don't have exact values.

Nope.  The values of any single-precision floating-point value correspond to
an integer so long as all octades of the mantissa below the magnitude of the
exponent are zero.  Thus,  1 * (8 ^ 0) is the canonic integer form; 64 * (8
^ -2)  (@210000000040@) is also an integer representation of 1, but 65 * (8
^ -2) (@210000000041@) does not evaluate to an integer; rather, it evaluates
to 1.015625.

There is no requirement that the mantissa of a floating-point item have
"fractional data".    Are floating-point values invariably inexact just
because they have exponents?

> 4. Do the math in packed-decimal. In that case some of the
> mathematical operators do require integers (mathematical definition).

No, we don't do that on the MCP systems.  Of course, we did on the Medium
System, and we also did it (in the virtual machines for the COBOLs and for
RPG) on the B1000.  We actually did consider hardware decimal arithmetic
some twenty years ago for the A Series, but decided for several reasons not
to go that route.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Classic RW

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T11:56:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
On Tue, 31 Aug 2004 11:23:44 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>I know environments in which integers, fixed-point and floating-point data
>items are "stored in memory" identically; the only differentiation among
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>to have an exact value. 

That seems dangerous. If the program subtracts a small number from a
large number, the hardware might lose decimals. The result would be
the original large number, as though zero had been subtracted.
```

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:iedbj091rf52f4jrjlatjjlo4tltoo2jgm@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 31 Aug 2004 11:23:44 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> >I know environments in which integers, fixed-point and floating-point
data
> >items are "stored in memory" identically; the only differentiation among
> >them is how they are handled and treated.  In that environment, in fact,
> >there is no requirement that integer data actually *be* in "canonic
integer
> >form"; a normalized floating-point representation of the value serves
> >equally well and is just as exact a representation.  Thus, in that
> >environment, a fixed-point numeric value may be stored, *not* as an
integer
> >defined for that implementation, but as a *floating-point* item that
happens
> >to have an exact value.
>
> That seems dangerous. If the program subtracts a small number from a
> large number, the hardware might lose decimals. The result would be
> the original large number, as though zero had been subtracted.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Classic RW

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T10:20:40-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch50d9$1pmr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408281150.124ce258@posting.google.com> <pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <NnvYc.34591$5s3.5528@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cgvkae$15bu$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v2h7j0dqgqga79u7ufe1nlrtnrfoav4944@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408302230.3f29c9ff@posting.google.com> <8ue9j05arsd8h5jhknobv9a46pur41tfhc@4ax.com> <ch2fnh$2f7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <iedbj091rf52f4jrjlatjjlo4tltoo2jgm@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:iedbj091rf52f4jrjlatjjlo4tltoo2jgm@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 31 Aug 2004 11:23:44 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> >I know environments in which integers, fixed-point and floating-point
data
> >items are "stored in memory" identically; the only differentiation among
> >them is how they are handled and treated.  In that environment, in fact,
> >there is no requirement that integer data actually *be* in "canonic
integer
> >form"; a normalized floating-point representation of the value serves
> >equally well and is just as exact a representation.  Thus, in that
> >environment, a fixed-point numeric value may be stored, *not* as an
integer
> >defined for that implementation, but as a *floating-point* item that
happens
> >to have an exact value.
>
> That seems dangerous. If the program subtracts a small number from a
> large number, the hardware might lose decimals. The result would be
> the original large number, as though zero had been subtracted.

Depends on what you mean by "large" and "small".  If the *values* are
*representable* as single-precision integers (magnitude no greater than
549,755,813,887) the subtraction produces exact results.  Likewise, if the
values are contained in double-word operands, and are *representable* as
double-precision integers (magnitude no greater than
302,231,454,903,657,293,676,543), the subtraction will produce exact
results.   It does not matter in either case whether the operands or the
results are integerized, normalized, or any of the various possible (exact)
representations in between.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
On Tue, 31 Aug 2004 16:31:48 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>Walt Kelly wrote "I refuse to have a battle of wits with an unarmed person."
>I daresay some of us that participate in this forum would probably do well
>to heed that advice.

In Martin Chuzzlewit, Dickens described the character Pecksniff this
way: "Some people likened him to a direction-post, which is always
telling the way to a place, and never goes there."

>William D. Blake wrote "Sometimes I suffer from delusions of adequacy."   I
>daresay some of us that participate in this forum would also be well-advised
>to include such a characterization in our own inventory of our individual
>strengths and weaknesses.

" Man would be fain be great and sees that he is little; would fain be
 happy and sees that he is miserable; would fain be perfect and sees
 that he is full of imperfections; would fain be the object of the
 love and esteem of men, and sees that his faults merit only their
 aversion and contempt. The embarrassment wherein he finds himself
 produces in him the most unjust and criminal passions imaginable, for
 he conceives a mortal hatred against TRUTH, which blames him and
 convinces him of his faults."
                            - Pascal, "Pensees"
```

- **References:** `<01vui0pp92mpjdnfmoukpj5k26icqpmlgj@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408281150.124ce258@posting.google.com> <pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <C8dYc.1456$8d1.397@newsread2.news.pas.earthlink.net> <NnvYc.34591$5s3.5528@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cgvkae$15bu$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v2h7j0dqgqga79u7ufe1nlrtnrfoav4944@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408302230.3f29c9ff@posting.google.com> <8ue9j05arsd8h5jhknobv9a46pur41tfhc@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408311505.50d920cd@posting.google.com> <vdebj0tf38bm3f8qqi5v8apqf83nnojfq1@4ax.com>`

```
When you clarify your position, there are two possibilities:

ONE - Say your original statement was mis-leading and state what you REALLY 
meant.  (Something that you have ALMOST done finally with this thread)

TWO - Just add additional information and never say that your original statement 
was either in error or incomplete (Something that you seem to almost always do).

Start doing the first right away when errors and omissions are pointed out to 
you and your posts will be better received.

Compare your most recent statement starting

 "Yes, of course. The numerical point I was trying to make is that fixed-point, 
like integers, are exact representations."

(which still  avoids ADMITTING your error in phrasing) with all the many posts 
before where you never even admitted that what you said "was more succinct and 
dramatic than carefully chosen words" with all your other posts where you 
continued to claim that "fixed point were integers".
```

calender in red).

recognize e-mail addresses.

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T09:12:03-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<ch4sck$1muf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<8a2qi094vn5ht6pfq3sa3rmvnpkihui5aq@4ax.com> <pmq1j0l9grmt07nvme0pgboa6fn22h4glm@4ax.com> <217e491a.0408282320.388765d7@posting.google.com> <afd5j01oq7bnh3117sh7sur0d11k9ss21p@4ax.com> <cgusef$9u4$1@panix5.panix.com> <fo07j05mcb78ga01l7i9l9tslce0f7sn33@4ax.com> <ch06ge$1hp3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c998j0ttprb0lmotpai4pl0u9ke7ge0k9c@4ax.com> <ch26em$2ti6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <0tg9j097mvsvnvclj39ukmmojdo7qsa5jj@4ax.com> <i5u9j09pfe6jps8ep4klit0a9vsh3ckf4b@4ax.com> <dotaj0dtfqkhp7ub8tr330g1jut3qm5h0i@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:dotaj0dtfqkhp7ub8tr330g1jut3qm5h0i@4ax.com...

> >Why should a lexicographer choose to omit the word for a piece of
> >machinery used in cloth-making?
>
> Because it is seldom used by the general public and is more likely a
> misspelling of calendar.

I consider Webster's New Collegiate a medium-sized *general-purpose*
lexicon.  It includes *two unrelated* definitions for "calender":  the verb
and noun forms whose context has already been cited, and an unrelated noun
form.  You may be in the habit of assuming that "calender" is usually a
misspelling for "calendar"; I, however, tend to operate under the assumption
that the person who wrote "calender" meant exactly what he wrote, and only
if the word doesn't make sense in that context would I begin to suspect that
perhaps the wrong (valid) word -- not a misspelling -- was used.

> As with most spelling checkers, mine has a supplemental word list. If
> the user is in the textile industry, he or she can easily add
> calender.

What if the user has an interest in the goings-on in the Middle East?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **In-Reply-To:** `<3c5cj0thpk6gued451akolsbmu8560qp2e@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0408282320.388765d7@posting.google.com> <afd5j01oq7bnh3117sh7sur0d11k9ss21p@4ax.com> <cgusef$9u4$1@panix5.panix.com> <fo07j05mcb78ga01l7i9l9tslce0f7sn33@4ax.com> <ch06ge$1hp3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c998j0ttprb0lmotpai4pl0u9ke7ge0k9c@4ax.com> <ch26em$2ti6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <0tg9j097mvsvnvclj39ukmmojdo7qsa5jj@4ax.com> <i5u9j09pfe6jps8ep4klit0a9vsh3ckf4b@4ax.com> <dotaj0dtfqkhp7ub8tr330g1jut3qm5h0i@4ax.com> <ch4sck$1muf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3c5cj0thpk6gued451akolsbmu8560qp2e@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>Hmm. "Wandering mendicant dervish" has a nice ring. Sounds like
>Gilbert & Sullivan. <Contemplates career change>
>  
>
Sounds like you might know your G  & S - but singing dervishes, I doubt it.

A little tale. We did Mikado at school. Considering it was in the middle 
of WWII they did a pretty good job on costumes, flesh coloured ladies 
stockings to represent bald heads with sideburns etc. So we start off 
with the male chorus line flashing their fans :-

If you want to know who we are
We are gentlemen of JAP-AN
On--nnn many a vase and jar,
On many a screen and fan.....

Then up pops yours truly about 10-11 years of age to do the first solo. 
Pish-Tush I think.  It went real well at dress rehearsals, so I sing :-

Our great Mikado a virtuous man,
When he to rule this land began,
To try and find...(memory loss !)
Where young men might best be suited,
So he decreed in words extinct,
That all who flirted, leered or winked,
Should forthwith be beheaded,
Beheaded,
Be-hhhhh-eaded etc.

Plump little Sister Tomilina provided the accompaniment on a piano, 
(substitute for the D'oly Carte Orchestra). What I didn't *know* was 
that Father Pat, (with shamrock still behind his ears), was sat out 
there in front controlling the lighting. As I started to sing he 
switched on one of those rotating fans with diagonal shades of colour, 
red, blue, green, yellow etc. - STRAIGHT AT ME.

I was absolutely bloody mesmerized. I sang the first verse at least five 
times. The 'orchestra' was getting frantic. It eventually clicked back 
into place, I finished the last verse with an audible sigh from Sister 
Tomilina.

I said recently I can't read music. Another problem. We had a madrigal 
number - a quartet - two of us supposedly signing Alto :-

Brightly dawns our wedding day,
Fa la, la; la; la; lal, la-la

I never did get the hang of it - and finished up singing the main theme !

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T13:26:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch5b98$20rs$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0408282320.388765d7@posting.google.com> <afd5j01oq7bnh3117sh7sur0d11k9ss21p@4ax.com> <cgusef$9u4$1@panix5.panix.com> <fo07j05mcb78ga01l7i9l9tslce0f7sn33@4ax.com> <ch06ge$1hp3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c998j0ttprb0lmotpai4pl0u9ke7ge0k9c@4ax.com> <ch26em$2ti6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <0tg9j097mvsvnvclj39ukmmojdo7qsa5jj@4ax.com> <i5u9j09pfe6jps8ep4klit0a9vsh3ckf4b@4ax.com> <dotaj0dtfqkhp7ub8tr330g1jut3qm5h0i@4ax.com> <ch4sck$1muf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3c5cj0thpk6gued451akolsbmu8560qp2e@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:3c5cj0thpk6gued451akolsbmu8560qp2e@4ax.com...

> >You may be in the habit of assuming that "calender" is usually a
> >misspelling for "calendar"; I, however, tend to operate under the
assumption
> >that the person who wrote "calender" meant exactly what he wrote, and
only
> >if the word doesn't make sense in that context would I begin to suspect
that
> >perhaps the wrong (valid) word -- not a misspelling -- was used.
>
> I assume people using a spelling checker are asking for help with
> spelling. I don't use one, nor do you.

Which doesn't really explain why "consanguinaceous" has to be a spelling
error simply because it isn't in any dictionary or spell-checking software
you could find.  Just because "antidisestablishmentarianism" doesn't appear
in any of the usual *American* English dictionaries (it's not in the
Webster's Third, but may, apparently, still be found in the current OED) or,
for that matter, whether anyone other than I has ever written or otherwise
used the word "antidisestablishmentarianistically" does not mean that it is
not an English word whose meaning is clear.
 For just about any English word ending in "-ism" for which a corresponding
word  "-ist" occurs the adjectival form ending in "-istical" and from that
the adverbial form ending in "-istically" can be deduced with clear meaning,
although *sometimes* shorter forms are synonymous (or nearly so), may be
more accepted and and are probably more readily understood.

Consider "mysticism".  The word "mystic" is widely understood.  "Mysticist"
is not in the dictionary, but I would contend that "mysticist" exists as a
word (one who practices the behavior of a mystic, specifically as distinct
from being one, and thus a slightly derogatory term).  Likewise, from there,
"mysticistical[ly]" is subtly different semantically from "mystical[ly]".  I
grant that on first hearing the audience might be puzzled by the words, but
if they take the trouble to apply the standard rules of English their
meaning (and the intended distinction from the more common words) should be
clear.

To return to "consanguinaceous", the suffixes "-ous" and "-aceous" are both
clearly defined in the Webster's Ninth as adjectival suffixes with subtly
different meanings.  I still can't figure out why you felt
"consanguinaceous" was a *spelling error* unless some software or dictionary
explicitly told you so.  The formation of words from the suffixes "-aceous"
and "-ous" is well-founded in English *grammar*, and is in fact described in
a common *lexicon*.    That's orthogonal to whether docdwarf *really meant*
to write "consanguineous" when he wrote "consanguinaceous"; the meaning of
the latter term is clear (at least to me), and it is spelled correctly
according to English rules.

I will admit I would have some trouble distinguishing the semantics of
"consanguineously" from those of "consanguinaceously", though.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch5db8$225k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0408282320.388765d7@posting.google.com> <afd5j01oq7bnh3117sh7sur0d11k9ss21p@4ax.com> <cgusef$9u4$1@panix5.panix.com> <fo07j05mcb78ga01l7i9l9tslce0f7sn33@4ax.com> <ch06ge$1hp3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c998j0ttprb0lmotpai4pl0u9ke7ge0k9c@4ax.com> <ch26em$2ti6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <0tg9j097mvsvnvclj39ukmmojdo7qsa5jj@4ax.com> <i5u9j09pfe6jps8ep4klit0a9vsh3ckf4b@4ax.com> <dotaj0dtfqkhp7ub8tr330g1jut3qm5h0i@4ax.com> <ch4sck$1muf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3c5cj0thpk6gued451akolsbmu8560qp2e@4ax.com> <ch5b98$20rs$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:ch5b98$20rs$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
I wrote:

> Just because "antidisestablishmentarianism" doesn't appear
> in any of the usual *American* English dictionaries (it's not in the
> Webster's Third, but may, apparently, still be found in the current OED)
or,
> for that matter, whether anyone other than I has ever written or otherwise
> used the word "antidisestablishmentarianistically" does not mean that it
is
> not an English word whose meaning is clear.
>  For just about any English word ending in "-ism" for which a
corresponding
> word  "-ist" occurs the adjectival form ending in "-istical" and from that
> the adverbial form ending in "-istically" can be deduced with clear
meaning,
> although *sometimes* shorter forms are synonymous (or nearly so), may be
> more accepted and and are probably more readily understood.

Wikipedia comments on this as "agglutinative construction" and cites
"propseudocontraneoantidisestablishmentarianistically" as an example, though
I don't think they decoded it accurately.

Given that "disestablishmentarianist" is a person who held that the ties
between the British government and the Church of England should have been
severed, I'd [decode and] define the agglutinized word above as "acting in a
manner consistent with individuals [-istically] who support the actions
[pro] of those who acted falsely like [pseudo] they were opposed [contra] to
the actions of those associated with a resurgence [neo] of the opposition to
the severing of ties between the British government and the Church of
England [disestablishmentarian[ism]].   Did I get that right?

In any event, *legitimate* English word,
"propseudocontraneoantidisestablishmentarianistically".

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T23:59:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<p0mcj0tmr9lfdpjli4k4pjo4v8bauq3tou@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cgusef$9u4$1@panix5.panix.com> <fo07j05mcb78ga01l7i9l9tslce0f7sn33@4ax.com> <ch06ge$1hp3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c998j0ttprb0lmotpai4pl0u9ke7ge0k9c@4ax.com> <ch26em$2ti6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <0tg9j097mvsvnvclj39ukmmojdo7qsa5jj@4ax.com> <i5u9j09pfe6jps8ep4klit0a9vsh3ckf4b@4ax.com> <dotaj0dtfqkhp7ub8tr330g1jut3qm5h0i@4ax.com> <ch4sck$1muf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3c5cj0thpk6gued451akolsbmu8560qp2e@4ax.com> <ch5b98$20rs$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Wed, 1 Sep 2004 13:26:15 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:3c5cj0thpk6gued451akolsbmu8560qp2e@4ax.com...

>> I assume people using a spelling checker are asking for help with
>> spelling. I don't use one, nor do you.

>To return to "consanguinaceous", the suffixes "-ous" and "-aceous" are both
>clearly defined in the Webster's Ninth as adjectival suffixes with subtly
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>according to English rules.

Now we've switched roles. To me, if a word is not in the OED, it's not
a valid word. As a form of COMPRESSION, I replace common runs of
letters such as suffixes with one byte, then replace common runs of
THOSE values with a single byte. As a result, words ending in -ous,
-eous and -aceous are probably stored in the dictionary once .. as the
root plus one byte.

But I don't make up new words because they logically 'should' be in a
dictionary. 

My adjective gripe is hierarchical rather than hierocratic. Is IMS
'related to or derived from' other databases? No, it is 'a member of
the class' of bad databases. If it really were hierarchical, why not
shorten the word to hierarchic? Moreover, since when is a file system
an 'authority' or 'ruling body'?

When scores on the English section of the GED are classified by field
of study, computer science majors finish dead last .. below business,
even below education majors.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-02T08:43:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch7f3v$de7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cgusef$9u4$1@panix5.panix.com> <fo07j05mcb78ga01l7i9l9tslce0f7sn33@4ax.com> <ch06ge$1hp3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c998j0ttprb0lmotpai4pl0u9ke7ge0k9c@4ax.com> <ch26em$2ti6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <0tg9j097mvsvnvclj39ukmmojdo7qsa5jj@4ax.com> <i5u9j09pfe6jps8ep4klit0a9vsh3ckf4b@4ax.com> <dotaj0dtfqkhp7ub8tr330g1jut3qm5h0i@4ax.com> <ch4sck$1muf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3c5cj0thpk6gued451akolsbmu8560qp2e@4ax.com> <ch5b98$20rs$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <p0mcj0tmr9lfdpjli4k4pjo4v8bauq3tou@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:p0mcj0tmr9lfdpjli4k4pjo4v8bauq3tou@4ax.com...

> Now we've switched roles. To me, if a word is not in the OED, it's not
> a valid word.

No, I think you've misunderstood the role of the OED and the force
dictionaries in general have in the English-speaking world.

There is no government organization comparable to the Academie Francaise (or
the Real Academia Espanola? -- pardon the lack of accents and diacritics in
both cases) chartered by a government to define and delimit what is Proper
English and what constitutes a Genuine English Word.

American and English dictionaries are reflections of USAGE, in both written
and spoken contexts, not reflections of a government list of what that
government has decreed may legitimately be termed an English word.

The OED, in particular, is particularly good as a source for etymology and
historical usage -- most uncommon words include a history of the usage of
the word *as found in literature*.   But the OED is a *reflection of* usage.
To impart greater authority than that to this dictionary is to impart
authority to it that its composers do not claim.

    -Chuck Stevens

> As a form of COMPRESSION, I replace common runs of
> letters such as suffixes with one byte, then replace common runs of
> THOSE values with a single byte. As a result, words ending in -ous,
> -eous and -aceous are probably stored in the dictionary once .. as the
> root plus one byte.

If -ous means something different from -aceous, that's not too good an idea
...

> But I don't make up new words because they logically 'should' be in a
> dictionary.

And if no word in the dictionary exactly suffices?

> My adjective gripe is hierarchical rather than hierocratic. Is IMS
> 'related to or derived from' other databases? No, it is 'a member of
> the class' of bad databases. If it really were hierarchical, why not
> shorten the word to hierarchic? Moreover, since when is a file system
> an 'authority' or 'ruling body'?

OK, I've got a meeting in fifteen minutes, so I'm not going to explore the
semantics here.

> When scores on the English section of the GED are classified by field
> of study, computer science majors finish dead last .. below business,
> even below education majors.

And your point being ... ?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

Chuck Stevens wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:p0mcj0tmr9lfdpjli4k4pjo4v8bauq3tou@4ax.com...
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>
While I might be 'chuffed' as an ex-Brit at the place the OED holds - 
Chuck is right.. Usage is very important. The OED is constantly looking 
at Americanisms which have become a part of the language through usage, 
incorporate some, and perhaps leave out others. It's only within about 
the last 3 or 4  years (?),  that OED introduced "irregardless" because 
of its very common N. American usage.. You may possibly recall that  
pronunciation-wise I once mentioned, according to the OED, it's now OK 
for the Chairman of the Board to have sung 'Noooh Yorrrrk'  instead of  
'Nue Yawk'.

Even my spellchecker, set to UK English -  kicked out 'irregardless'.

Jimmy

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-02T11:53:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch7q6r$l1h$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cgusef$9u4$1@panix5.panix.com> <fo07j05mcb78ga01l7i9l9tslce0f7sn33@4ax.com> <ch06ge$1hp3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c998j0ttprb0lmotpai4pl0u9ke7ge0k9c@4ax.com> <ch26em$2ti6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <0tg9j097mvsvnvclj39ukmmojdo7qsa5jj@4ax.com> <i5u9j09pfe6jps8ep4klit0a9vsh3ckf4b@4ax.com> <dotaj0dtfqkhp7ub8tr330g1jut3qm5h0i@4ax.com> <ch4sck$1muf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3c5cj0thpk6gued451akolsbmu8560qp2e@4ax.com> <ch5b98$20rs$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <p0mcj0tmr9lfdpjli4k4pjo4v8bauq3tou@4ax.com> <ch7f3v$de7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <daJZc.301399$J06.16299@pd7tw2no>`

```
Note that I have both a Webster's Third Unabridged and a Compact Edition of
the OED (complete with magnifying glass) at home.  I regard both as
definitive (but not delimitative) for their respective dialects, and I
regard the OED as *far* superior in its recording of etymology, at the
possible expense of the number of entries.  .

All Merriam-Webster's dictionaries derive from either published or
working-draft versions of their Unabridged, and as far as I'm concerned all
other American English dictionaries are inferior to Merriam-Webster's
unabridged offering.

The OED stands alone.  I have nothing but the highest respect for it.

> You may possibly recall that
> pronunciation-wise I once mentioned, according to the OED, it's now OK
> for the Chairman of the Board to have sung 'Noooh Yorrrrk'  instead of
> 'Nue Yawk'.

Should have been all along.  What is often described as a "New York" accent
is, at least historically, an amalgam of the pronunciations heard in two of
the five boroughs of New York city than being typical of the city as a
whole, and certainly not historically typical of *Manhattan residents*.

There is not *a single* New York phonology, there are many, just as there
are for London and Boston.    My mother, born and raised on Staten Island,
and my father, raised on Manhattan (born upstate during a vacation), didn't
have a trace of the "Nue Yawk" accent.

They were, of course, recognizable (and to some degree shunned) as
"yankees" -- or is that "yankihs" -- when they lived in Florida and Texas.

        -Chuck Stevens
```

>If -ous means something different from -aceous, that's not too good an idea

My spelling checker doesn't know about syntax; it just looks up words
in a list.

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch445c$722$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<8a2qi094vn5ht6pfq3sa3rmvnpkihui5aq@4ax.com> <0tg9j097mvsvnvclj39ukmmojdo7qsa5jj@4ax.com> <ch31ih$r2$1@panix5.panix.com> <59uaj0hqucb9uqkju4podsda9ktft0d1qi@4ax.com>`

```
In article <59uaj0hqucb9uqkju4podsda9ktft0d1qi@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:
>On 31 Aug 2004 19:28:17 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>misspellings. Sorry. 

Mr Wagner, this sounds remarkably similar to statements put forward by 
some shops I have worked at... 'We've done studies and they show how Our 
Standards make for faster, more efficient programs.'

'When were the studies done?'

'Thirty years ago.'

'Has anyone seen these studies?'

'No... the report was limited to Upper Management.'

'Have they been duplicated since?  Things can change in a decade.'

'No.'

'So... you ask me to believe in something I cannot see and which hasn't 
been duplicated... sounds like a Religion, to me.'

'HERETIC!  BLASPHEMER!'

DD
```

On 1 Sep 2004 05:18:36 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <59uaj0hqucb9uqkju4podsda9ktft0d1qi@4ax.com>,

>>>>And most public-domain
>>>>dictionaries contain numerous UNintentional misspellings.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>easy for you since, as you have stated, 'most' contain these.

Ok, I went to Source Forge, which is one of the most reputable
sources, downloaded their American English and checked  with my
dictionary until I had 10 bad entries. For verification, I also found
them missing from the 1,000,000-word Merriam-Webster unabridged. I
don't have OED here. Do these look like valid words:

aphereses
adonized
novene
metathesized
jewelery   (common error)
wackes
reprized    (common error)
velor         (common error)
torses
deres

http://prdownloads.sourceforge.net/wordlist/scowl-6.zip?use_mirror=voxel
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T21:26:44-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch5ssk$q0b$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<8a2qi094vn5ht6pfq3sa3rmvnpkihui5aq@4ax.com> <59uaj0hqucb9uqkju4podsda9ktft0d1qi@4ax.com> <ch445c$722$1@panix5.panix.com> <jcnbj0hrvcbpo49lqp5ahrhqbte28qdc08@4ax.com>`

```
In article <jcnbj0hrvcbpo49lqp5ahrhqbte28qdc08@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:
>On 1 Sep 2004 05:18:36 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>aphereses

That looks to be a valid (compare Caesar with Cesar) variant for the 
plural of aphaeresis. (www.m-w-com)

>adonized

British formation (-ise vs. Amer. -ize) used to verb the noun Adon (OED, 
'a fop or exquisite'

>novene

Either an Anglicised form of NOVENA or 'preceded by nines' (both OED).

>metathesized

British formation (-ise) to verb the noun Metathesis (OED).

>jewelery   (common error)

Finally!

>wackes

Plural of wacke (OED)

>reprized    (common error)

obs f. reprise (OED)

>velor         (common error)

And a second!

>torses

plural of torse (OED)

>deres

plural of dere (OED)

>
>http://prdownloads.sourceforge.net/wordlist/scowl-6.zip?use_mirror=voxel
>

And so, Mr Wagner, most of the seem to be variants of OED-listed words... 
but those two, 'jewelery' and 'velor'...

... two errors in one dictionary become ' numerous UNintentional 
misspellings' in 'most public-domain dictionaries'... by what mechanism, 
please?

DD
```

Remember, this is a high-quality list that has been used by dozens to

lists that come from one individual are much worse.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
On 2 Sep 2004 05:20:20 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3c0dj0pr1ao45lbr8ued3a3pufk9anogfg@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:
>>On 1 Sep 2004 21:26:44 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>>I stopped at ten errors, found in a small fraction of the list (it is
>>not in alphabetical order). 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>'Now who're ya gonna believe... me or yer liein' eyes?'

You talk like a lawyer. 'You showed me two errors, so that's all the
errors there are.'

If E errors are found in a sample size of S, where 100S/N is the
percentage sampled, then we can extrapolate NE/S errors in the whole
polulation (plus or minus A standard deviations, blah blah). That's
Statistics 101. 

I sampled 10-20%, which indicates 20-10 errors in the list.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-09-02T07:47:34-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch718m$qu5$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<8a2qi094vn5ht6pfq3sa3rmvnpkihui5aq@4ax.com> <3c0dj0pr1ao45lbr8ued3a3pufk9anogfg@4ax.com> <ch6okk$k1o$1@panix5.panix.com> <h70ej0tmc6t4hvgd0j0a38gqa7nj6atbqr@4ax.com>`

```
In article <h70ej0tmc6t4hvgd0j0a38gqa7nj6atbqr@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:
>On 2 Sep 2004 05:20:20 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>errors there are.'

No, Mr Wagner.  You stated, clearly, unambiguously and without challenging 
or modifying the reprtition that there were 'numerous UNintentional 
misspellings' in 'most public-domain dictionaries'.

I requested that this assertion be supported with data.  The data you 
supplied showed two errors in a single public-domain dictionary.  

I asked - and you refused to answer once, so this is the second request 
for the same: 'two errors in one dictionary become ' numerous 
UNintentional misspellings' in 'most public-domain dictionaries'... by 
what mechanism, please?'

>
>If E errors are found in a sample size of S, where 100S/N is the
>percentage sampled, then we can extrapolate NE/S errors in the whole
>polulation (plus or minus A standard deviations, blah blah). That's
>Statistics 101. 

I did not ask about statistics, Mr Wagner, as I am familiar with how they 
have been ranked with lies and damned lies.  I asked for the mechanism you 
used to change the two errors in one dictionary you have shown to 
something that supports your assertion of something else, entire.

>
>I sampled 10-20%, which indicates 20-10 errors in the list.

Mr Wagner, you showed two errors in a single dictionary; these are not, by 
definition, 'numerous' ('consisting of great numbers of units or 
individuals', http://www.m-w.com) errors in 'most public-domain 
dictionaries' (plural vs. singular).

Your data, by the definitions shown, do not support your assertion; your 
assertion, then, remains unsupported.  It does not take a lawyer to see 
that, a mere COBOL-jockey might be capable of such.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding errors

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-02T11:46:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6s0ej0prmpu5539pu9imiafcf36f90u5c9@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<8a2qi094vn5ht6pfq3sa3rmvnpkihui5aq@4ax.com> <jcnbj0hrvcbpo49lqp5ahrhqbte28qdc08@4ax.com> <ch5ssk$q0b$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0409012344.3393cab6@posting.google.com> <ch6oo9$k69$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 2 Sep 2004 05:22:17 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <217e491a.0409012344.3393cab6@posting.google.com>,
>Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>or 'Kleenex' are 'valid words'.

Companies Defend Their Trademark in court by insisting the first
letter be upper-case. The court decides whether the word has become a
common noun. 

Winners: 
Kleenex
Xerox
Band-Aid
Google (as a noun but not as a verb, I think)
Realtor
Kelly Girl
Jeep
Frisbee

Losers:
escalator 
trampoline
raisin bran
dry ice
high octane
lanolin  
linoleum
nylon  
shredded wheat
kerosene 
yo-yo 
mimeograph
aspirin
heroin (invented by Beyer)
```

On 2 Sep 2004 11:51:30 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Nim is also a place in france which manufactured a very hardy material
>known originally as 'de Nim'.

Denim is distinguished by its thread pattern of colored warp and white
woof. Twill is the generic  name for jeans fabric, including solid
colored. 

When threads are diagonal rather than perpendicular, the fabric is
called serge. That's what they made in de Nimes.

Using denim to describe white jeans is a DOUBLE misnomer.
```

#### ↳ Re: Rounding errors

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T13:14:11-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10jc0u1eql5u2c8@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<s39mi0ti3ruelndhnqkekivbg8o39ip3v0@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:s39mi0ti3ruelndhnqkekivbg8o39ip3v0@4ax.com...
[snip]
> Our error
> is 500 parts per million.

Mr Wagner, this is not an accurate finding. I find that, for X/n,
where the remainders of X/n are perfectly distributed in
{0, 1, ..., n-1), the error, when n is even, is 0.5/n; when n is
odd, 0.0. It happens that when n=1000 (a factor in your
example), 0.5/n = 0.5/1000 = 500 ppm.

The errors introduced by rounding may be seen by example.
(Mr Wagner, I note that you made a similar observation but
did not extend it to the general case n, thus limiting the
accuracy of your finding to the single case where n=1000.)

For 2, {0, +1/2) = 0.5
For 3, (0, -1/3, +1/3) = 0.0
For 4, (0, -1/4, +2/4, +1/4) = 0.5
For 5, (0, -1/5, -2/5, +2/5, +1/5) = 0.0
For 6, (0, -1/6, -2/6, +3/6, +2/6, +1/6) = 0.5
And so on.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding errors

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T20:48:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0acj0d02rb4nu4f1maoaparrbte7b5lun@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<s39mi0ti3ruelndhnqkekivbg8o39ip3v0@4ax.com> <10jc0u1eql5u2c8@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Wed, 1 Sep 2004 13:14:11 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:s39mi0ti3ruelndhnqkekivbg8o39ip3v0@4ax.com...
>[snip]
>> Our error is 500 parts per million.

I realize now that I was mistaken to give the error rate in parts per
million. In the first example, rounding mills to cents, the error was
.0005 per LINE, which is 500 dollars per million LINES. The error is
not a percentage of the dollar amounts, as implied by the term ppm.

I was so accustomed to seeing error rates in ppm in other statistics,
I applied it here without enough thought.

>Mr Wagner, this is not an accurate finding. I find that, for X/n,
>where the remainders of X/n are perfectly distributed in
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>And so on.

Your numbers aren't incrementing by 1. 
Take the case of 0.0, 0.1, 0.2 ...0.9, 1.0  rounding to integer
For 11, (0, -.1, -.2, -.3, -.4, +.5, +.4, +.3, +.2, +.1, 0) =  .5 
Average per line: .045

For 1000, (0, -.1 ...+.1) = 50  average per line .05
For 1001, (0, -.1 ...+.1, 0) = 50  average per line .04995

For the general case of odd-membered sets, the correction to the
average is -.05 / (n - 1)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding errors

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T18:19:11-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10jcip694tucf8a@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<s39mi0ti3ruelndhnqkekivbg8o39ip3v0@4ax.com> <10jc0u1eql5u2c8@corp.supernews.com> <b0acj0d02rb4nu4f1maoaparrbte7b5lun@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:b0acj0d02rb4nu4f1maoaparrbte7b5lun@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 1 Sep 2004 13:14:11 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> Your numbers aren't incrementing by 1.

The perfectly distributed remainders are.

> Take the case of 0.0, 0.1, 0.2 ...0.9, 1.0  rounding to integer
> For 11, (0, -.1, -.2, -.3, -.4, +.5, +.4, +.3, +.2, +.1, 0) =  .5
> Average per line: .045

That case doesn't fit the conditions set forth, above.
Essentially, it is 11 separate cases of divide by 1, which
creates no remainder by the division and therefore no error.
The differences reported are the result of 11 separate moves.
In fact, by inserting a 12th value, 1.1, the additional adjustment
of -.1 brings the error down to .4.
```

---

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding (COBOL and otherwise)

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T13:04:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6hfbj09c01ddc2f4v8jel0mkb45vrgodob@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<i-CdnbS49dZubK7cRVn-rQ@comcast.com> <lit9j0dbqtfr351265jkg7dft4qe8fls0p@4ax.com> <t56dnTTJBaZ3pajcRVn-sA@comcast.com>`

```
On Tue, 31 Aug 2004 21:50:16 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>I thought that you had access to the 2002 Standard
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>communicating to the Standards committees since 1960 or so).

Yes, it's there for fixed-point. I was referring to floating-point
being under consideration for 2008, or at least on the wish list.

There was a tiny change made. Standard Arithmetic says intermediates
must be truncated. Under Native Arithmetic, it's likely they're being
rounded. That doesn't bother me because the high precision of Standard
intermediates minimizes loss of information. 

>Furthermore, during the time when J4/WG4 were soliciting ideas for the next 
>(2008) Standard, (a fact I advertised here) no one indicating any problems with 
>the ROUNDED phrase.

I didn't think of it at the time. 

>As Chuck has already indicated, you could certainly suggest such to J4/WG4.  My 
>guess is that (outside of POSSIBLY providing LIA support - probably post-2008), 
>there would be minimal support for any change to this phrase.

Chuck described in detail why he thought ROUNDING should piggy-back on
other numerical issues. 

Had I noticed that exponentiation was limited to integers in the range
0 through 4, I would have recommended it be changed to work like other
languages. Given that Cobol has a LOG function, how hard could that
be?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding (COBOL and otherwise)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T09:47:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch4ufe$1o6q$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<i-CdnbS49dZubK7cRVn-rQ@comcast.com> <lit9j0dbqtfr351265jkg7dft4qe8fls0p@4ax.com> <t56dnTTJBaZ3pajcRVn-sA@comcast.com> <6hfbj09c01ddc2f4v8jel0mkb45vrgodob@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:6hfbj09c01ddc2f4v8jel0mkb45vrgodob@4ax.com...

> Yes, it's there for fixed-point. I was referring to floating-point
> being under consideration for 2008, or at least on the wish list.

There is nothing in ISO/IEC 1989:2002 that prevents or prohibits any
implementor from defining a format for, say, FLOAT-EXTENDED that is
absolutely identical both in format and behavior to a SIDI.

USAGE FLOAT-SHORT, FLOAT-LONG and FLOAT-EXTENDED are also in ISO/IEC
1989:2002.

What *isn't required* is for the implementor to specify a USAGE
corresponding directly to SIDI's.

> There was a tiny change made. Standard Arithmetic says intermediates
> must be truncated. Under Native Arithmetic, it's likely they're being
> rounded.

Under native arithmetic the implementor defines the way arithmetic
expressions are evaluated.

> That doesn't bother me because the high precision of Standard
> intermediates minimizes loss of information.

But Standard Intermediate Data Items aren't used with *native* arithmetic.
They're used with *standard* arithmetic.

> I didn't think of it at the time.

A clear failing on the part of J4, WG4, and all the rest of the COBOL
community over the last forty years.  They should have known you wanted
this.

> Had I noticed that exponentiation was limited to integers in the range
> 0 through 4,

ummm.... no ....

In *standard* arithmetic, exponentiation is *not* limited to integers in the
range 0 through 4.

Given "COMPUTE A = X ** Y",
    When Y = 0, A = 1.
    When Y = 1, A = X.
    When Y = 2, A = X * X.
    When Y = 3, A = (X * X) * X.
    When Y = 4, A = ((X * X) * (X * X)).
    When Y = -1, A =  1 / X.
    When Y = -2, A = 1 / (X * X).
    When Y = -3, A = 1 / ((X * X) * X).
    When Y = -4, A = 1 / ((X * X) * (X * X)).
In *all* other instances, the result is implementor-defined.

In *native* arithmetic, the result is implementor-defined as for all
arithmetic operations.

> I would have recommended it be changed to work like other
> languages. Given that Cobol has a LOG function, how hard could that
> be?

Since I'm pretty sure you didn't know what it does now, I can't  help but
wonder as to your justification for changing it.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding (COBOL and otherwise)

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T09:31:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch4tgq$1noo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<i-CdnbS49dZubK7cRVn-rQ@comcast.com> <lit9j0dbqtfr351265jkg7dft4qe8fls0p@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:lit9j0dbqtfr351265jkg7dft4qe8fls0p@4ax.com...

> Isn't Standard Math under discussion for 2008?

Last I read it's in 2002 COBOL.  So far as I know it's not under discussion
for 2008.  Did I miss something?

> Someone will have to
> define how intermediates are rounded, hopefully only when absolutely
> necessary.

In standard arithmetic, "Any operand of an arithmetic expression that is not
already contained in a standard intermediate data item shall be converted
into a standard intermediate data item."  I've quoted that one before,
because it also covers "COMPUTE A= B".  A SIDI is an abstract, signed,
normalized, *decimal* floating-point data item with a three-digit exponent
and a 32-digit precision.  The rounding rules are specified, though I
suspect you might not like them.  See ISO/IEC 1989:2002 Pages 121-122,
8.8.1.3, Standard arithmetic.

> LIA-1 gives the implementor three choices. Seems to me
> there should be one, if programs are to produce the same result when
> ported to another compiler.

There already are TWO choices (rounding to zero and rounding to nearest) in
standard COBOL, and they work the same on all compilers.  It's just that
standard COBOL doesn't seem to provide syntax supporting the choice you
want.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding (COBOL and otherwise)

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T23:00:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<92kcj0d2sh08hu9eqhjqe550fhrs9031rp@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<i-CdnbS49dZubK7cRVn-rQ@comcast.com> <lit9j0dbqtfr351265jkg7dft4qe8fls0p@4ax.com> <ch4tgq$1noo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Wed, 1 Sep 2004 09:31:22 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>for 2008.  Did I miss something?

You talked of looking for a binary floating-point SIDI. Sounds to me
like development.

>> Someone will have to
>> define how intermediates are rounded, hopefully only when absolutely
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>8.8.1.3, Standard arithmetic.

The 'rounding' rule for decimal SIDIs is truncation. I was referring
to binary SIDIs, if we get them in the future.

>> LIA-1 gives the implementor three choices. Seems to me
>> there should be one, if programs are to produce the same result when
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>want.

You're talking about rounding the final result; I'm talking about
rounding intermediates.  For example:

compute x rounded = ((123456789 - .000000001) * 12) / 3.1416

            123456789.000000000
         + 000000000.000000001
        = 123456789.00000000?   first intermediate 
        *  000000012.000000000
        = 1481481468.0000000?? second intermediate
       /   0000000003.141600000
      =  4654222179.8688000?????? third intermediate
->  x   4654222179.8688000??
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rounding (COBOL and otherwise)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T16:18:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch5lcq$27fh$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<i-CdnbS49dZubK7cRVn-rQ@comcast.com> <lit9j0dbqtfr351265jkg7dft4qe8fls0p@4ax.com> <ch4tgq$1noo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <92kcj0d2sh08hu9eqhjqe550fhrs9031rp@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:92kcj0d2sh08hu9eqhjqe550fhrs9031rp@4ax.com...

> >Last I read it's in 2002 COBOL.  So far as I know it's not under
discussion
> >for 2008.  Did I miss something?

> You talked of looking for a binary floating-point SIDI. Sounds to me
> like development.

No.  Please don't misquote me.  The behavior of SIDI's *and* of standard
arithmetic is already defined in the standard.  What I think what you are
referring to is the possibility of using a BINARY128 format as defined in
the draft for IEEE 754 as a *container* for a SIDI.

> The 'rounding' rule for decimal SIDIs is truncation. I was referring
> to binary SIDIs, if we get them in the future.

SIDI's are *decimal* by definition.  You've already got what you want in the
FLOAT usages, and you're wrong about the rounding rules.   Standard COBOL
"rounded" logic applies; that's why we'd need new syntax (and thus
associated code) to handle the "other" rounding rules.

> You're talking about rounding the final result; I'm talking about
> rounding intermediates.  For example:

Start over with 32 decimal digits of precision.  18 digit maxima are *so*
1960's.

       -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ SIDI, Standard arithmetic, etc (was: Rounding (COBOL and otherwise)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-01T18:20:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vo6dndFJf-C7xKvcRVn-rQ@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<i-CdnbS49dZubK7cRVn-rQ@comcast.com> <lit9j0dbqtfr351265jkg7dft4qe8fls0p@4ax.com> <ch4tgq$1noo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <92kcj0d2sh08hu9eqhjqe550fhrs9031rp@4ax.com>`

```
Robert,
  Have you read the ISO 2002 Standard Arithmetic section?

COBOL's definition (current) for Standard Arithmetic *only* talks about 2 
operand operations.  It is quite clear that is all it is talking about.  If you 
have a more "complex" arithmetic expression, you do the single 2 (or 1) operand 
operation and store the results in an SIDI.

Therefore, when you say

 "You're talking about rounding the final result; I'm talking about  rounding 
intermediates"

and then go on and gtve an example, I think you are missing the entire approach 
used in the current Standard.

Again, it is always possible that some future standard will talk about other 
ways and types of arithmetic, but it would surprise me GREATLY if defining 
"intermediate results" (with or without rounding) will ever take the place of 
the current 1 or 2 operand operations and storing in an SIDI (whether the SIDI 
is decimal floating point, binary floating point, or other)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SIDI, Standard arithmetic, etc (was: Rounding (COBOL and otherwise)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-02T02:07:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2stcj0tsk76rcf1hmn9uvod5vlmedfv169@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<i-CdnbS49dZubK7cRVn-rQ@comcast.com> <lit9j0dbqtfr351265jkg7dft4qe8fls0p@4ax.com> <ch4tgq$1noo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <92kcj0d2sh08hu9eqhjqe550fhrs9031rp@4ax.com> <vo6dndFJf-C7xKvcRVn-rQ@comcast.com>`

```
On Wed, 1 Sep 2004 18:20:04 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Robert,
>  Have you read the ISO 2002 Standard Arithmetic section?

Yes. I was referring to these passages:

2) Standard intermediate data items are used to contain each operand
in an arithmetic expression and the result
of every arithmetic operation, arithmetic expression, and integer and
numeric intrinsic functions. A standard
intermediate data item is a conceptual data item that the implementor
implements as appropriate.

3) The binary arithmetic operators +, ï¿½, *, and / and the SQRT
function are defined to give results that are accurate
to 31 digits.

6) In each binary operation and the SQRT function, digits beyond the
thirty-second significant digit are truncated.
Truncation is a form of rounding that is also referred to as "rounding
to zero."

>COBOL's definition (current) for Standard Arithmetic *only* talks about 2 
>operand operations.  It is quite clear that is all it is talking about.  If you 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>used in the current Standard.

Here is an example taken from the standard. It looks like mine. It
shows the compiler, not the programmer, setting up SIDIs to hold
intermediates, here called ir-n. Note the word 'truncate'.

764 C: Concepts
COMPUTE D = A + B + C.

begin
calculate ir-1 = A + B
convert A to sidi                                  +0.80000 E+4
convert B to sidi                                 +0.30000 E+4
add (one possible representation)    +1.10000 E+4
normalize and truncate, store in ir-1 +0.11000 E+5
calculate ir-2 = ir-1 + C
no conversion needed for ir-1           +0.11000 E+5
convert C to sidi ï¿½                            0.40010 E+4
add (one possible representation)   +0.06999 E+5
normalize (truncation not needed), store in ir-2 +0.69990 E+4
store ir-2 in D
round from last                                +0.6999 E+4
move, D =                                        +6999

>Again, it is always possible that some future standard will talk about other 
>ways and types of arithmetic, but it would surprise me GREATLY if defining 
>"intermediate results" (with or without rounding) will ever take the place of 
>the current 1 or 2 operand operations and storing in an SIDI (whether the SIDI 
>is decimal floating point, binary floating point, or other)

It's possible we could  get a third arithmetic, called Portable, based
on an external standard such as LIA. 

My suggestion was to Standardize its options when used by Cobol,
specifically the way it rounds intermediates -- the  ir-n variables
above.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SIDI, Standard arithmetic, etc (was: Rounding (COBOL and otherwise)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-02T12:17:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ch7rjn$lro$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<i-CdnbS49dZubK7cRVn-rQ@comcast.com> <lit9j0dbqtfr351265jkg7dft4qe8fls0p@4ax.com> <ch4tgq$1noo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <92kcj0d2sh08hu9eqhjqe550fhrs9031rp@4ax.com> <vo6dndFJf-C7xKvcRVn-rQ@comcast.com> <2stcj0tsk76rcf1hmn9uvod5vlmedfv169@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:2stcj0tsk76rcf1hmn9uvod5vlmedfv169@4ax.com...

> Yes. I was referring to these passages:
>
> 2) Standard intermediate data items are used to contain each operand
> in an arithmetic expression and the result ...

Yeah, found your citations on page 763, starting at E.16, Standard
Arithmetic.

What I think you failed to note is a parenthetical entry on page 705,
namely, "(informative)" immediately under the heading Annex E, the line
immediately following that, namely, "Concepts", and the first paragraph
which reads "This annex describes major features in the language using
examples of the use of the features and textual discussion on how the
features function."

What you are reading in E.16 is a textual discussion that is intended to
*clarify* the rules, and should only be viewed in that context.   Note also
that the index entry has eight references to "standard arithmetic", of which
this is the *last*.

The "normative" description of "standard arithmetic" may be found starting
on page 121 under (mirabile dictu!) the heading "8.8.1.2 Standard
arithmetic".   What counts is the *rules* (in a *normative* part of the
standard) not a description "hey folks, here's kinda sorta how we see it
working" (in an *informative* part of the standard).

The "normative" part of the standard contains the *rules*; the "informative"
part contains commentaries and explanations.

Read the *rules*.

   -Chuck Stevens
```

>

…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>part contains commentaries and explanations.

Ok, the normative 8.8.1.2 is under 8.8.1 Arithmetic Expressions -- the
phrases found in COMPUTE statements, possibly containing parentheses.
They require intermediates provided by the compiler.

"The permissible combinations of identifiers, numeric literals,
arithmetic operators, and parentheses are given in table 4,
Combinations of symbols in arithmetic expressions."
