###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-08T23:13:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3u2fj01k5p28pca6imktrckrd1ulp1mbml@4ax.com> <d2lgj0tsrs9tu0tqkkk3dp9n3u5fr4fsn6@4ax.com> <cha3bn$2694$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <p68hj0p9f52si1kpsbs870up126ek6lfhh@4ax.com> <chaj3r$2h0n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <iscij015s0h8to660uehaog7lbnkjqvbdo@4ax.com> <chl5cc$hkq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <chl6u2$m95$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <j4isj0d9pjsoc92mf6h7tdmcbsnem3kf39@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409072338.3ded2d39@posting.google.com> <c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> .. and delete table extension(s) .. and possibly delete the BLL cell
> fixup code . 

Those are not _necessary_, but they may be done.  Even is _far_ from
being the "rewrite" that you claimed.

Changing from MFs SET ADDRESS (unsupported by Fujitsu) to
BASED-STORAGE now _that_ is a rewrite.
 
> A recurring beginners' question is 'why don't databases have OCCURS?'

Which seems completely irrellevant.  And besides I've never come
across that.

> Seems to me like good design. If it makes sense for structures on
> disk, why doesn't it make as much sense for structures in memory?

> In databases, 'table' is a misnomer. The structures are actually lists
> linked by a tree-like index.

In relational databases the data is held in 'tables' . They are
logically exactly the same as tables there is no 'misnomer'.  How
these are implemented is quite irrelevant and completely opaque. 
There is no reason that they can't be done exactly like your CISAM MF
files which are just a table with a _separate_ index.  In fact some
databases, notably Informix, which does, or at least did, use exactly
those CISAM files.

> You're confusing tables with  'direct' structures accessed via hash.
> How does one calculate the index number in a table?

How about: divide by 2 for a binary chop search ?  Try doing that on a
list.
 
> >Simplistic lists, as implemented in the way you have shown, are
> >generally slower than tables, and may be _much_ slower.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> and not the other.

Strangely they are also slower in Fujitsu.  In fact they are
inherently slower even when they are simple single linked lists.
 
> >Double linked lists, trees, and other exotic structures are far more
> >complex and error prone and may be considerably slower.
> 
> Talk about religion. You're asking the simplicity gods to protect us
> from our own mistakes. 

Exactly.  The principle is KISS. If a system can be made to work
reliably using simple components that are demonstrably error free then
only a fool would complexify it for no benefit.

> Lookups via a balanced tree run exactly as fast
> as a binary chop -- both take log2 (n) looks.

But they are _much_ slower to create.  They are also slower for each
'look'.

> In practice, there are
> two differences that make tree lookups faster:
 
> 1.  If the table is not truncated to its actual size by using Occurs
> Depending On, binary chop will take log2 (max) rather than log2 (n).

This does not make 'tree lookups faster'.  It may make a table binary
chop fractionally slower, but still faster overall than loading and
searching a tree.

> Simplicity advocates, hung by their own petard,  usually avoid ODO
> because it's 'too complicated'.

Well there yer go, everyone except Robert is just too stupid to do it
right.
 
And is it these same 'simplicity advocates' that you are trying to
encourage to code up double linked lists and tree traversals ?


> 2.  Binary chop REQUIRES the entries be sorted. If they aren't
> naturally, one must add sort time to the comparison. Balancing a tree
> is analogous to the sort time, but tree lookups don't REQUIRE that the
> tree be balanced. They run correctly albiet a little slower with an
> unbalanced tree 

When presented with ordered data they degrade to a linked list with
only the right branch ever used and thus work much slower then a
single linked list.

To produce a reasonable balanced tree either they must be given data
ordered randomly, for example by sorting using a random number as the
key, or the tree must be rotated when unbalance is out by given
amount.

Rotation code can be very complex, with a need to detect when it is
actually required. Poorly done it can become unstable in some cases
when the rotation is done and then undone on the next access.

> (think of an indexed file requiring reorganization).
> Overall speed depends on the ratio of looks vs. organization time.
> When the number of looks is less than approximately n, tree lookup is
> faster because it doesn't have the overhead of organization.

You were wrong when you claimed simple lists were faster. There may be
specific cases where loading a tree does produce an insignicant time
saving over a SORT plus SEARCH ALL, and it would be _much_ slower if
the data were naturally ordered.

But quite frankly I wouldn't care it was faster or not. I know from
having done it how complex trees can be and I really don't see the
point in recoding 'what C did in the 70s'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-09T22:22:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<d2lgj0tsrs9tu0tqkkk3dp9n3u5fr4fsn6@4ax.com> <cha3bn$2694$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <p68hj0p9f52si1kpsbs870up126ek6lfhh@4ax.com> <chaj3r$2h0n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <iscij015s0h8to660uehaog7lbnkjqvbdo@4ax.com> <chl5cc$hkq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <chl6u2$m95$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <j4isj0d9pjsoc92mf6h7tdmcbsnem3kf39@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409072338.3ded2d39@posting.google.com> <c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com>`

```
On 8 Sep 2004 23:13:09 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

>Changing from MFs SET ADDRESS (unsupported by Fujitsu) to
>BASED-STORAGE now _that_ is a rewrite.

SET ADDRESS is unsupported but implemented by Fujitsu. 

I really doubt compiler companies will delete extensions that once
worked. When Micro Focus or IBM releases a 2002 compiler, do you think
we'll have to rewrite SET ADDRESS to BASED-STORAGE?

>> In databases, 'table' is a misnomer. The structures are actually lists
>> linked by a tree-like index.
>
>In relational databases the data is held in 'tables' . They are
>logically exactly the same as tables there is no 'misnomer'. 

One cannot insert a row between two existing ones in a Cobol table.

>> You're confusing tables with  'direct' structures accessed via hash.
>> How does one calculate the index number in a table?
>
>How about: divide by 2 for a binary chop search ?  Try doing that on a
>list.

Try doing that on an unsorted table. While you're sorting the table,
I'll build a tree. Never mind, it's already built. I'll do the lookups
in the unbalanced tree while you're sorting. Want to see it again?
I'll do them a second time while you're STILL sorting.
 
>> >Double linked lists, trees, and other exotic structures are far more
>> >complex and error prone and may be considerably slower.

Sorting is inherently complex too. We hide that from the user by
putting the logic in a library called by the SORT verb. The same could
have  done for lists.

In the old days, structures natively supported by the language, such
as tables, had a simplicity advantage over structures we had to manage
ourselves. OO changed the balance. We can now create types and
supporting methods that are nearly as easy to use. 

Someone said 'Two things would sicken the average citizen: the inside
of a slaughterhouse and seeing how government actually works.' We seem
to have found a third in data management algorithms.

>> Talk about religion. You're asking the simplicity gods to protect us
>> from our own mistakes. 
>
>Exactly.  The principle is KISS. 

The principle is MISS -- Make It Simple, Stupid .. even if it isn't.

>If a system can be made to work
>reliably using simple components that are demonstrably error free then
>only a fool would complexify it for no benefit.

A pointer to the next entry in a list is no more complex than adding 1
to a subscript. In fact, it's simpler. I don't have to multiply by the
size of an occurrence to find it.

>> Lookups via a balanced tree run exactly as fast
>> as a binary chop -- both take log2 (n) looks.
>
>But they are _much_ slower to create.  They are also slower for each
>'look'.

Do you have evidence? I posted code that did it both ways and measured
total build and lookup time to be:

 MF table sort, search all    5.1
 tree, lookup (unbalanced) 2.6
 tree, balance, lookup        2.6

>> Simplicity advocates, hung by their own petard,  usually avoid ODO
>> because it's 'too complicated'.
>
>Well there yer go, everyone except Robert is just too stupid to do it
>right.

I didn't say everyone; I said simplicity advocates.

>And is it these same 'simplicity advocates' that you are trying to
>encourage to code up double linked lists and tree traversals ?

No, I posted that for people wanting to learn how to program. Others
will continue doing it the slow way until someone hands them an
easy-to-use package.

>> 2.  Binary chop REQUIRES the entries be sorted. If they aren't
>> naturally, one must add sort time to the comparison. Balancing a tree
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>single linked list.

In that case, the lookup could switch to binary chop. I posted a demo
that sorted by rotating the tree until everything was on the right,
then did lookups with binary chop. 

 tree, to table, search all     2.9 

If inputs had been in sequence, rotation would have stopped after one
pass. Time would have been 1.3.

>To produce a reasonable balanced tree either they must be given data
>ordered randomly, for example by sorting using a random number as the
>key, or the tree must be rotated when unbalance is out by given
>amount.

Random inputs do not produce a well-balanced tree. One has to
unbalance it until everything is on the right (or left), in other
words sort the entries. Only then can a balanced tree be built. 

>Rotation code can be very complex, with a need to detect when it is
>actually required. Poorly done it can become unstable in some cases
>when the rotation is done and then undone on the next access.

'I tried that once and ran into problems. It follows, therefore, that
nobody else should be allowed to use it.' I once worked for a manager
who set shop standards by that logic. She wouldn't allow Cobol SORT
because she had trouble with it ten years earlier.

>You were wrong when you claimed simple lists were faster. There may be
>specific cases where loading a tree does produce an insignificant time
>saving over a SORT plus SEARCH ALL, 

See above. Twice the speed is not insignificant. The specific case was
50,000 random 30-byte keys, which seems typical of everyday use.

>and it would be _much_ slower if the data were naturally ordered.

It would be the same, because tree search would detect that and switch
to binary chop.

>But quite frankly I wouldn't care it was faster or not. I know from
>having done it how complex trees can be and I really don't see the
>point in recoding 'what C did in the 70s'.

It is a prelude to managing more complex structures in databases. You
have to crawl before you can walk.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ "Undocumented Extensions" (was: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-09T22:32:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dS40d.1072808$6p.185328@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<d2lgj0tsrs9tu0tqkkk3dp9n3u5fr4fsn6@4ax.com> <cha3bn$2694$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <p68hj0p9f52si1kpsbs870up126ek6lfhh@4ax.com> <chaj3r$2h0n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <iscij015s0h8to660uehaog7lbnkjqvbdo@4ax.com> <chl5cc$hkq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <chl6u2$m95$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <j4isj0d9pjsoc92mf6h7tdmcbsnem3kf39@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409072338.3ded2d39@posting.google.com> <c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com>`

```
Robert,
  We have been thru this before.  Some compiler vendors continue support for 
"undocumented extensions" and some don't.  Any programmer relying on such, seems 
silly to me (but YMMV).

For an example of exactly HOW many undocumented extensions IBM dropped support 
for, see:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3MG20/3.3.14

   and

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3MG20/4.1.6

  ***

Unlike IBM,  Micro Focus often (not quite always) adds new directives, cf
   OLDNEXT-SENTENCE
  and several other OLDxxxx directives

Anyone who assumes that Fujitsu will always support their current (undocumented) 
SET ADDRESS OF (not conforming to ISO 2002) syntax, will EVENTUALLY find out if 
this was or wasn't a safe assumption.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-09T21:07:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d2lgj0tsrs9tu0tqkkk3dp9n3u5fr4fsn6@4ax.com> <p68hj0p9f52si1kpsbs870up126ek6lfhh@4ax.com> <chaj3r$2h0n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <iscij015s0h8to660uehaog7lbnkjqvbdo@4ax.com> <chl5cc$hkq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <chl6u2$m95$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <j4isj0d9pjsoc92mf6h7tdmcbsnem3kf39@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409072338.3ded2d39@posting.google.com> <c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> SET ADDRESS is unsupported but implemented by Fujitsu. 
> 
> I really doubt compiler companies will delete extensions that once
> worked. When Micro Focus or IBM releases a 2002 compiler, do you think
> we'll have to rewrite SET ADDRESS to BASED-STORAGE?

You may have to.
 
> >> In databases, 'table' is a misnomer. The structures are actually lists
> >> linked by a tree-like index.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> One cannot insert a row between two existing ones in a Cobol table.

Nor can one in a file such as a data file as used by CISAM, the well
known Informix underlying file system.

> Try doing that on an unsorted table. 

Of course it can be done.  It may not be meaningful in that case, but
it can be done.

> While you're sorting the table,
> I'll build a tree. 

While you are writing the code and debugging the rotations, I'll just
do a SORT with an output procedure.

> Sorting is inherently complex too. We hide that from the user by
> putting the logic in a library called by the SORT verb. The same could
> have  done for lists.

Please do supply a library for this.  When there is one that works and
is generally available and generic then I may be bothered to compare
it to the handful of code required to do this task adequately for the
last few decades.

 > In the old days, structures natively supported by the language,
such
> as tables, had a simplicity advantage over structures we had to manage
> ourselves. OO changed the balance. We can now create types and
> supporting methods that are nearly as easy to use. 

Only 'nearly'.  I would have no problem using a collection class, what
I am criticising is your 'what C did in the 70s approach'.

> Someone said 'Two things would sicken the average citizen: the inside
> of a slaughterhouse and seeing how government actually works.' We seem
> to have found a third in data management algorithms.

I've been there, done that.  They work but are insignificantly an
advantage and are mostly a pain.  If I want an infinite sized
collection to be searched and ordered I'll throw it at a database
table or even an ISAM file where it has the advantages of being shared
and permanent (if required).
 
> >Exactly.  The principle is KISS. 
> 
> The principle is MISS -- Make It Simple, Stupid .. even if it isn't.

You'll have to explain that one, or was it just a meaningless random
crack.

> A pointer to the next entry in a list is no more complex than adding 1
> to a subscript. In fact, it's simpler. 

Excuse me but you were advocating trees with rotations, suddenly ity
has reverted to serial linking.

> I don't have to multiply by the size of an occurrence to find it. 

Show me some _Cobol_ where the programmer does have to do this.


> No, I posted that for people wanting to learn how to program. 

They could get that from any 70s C tutorial.

> Others
> will continue doing it the slow way until someone hands them an
> easy-to-use package.

Exactly.  It works, it is standard, everyone can see how it works at
2am.

Even if (and I emphasise if) the tree is faster then CPU time is a
trivial saving compared to programmer complexity costs.
 
What was cost effective in the 70s is irrelevant today.

> >When presented with ordered data they degrade to a linked list with
> >only the right branch ever used and thus work much slower then a
> >single linked list.
> 
> In that case, the lookup could switch to binary chop. 

Do you do this by dividing the pointer by 2 ?

> >To produce a reasonable balanced tree either they must be given data
> >ordered randomly, for example by sorting using a random number as the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Random inputs do not produce a well-balanced tree. 

Yes it does, or at least it produces an acceptable one that is far
better than ordered.

> >Rotation code can be very complex, with a need to detect when it is
> >actually required. Poorly done it can become unstable in some cases
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> because she had trouble with it ten years earlier.

You may note, as you have failed to do in your first attempt at
reading, that I did not 'disallow' the use of such code.  Your counter
is an irrelevancy.  I pointed out that it can be complex and can
become unstable.  This results in additional costs of programming
and/or slow performance.


> See above. Twice the speed is not insignificant. The specific case was
> 50,000 random 30-byte keys, which seems typical of everyday use.

I would doubt that is in any way 'typical' of data that needs to be
stored in a table in a program. I am sure that you can contrive to
find such atypical cases as credit card companies where there may be a
dozen such in the world.

> >and it would be _much_ slower if the data were naturally ordered.
> 
> It would be the same, because tree search would detect that and switch
> to binary chop.

You have just added another level of complexity to the code you are
advocating everyone to reinvent.  While you are debugging the 'detect
that' code, my programmes will be running yet again.

> It is a prelude to managing more complex structures in databases. You
> have to crawl before you can walk.

If I ever need to write another database server than I will revist
such structures in the meantime I will just use the database that I
have and write such client programs in Cobol that my clients pay me to
do.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-10T20:33:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<chaj3r$2h0n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <iscij015s0h8to660uehaog7lbnkjqvbdo@4ax.com> <chl5cc$hkq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <chl6u2$m95$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <j4isj0d9pjsoc92mf6h7tdmcbsnem3kf39@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409072338.3ded2d39@posting.google.com> <c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com>`

```
On 9 Sep 2004 21:07:54 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

>> >> In databases, 'table' is a misnomer. The structures are actually lists
>> >> linked by a tree-like index.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>known Informix underlying file system.

The underlying file system is irrelevant. In database view, one can
not only insert a row in the middle, it can even have a duplicate key.
Try creating duplicate subscripts in a Cobol table.

>> While you're sorting the table,
>> I'll build a tree. 
>
>While you are writing the code and debugging the rotations, I'll just
>do a SORT with an output procedure.

We can sort a file and we can sort a table (2002 & MF). Why can't we
say SORT .. GIVING tablename?

>> Sorting is inherently complex too. We hide that from the user by
>> putting the logic in a library called by the SORT verb. The same could
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>last few decades.

Most compilers already do. It's generically called a collection class,
specifically a dictionary. J4 appears to be standardizing the class.

> > In the old days, structures natively supported by the language,
>such
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Only 'nearly'. 

I wish we could invoke a method the way we call an intrinsic function
-- by saying its name. A method is a verb. When a verb is the object
of another verb it becomes a gerund, which is a substantive (noun),
and should be ended with -ing. For example, 'invoke file read' is
ungrammatical; it should be 'invoke file reading', or better 'invoke
reading of file'. Replace 'invoke' with 'start' to see the error more
clearly.

A possible solution is turning the verb into an infinitive (noun) by
adding the word 'to'. 'He toils to read the file'  is equivalent to
'He is reading the file'. But we want to avoid split infinitives,
which have another word between 'to' and the verb. For instance, 'To
boldly go where no man has gone before.'  Bad adverb .. bad, BAD.
Infinitives won't work in Cobol because the noun (object) is between
invoke and the verb. 

Literate programmers will just have to name their methods in gerundial
form -- opening, reading, finding, etc.

[Gerunds should not be confused with present participles, which also
end -ing but function as adjectives.]

> I would have no problem using a collection class, what
>I am criticising is your 'what C did in the 70s approach'.

You object to MY approach? Perhaps I should post under an alter ego.

>  If I want an infinite sized
>collection to be searched and ordered I'll throw it at a database
>table or even an ISAM file where it has the advantages of being shared
>and permanent (if required).

Informix temp tables reside in memory and are automagically defined.
They offer the benefit of database logic without the overhead of disk
access, separate definition and security issues. When the job ends,
they disappear in puff of smoke.

>> >Exactly.  The principle is KISS. 
>> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>crack.

It was a swipe at simplicity advocates. For example, operations people
who try to do real programming with scripting languages. 

>> Others
>> will continue doing it the slow way until someone hands them an
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>2am.

Everyone's not a dumb consumer. Someone has to write those easy-to-use
tools. I was talking to the tool makers and anyone curious about the
subject.

>Even if (and I emphasise if) the tree is faster then CPU time is a
>trivial saving compared to programmer complexity costs.

Like matter and energy, complexity is conserved. It doesn't go away,
it just gets repackaged. When you say "programmer complexity", you are
referring to Application Programmers, not all programmers. An analogy
is PC hardware. In the early '80s, we had to deal with IRQs, port
addresses and DMA channels. The life of a computer consumer was
complicated. Today, those things (or their analogs) are still inside
the computer but they are hidden from your view. They are managed by
software  (Plug 'n Play) or hardware (SCSI controller). Complexity
didn't decrease; it moved upstream .. from the user's desk to the
factory. In the same way, software complexity is moving from the
Application Programmer domain to the software factory e.g. database
provider.

>What was cost effective in the 70s is irrelevant today.

Consider airline reservations. In the '70s, the industry handled
speed and volume problems by writing everything in assembly language,
and paid the complexity price. Later, their complex code base was no
longer necessary or relevant, so they rewrote in modern languages,
Unix and MySQL. Complexity hadn't changed, it moved from application
code to MySQL code.

>> >When presented with ordered data they degrade to a linked list with
>> >only the right branch ever used and thus work much slower then a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Do you do this by dividing the pointer by 2 ?

By using SEARCH ALL. In the demo, tree nodes lived  in a table.
```

William M. Klein wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message 
>news:2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com...
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…

Just picked up on this one. I for sure DON'T like the 'inline 
invocation' - I'd put that one in the category where you said 'COBOL was 
being C-ified' - too cryptic. Sure don't much care for Vocabulary.

Now what is Robert's problem with  using a verb - INVOKE <someObject> -

<PC_PRINT/CBL_FILE/Screen-Attribute routine> ????  While COBOL is

grammatical shortcuts and still convey an idea. Anyway Robert, be 
assured it aint gonna be changed.

Jimmy

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-09-10T21:52:41-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r_s0d.44312$Nd6.1344703@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<%Mq0d.365773$M95.203590@pd7tw1no>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> William M. Klein wrote:
> 
>> "Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message 

> Just picked up on this one. I for sure DON'T like the 'inline 
> invocation' - I'd put that one in the category where you said 'COBOL was 
> being C-ified' - too cryptic. Sure don't much care for Vocabulary.
> 

I rather do like it.
	
   Move customer-number to
    customer-number of customer-object.
:
is a rather elegant way of reading an isam file. Creates it if empty, 
rebuilds it if buggered(or asks if it should be restored from backup) 
flags it as a syntax error at compile time if the arguements mismatch, 
etc., etc.

Cannot see that it looks much like C either, to tell the truth.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-09-11T03:46:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Cyu0d.367401$M95.25963@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<r_s0d.44312$Nd6.1344703@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<chaj3r$2h0n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <iscij015s0h8to660uehaog7lbnkjqvbdo@4ax.com> <chl5cc$hkq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <chl6u2$m95$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <j4isj0d9pjsoc92mf6h7tdmcbsnem3kf39@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409072338.3ded2d39@posting.google.com> <c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <VIo0d.1122395$6p.192124@news.easynews.com> <%Mq0d.365773$M95.203590@pd7tw1no> <r_s0d.44312$Nd6.1344703@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:

> James J. Gavan wrote:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Correct me if I'm wrong Donald but that isn't an in-line invocation so 
far as I know - or is it ?  In-line invocation is dependent upon that 
"double colon". Following from the N/E 4.0 manual - which is very much 
up-to-date :-
------------------------------------


      In-line Method Invocation

You can invoke a method in-line, as long as it has a returning 
parameter, by coding the object reference followed by the method and 
separated by two colons in place of an operand in a statement, for example:

move anAccount::"getBalance" to current-balance

This is equivalent to the following INVOKE statement:

invoke anAccount "getBalance" returning current-balance

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-11T04:08:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4Tu0d.1139701$6p.195331@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<chaj3r$2h0n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <iscij015s0h8to660uehaog7lbnkjqvbdo@4ax.com> <chl5cc$hkq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <chl6u2$m95$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <j4isj0d9pjsoc92mf6h7tdmcbsnem3kf39@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409072338.3ded2d39@posting.google.com> <c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <VIo0d.1122395$6p.192124@news.easynews.com> <%Mq0d.365773$M95.203590@pd7tw1no> <r_s0d.44312$Nd6.1344703@news20.bellglobal.com> <Cyu0d.367401$M95.25963@pd7tw1no>`

```
What Donald was using was "implicit" Get/Set-property syntax.  Inline invocation 
would be something like:

Move  customer-object::customer-number ("Next") to Next-Cust-Number
```

On Sat, 11 Sep 2004 08:45:27 -0400, Donald Tees
<donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>James J. Gavan wrote:
>> Donald Tees wrote:
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>AH!  now I can see why Robert dislikes it.  Sorry about the misnomer.

James Gavan wrote the passage about not liking it.

I said, in another thread, the double colon syntax was copied from C++
without objecting to it. The danger of that form is permitting C-isms
such as:

if  file::open(filename) equal to zero
    perform until end-of-file
       ....
    end-perform
else
    display 'open error ...'
end-if

in which the primary activity of the first line is opening a file, but
the syntax looks like an if statement.
```

```
On Fri, 10 Sep 2004 23:28:59 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
wrote:

>Now what is Robert's problem with  using a verb - INVOKE <someObject> - 

<someObject> is just a qualifier.  The gist of the statement is INVOKE
<someObject> "Method". The program is performing an action and the
double verb reveals grammatical clunkiness.

>where does if differ from another verb - CALL <someProgram> or CALL 
><PC_PRINT/CBL_FILE/Screen-Attribute routine> ????  While COBOL is 
>*close* to the English language - there's no valid reason you can't take 
>grammatical shortcuts and still convey an idea. Anyway Robert, be 
>assured it aint gonna be changed.

This is a Brooklyn Bridge defense. 'Everyone else is doing it
wrong...'

It seems to me that English grammar has a better handle on what makes
sense than illiterate techie constuctions .. however cloaked they are
in polysyllabic words. Techies just don't get it. They try to conceal
their ignorance under a panoply of abstruse constructions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-11T23:37:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a8k6k0h450vdg6k90lkm70d5cjq31k30ne@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409072338.3ded2d39@posting.google.com> <c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <VIo0d.1122395$6p.192124@news.easynews.com> <%Mq0d.365773$M95.203590@pd7tw1no> <2a05k0pn0hqqtfsf67tc598dpqqskl0je1@4ax.com> <dIG0d.391290$gE.384540@pd7tw3no>`

```
On Sat, 11 Sep 2004 17:36:09 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>>>where does if differ from another verb - CALL <someProgram> or CALL 
>>><PC_PRINT/CBL_FILE/Screen-Attribute routine> ????  While COBOL is 
>>>*close* to the English language - there's no valid reason you can't take 
>>>grammatical shortcuts and still convey an idea. Anyway Robert, be 
>>>assured it aint gonna be changed.

>>It seems to me that English grammar has a better handle on what makes
>>sense than illiterate techie constuctions .. however cloaked they are
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>currently,  the J4 folks !

I wasn't thinking of J4 when I wrote that .. honest; I was thinking of
OO nomenclature and other pretentious language. In truth, I wasn't
thinking at all else I would have answered your question about the
difference between a method and a called program.

I think of natural and programming languages as two-dimensional,
having nouns on one axis and verbs on the other. Language is
describing the intersection between them. Because our communication
channel is one-dimensional, we intersperse verbs and nouns in a way
that makes it easy for the receiver (reader) to accurately recreate
the rows and columns in our mind, and thereby see the same
intersection.

OO makes relationships unambiguous by linking an action to its target
with the  symbol ::. A called program, on the other hand,  isn't an
ACTION, it's an ACTOR -- a lump of code that could perform many
actions, depending on its internal decisions. Using your example,
CBL_FILE is not a single action, it's a file system. The statement
'call CBL_FILE using 'open' filename' is similar to 'call your mom and
ask her to start dinner'. The requested action is communicated in a
message. OO lets us bypass the middle-man and talk directly to the
object.
```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> <someObject> is just a qualifier.  The gist of the statement is INVOKE
> <someObject> "Method". The program is performing an action and the
> double verb reveals grammatical clunkiness.

I would suggest that you may be happier with some other language. 
Perhaps you should try comp.lang.perl.  There you can write the
programs using just about any word order you would prefer.
 
> It seems to me that English grammar has a better handle on what makes
> sense than illiterate techie constuctions .. however cloaked they are
> in polysyllabic words. Techies just don't get it. They try to conceal
> their ignorance under a panoply of abstruse constructions.

I would suggest that you may be happier if you design your own
language that would suit your own ideas of perfection.  I would be
quite happy to give pointers on how to set up
comp.lang.wagner_is_perfect.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-10T23:31:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<chl5cc$hkq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <chl6u2$m95$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <j4isj0d9pjsoc92mf6h7tdmcbsnem3kf39@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409072338.3ded2d39@posting.google.com> <c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <VIo0d.1122395$6p.192124@news.easynews.com>`

On Fri, 10 Sep 2004 21:08:05 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message 
>news:2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>people do)

Wow! I love both. I didn't know they existed until you just pointed
them out and I read about them. Inline invocation is basically a copy
of C++ syntax. Vocabulary cuts to the heart. It lets us define 'Cobol
verbs' and 'Cobol types'.  Micro Focus has turned Cobol into a meta
language. The ability to roll your own language in a portable way is
at once intoxicating,  powerful, and very dangerous.

Thanks for the educadtion.

Sincerely,
Robert
```

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<chl5cc$hkq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <chl6u2$m95$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <j4isj0d9pjsoc92mf6h7tdmcbsnem3kf39@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409072338.3ded2d39@posting.google.com> <c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Fri, 10 Sep 2004 19:56:15 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>The name of a method is a noun.

It is when it's the object of INVOKE, in which case it should be
written in gerundial form. For example, the name of the method that
writes to a Dictionary should be 'putting' rather than 'put'.
```

- **References:** `<chl5cc$hkq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <chl6u2$m95$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <j4isj0d9pjsoc92mf6h7tdmcbsnem3kf39@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409072338.3ded2d39@posting.google.com> <c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-11T15:06:43-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<chl5cc$hkq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <chl6u2$m95$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <j4isj0d9pjsoc92mf6h7tdmcbsnem3kf39@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409072338.3ded2d39@posting.google.com> <c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 10 Sep 2004 19:56:15 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> writes to a Dictionary should be 'putting' rather than 'put'.

Mr Wagner, consider that a method is an identified, perhaps
named, procedure (function, process, recipe, etc.) for doing
something. Now consider the difference between
ChocolateCake:'Baking' and ChocolateCake:'Bake'.
The first suggests the recipe itself. The second a command
to follow the recipe. Likewise, Dictionary:'Putting' is the
procedure. Dictionary:'Put' is the command.

This suggests that the name of a method, that which is to be
invoked, *should be* a command, not a form that refers to
the description of that command.

One other point, Mr Wagner. INVOKE in COBOL has
a similarity to LET in BASIC. It is not necessary for one to
mentally process the word to understand that a command
is to be executed, though INVOKE is necessary for syntax.
Consider that
    [Invoke] EmployeeMaster:'Read' using EmployeeRecord
is understandable without 'Invoke' and, under certain conditions
is equivalent to the COBOL statement
    Read EmployeeMaster into EmployeeRecord.
That is, under certain conditions, method names have an
equivalence to COBOL verbs and may be considered as
extension to the instruction set.

Given the subject of this thread, Mr Wagner, would your
COBOL instructions be READING, WRITING, ADDING,
SUBTRACTING, etc.?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-09-11T17:16:23-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<w1K0d.36038$lP4.2449061@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<chl5cc$hkq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <chl6u2$m95$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <j4isj0d9pjsoc92mf6h7tdmcbsnem3kf39@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409072338.3ded2d39@posting.google.com> <c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> "Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message

<big snip>

>     [Invoke] EmployeeMaster:'Read' using EmployeeRecord
> is understandable without 'Invoke' and, under certain conditions
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

YES!
Yes, yes yes.

The perfect cobol program includes all its own statements at the source 
level as objects, as well as compiled code at the object-code level. 
When you invoke the method "write_local_cobol_code" for the object, then 
it generates Cobol code consistant with the Local_Dialect. When you 
invoke the verb, it executes.

The problem is the chicken and egg problem.  Once it is written, it can 
re-write itself for any platform, but you cannot write it until you can 
execute it to generate it's own code.

Donald.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-12T12:01:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<j4isj0d9pjsoc92mf6h7tdmcbsnem3kf39@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409072338.3ded2d39@posting.google.com> <c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Sat, 11 Sep 2004 15:06:43 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>to follow the recipe.

Compare 'Start baking a cake' to 'Start bake cake'. The latter is how
two-year-olds talk.

>One other point, Mr Wagner. INVOKE in COBOL has
>a similarity to LET in BASIC. It is not necessary for one to
>mentally process the word to understand that a command
>is to be executed, though INVOKE is necessary for syntax.

In 'Let A take the value of B' and 'Let A equal B', the object of Let
is A. There is no need to modify the verbs because they are not the
objects of Let.

>Consider that
>    [Invoke] EmployeeMaster:'Read' using EmployeeRecord
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>extension to the instruction set.

This is true. It explains why Inline Invocation is better than INVOKE
-- because it lets us say the verb in the same way we say normal Cobol
verbs such as READ.

>Given the subject of this thread, Mr Wagner, would your
>COBOL instructions be READING, WRITING, ADDING,
>SUBTRACTING, etc.?

That would be wrong because they are not preceeded by a verb. 

If they were, the form would be PERFORM READING, DO WRITING, CARRY OUT
ADDING, etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-12T10:15:49-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<j4isj0d9pjsoc92mf6h7tdmcbsnem3kf39@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409072338.3ded2d39@posting.google.com> <c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 11 Sep 2004 15:06:43 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >> I wish we could invoke a method the way we call an intrinsic
function
> >> >> -- by saying its name. A method is a verb.
> >> >
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> two-year-olds talk.

Consider, *preparing* (the act of a person who prepares)
Wagner's Vegetarian Medley cannot exist independently of
Wagner writing the recipe. Wagner is not necessary to
*prepare* the dish according to the recipe.

Also, invoke has a sense of 'do the following'. Thus "Do the
following, 'Baking a cake'", makes no sense; but "Do the
following, 'Bake a cake'", does make sense. I have not yet
found a sense of invoke the includes 'start'.

[invoke: to cause, call forth, or bring about. -- RHCD]

> >One other point, Mr Wagner. INVOKE in COBOL has
> >a similarity to LET in BASIC. It is not necessary for one to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> objects of Let.

As I show above, the entire command is the object of invoke,
not just the method name. Computer languages are not natural
languages. But, if one defines 'this command' as the text
following 'invoke', then 'invoke this command' gives the
verb-noun combination you're trying to achieve. And, this is
why 'invoke' is not necessary to the understanding.

> >Consider that
> >    [Invoke] EmployeeMaster:'Read' using EmployeeRecord
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> verbs such as READ.

Mr Wagner, better? Or just different? INVOKE is required
for syntax. It is not required for understanding. Treating
INVOKE as more than it is invites confusion.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-12T17:25:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Sun, 12 Sep 2004 10:15:49 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>*prepare* the dish according to the recipe.

The definition you gave is either the gerundial or intransitive form
of prepare. An intransitive has no direct object, therefore could not
take the command is its object. Example: "He is  preparing for a
carreer." By citing the gerundial form, you supported my argument.

>Also, invoke has a sense of 'do the following'. Thus "Do the
>following, 'Baking a cake'", makes no sense; but "Do the
>following, 'Bake a cake'", does make sense. I have not yet
>found a sense of invoke the includes 'start'.

"Do the following" is a complete sentence in which following is an
adjective and the word action has been elided. Its full form is "Do
the following action:", where action is the object of do. "Bake a
cake" is also a complete sentence. The rules of punctuation call for a
colon between the two sentences because "Bake a cake" expands the word
action. If a list of sentences followed, that would be an enumeration:
"Do the following: Bake a cake, Decorate it, Serve it to the guests."

The word invoke is not a sentence, it's a verb. If it were a sentence,
it would have punctuation after it, for example:

Invoke this: Bake a cake.
Hark! The herald angels sing.

>> >One other point, Mr Wagner. INVOKE in COBOL has
>> >a similarity to LET in BASIC. It is not necessary for one to
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>why 'invoke' is not necessary to the understanding.

A sentence cannot be the object of a verb, but a gerund (ending with
-ing) can be.

Read a record.
Carry out reading a record.
Perform reading a record.

>> >Consider that
>> >    [Invoke] EmployeeMaster:'Read' using EmployeeRecord
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Mr Wagner, better? Or just different?

Grammatically correct is better.

> INVOKE is required
>for syntax. It is not required for understanding. Treating
>INVOKE as more than it is invites confusion.

The word invoke is NOT required for syntax. The standard says we can
use Inline Invocation instead.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message

> On Sun, 12 Sep 2004 10:15:49 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>

message
> >> >> >news:2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com...
> >> >> >[snip]
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> carreer." By citing the gerundial form, you supported my argument.

You take that back! I am not supporting your argument
at all. <g>

What I am saying is that, as it relates to computer languages,
the gerundial form implies something different than the verb.
Perhaps it's just me; but, as I see it, the gerundial form, 'the
act of', is a process to be described to the computer. The
verb is a command to follow the process described.

> >Also, invoke has a sense of 'do the following'. Thus "Do the
> >following, 'Baking a cake'", makes no sense; but "Do the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> "Do the following: Bake a cake, Decorate it, Serve it to the guests."

Mr Wagner, the last is not a valid form for COBOL where
natural language rules do not apply.

invoke cake 'bake'
invoke cake 'decorate'
invoke cake 'serve'

[invoke: to cause, call forth, or bring about. -- RHCD]

to cause the baking of a cake
to cause the decorating of a cake
to cause the serving of a cake

Mr Wagner, the weakness in your argument, as I see it, is
your insistence at applying the rules of natual language to an
artificial language. COBOL is not a natural language. It is a
contrived imperative language.

[contrive: to bring about or effect by a device, strategem,
plan, or scheme. -- RHCD]

[imperative: noting or pertaining to the mood of the verb used
in commands, requests, etc. -- RHCD]

Verbs are chosen for method names because they fit the nature
of the language. With respect to COBOL, using gerunds is,
well, unnatural!

> The word invoke is not a sentence, it's a verb. If it were a sentence,
> it would have punctuation after it, for example:
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> Grammatically correct is better.

Mr Wagner, grammatically correct is irrelevant because
COBOL is not a natural language.

> > INVOKE is required
> >for syntax. It is not required for understanding. Treating
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> use Inline Invocation instead.

See FDIS, page 94, 8.4.2.4.3 General rules.
"1) An inline method invocation references a temporary data item
with the same class, category, and content as the temp-identifier
that would be returned from the execution of the applicable form
of INVOKE statement, as follows:

INVOKE identifier-1 literal-1 USING arguments RETURNING
temp-identifier
INVOKE identifier-1 literal-1 RETURNING temp-identifier
INVOKE class-name-1 literal-1 USING arguments
RETURNING temp-identifier
INVOKE class-name-1 literal-1 RETURNING temp-identifier

where:

a) arguments are the operands specified within parentheses in the
inline method invocation, if any;

b) temp-identifier has the same description, class, and category
as the RETURNING parameter in the specification of the method
identified by literal-1 and either identifier-1 or class-name-1;

c) temp-identifier is a temporary item that exists for the purpose
of effecting the inline invocation in this way and for no other
purpose."

In other words, 'Inline method invocation' can be used only when
the method returns a value that meets the requirements for a
'temp-identifier'. Otherwise, use an INVOKE statement, where
'INVOKE' is required for syntax.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

- **From:** Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T02:14:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41450215.2070205@optonline.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> "Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
> news:n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com...
…[207 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I will add to this discussion the following:

The COBOL spec, as far as possible, was contrived, if that is the word
to emply that except for IF, each statement was a command to the 
computer to do something. It's relationship to ENGLISH is only that
people can read it, and understand what it is saying to the computer.

That is, until new features somehow seem to contrive overwise.
Back then, program listings were in computer instrution form including
in some cases binary. When the techs tried to explain what was wrong
and how long it was going to fix the problem, they had to show the
"authorities" gibberish. 'What the hell does that mean?" So, COBOL,
by in large issues commands as in the army, etc.

Today, the only difference is that if something is needed, it can be
added to the logic of the computer. When I say "squat", I want to see
bare butts. In COBOL, that might be: SQUAT. DISPLAY BUTS. 	

The fact that the source can now be stated in lower case, etc. may
tend to suggest that the method is to write something for someone to
read, but it isn't.  It's something that a program has to read, and
compile to comply with a set of specifications on how it will be
done on a specific computer and match what is done on other computers
doing the same thing.

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ INVOKE (English vs COBOL (was: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T02:44:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hQ71d.677132$ic1.71235@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <41450215.2070205@optonline.net>`

```
Relatively early in the development of the 2002 Standard (I think when we were 
still hoping for a '97 Standard), I wrote a number of papers on WHY I thought 
that

  Invoke object-reference "method-name" Using Parameter

was "wrong" and not consistent with the rest of COBOL (or the definitions of 
what was going on).

It seemed to me then (and still does) that what this statement is ACTUALLY 
trying to convey was:

Send-a-message to the object referenced by Object-Reference to INVOKE the 
"method-Name" on itself - using parameters ...

  or my COBOL-like (sort-of)

Send-Message "method-name" TO object-reference Using Parameter-1 ...

These papers "got nowhere" and the INVOKE syntax became a "de facto" in COBOL 
(as extensions) for a number of implementations - long before the final drafts 
of the Standards went out for public review.

I still do NOT agree that one "invokes" the Object-Reference - but COBOL is not 
English and no matter how much it is influenced by English, the differences 
exist and always will exist.
```

is not

differences
> exist and always will exist.

FDIS, page 468, 14.8.22 INVOKE statement
"The INVOKE statement causes a method to be invoked."

Mr Klein, one problem may be that methods are qualified by
either a class name or an object reference; but the syntax
does not follow the normal rules for qualification. That is,

   invoke method-name of object-reference
or
   invoke literal of class-name

might seem more COBOL-like and therefore more agreeable.

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T14:40:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0fabk0t5dsh4le8msfudnui8lffg7jfipm@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <41450215.2070205@optonline.net> <hQ71d.677132$ic1.71235@news.easynews.com> <10ka73craibvu63@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Mon, 13 Sep 2004 00:03:47 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
>news:hQ71d.677132$ic1.71235@news.easynews.com...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>might seem more COBOL-like and therefore more agreeable.

Many people, especially those versed in other programming languages,
dislike Cobol's qualification syntax. They'd prefer to see qualifiers
before the target. I think they used OO as an opportunity to bend the
rules to their preference. 

Also, I think they view Invoke as just another Call. Since the word
CALL was already taken, they used a synonym. 

I don't see it that way; I see methods as verbs. We all know READ
doesn't produce inline code to read a file. It sets up parameters and
calls a file system. Similarly, LOG(n) doesn't generate inline code to
compute a logarithm; it calls an external program. 

The design of a language need not, indeed should not, follow
implementation details. If it did, most things we do would be written
as CALL. Cobol would be as awkward as C, which makes you call a
function to compare two strings.

I think Micro Focus got it right with their Vocabulary feature, which
turns method names into verbs that are referenced with normal Cobol
syntax. For example, FIND 'ABC' INSIDE collectionclass, where FIND is
a method of collectionclass.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INVOKE (English vs COBOL

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T17:50:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%5l1d.419674$gE.165944@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<hQ71d.677132$ic1.71235@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <41450215.2070205@optonline.net> <hQ71d.677132$ic1.71235@news.easynews.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

>Relatively early in the development of the 2002 Standard (I think when we were 
>still hoping for a '97 Standard), I wrote a number of papers on WHY I thought 
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Interesting. So now I go back to Raymond Obin's book "OO An Introduction 
for COBOL Programmers" - 1993 published on behalf of the OCTG. First 
mention of  INVOKE - Page 11 :-
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3.1.3. Using Methods.

Methods in an object can be called. Calling a method is like calling a 
program except that the method is a 'program within an object'. (<< that 
ties back with when I viewed a method as a 'mini-program' - still is but 
not quite >>). To call a program, we use the name of the program; to 
call a method, we use a combination of the object and the name of the 
method within the object.

In an application, methods can be called  from a number of different 
places (<<my Cart Wheel>>); the end-user can cause methods to be called 
through the user interface; other methods can call methods as they 
execute; and the system can call methods, for example, a "MakeBackup" 
method could be called every day at 5.00pm.

The he has a greyed, highlight box :-

Calling a Method

The procedure code in an object is used by Calling it. This is done just 
like calling a program except that it is a call to a Method in an Object.

The term Invoke a Method is also used.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Reasonably clear to me - and a couple of "co-conspirators" to get the 
book out, (amongst others), Schricker and Klein  :-)

CALL <aProgram> using aaaaaa returning bbbbbb

CALL <aProgram> <entry-point> using aaaaaaa returning bbbbbbb
INVOKE <aProgram = it's object version> <entry-point = methodname> using 
aaaaaaa returning bbbbbbb

Add entry-points and CALL and INVOKE look the same to me.

Anyway as you concluded - lump it or like it, (and I like it), we are 
stuck with the INVOKE syntax as is.

Jimmy
```

news:4145F413.4050303@optonline.net...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> > "Warren Simmons" <wsimmons5@optonline.net> wrote in message

Let's see, Mr Simmons.

[invent: 1. to originate as a product of one's own device
or contrivance. 2. to produce or create with the imagination.
-- RHCD]

The first definition includes the notion of 'contrived'. The
second seems too general; that is, it lacks the idea that
COBOL was the result of a plan to create a language
for a specific purpose (business oriented). Based upon
my knowledge of its history, I think C is closer to
invented; while C++ might be seen as contrived (object
oriented). Also, FORTRAN might be seen as contrived
(scientific oriented).

- **Date:** 2004-09-13T12:49:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Sun, 12 Sep 2004 17:24:35 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>Mr Wagner, the weakness in your argument, as I see it, is
>your insistence at applying the rules of natual language to an
>artificial language. COBOL is not a natural language. It is a
>contrived imperative language.

Special pleading is a common defense for illiteracy. People say the
stodgy old rules don't apply because they've been ... what, liberated
by ignorance. Terms like split infinitive and dangling participle
recall images of a  humorless schoolteacher and tests we barely passed
with a C. It's a new world now, a world where the rules are whatever
we make up.

To that I say, you can't send up what you don't have down. You can't
break the rules until you've first mastered them. Anything less is
lazy uncouthness.

>> The word invoke is NOT required for syntax. The standard says we can
>> use Inline Invocation instead.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>of INVOKE statement, as follows:

>In other words, 'Inline method invocation' can be used only when
>the method returns a value that meets the requirements for a
>'temp-identifier'. Otherwise, use an INVOKE statement, where
>'INVOKE' is required for syntax.

Returning an object of its own class is not remarkable. Would you
expect a pair of elephants to give issue to a goldfish?

Cobol cannot do type conversions between user-defined objects, it can
only diagnose mismatches. So unless you're returning a string or a
number, INVOKE has no advantage over Inline Invocation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T11:58:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

><http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=afd5j01oq7bnh3117sh7sur0d11k9ss21p%404ax.com&output=gplain>
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> better opinions.

No one said that at all. Not even close. Your response (above) and
reference to 'social class' at the was clearly due to your 'reading'
words that were never written.

Specifically what was said was: learning what to expect improves your
expectations.

> In this discussion, I didn't compare formal education
> of two individuals nor recommend someone "should take a course."  

Yes. Your expectation was not that which was defined for the action at
issue. The issue was not about opinion, it was about facts (though you
failed to understand that).

> I
> advocated following the rules of English, which are easily found on
> Web sites.

Many things are found on web sites, most of which should be
disbelieved.

Regardless of that there is no reason at all for Cobol to follow any
'rules of english'.  It is not english, it is a contrived language
with its own rules. It is not supposed to be judged alongside
literature, or even formal writing. Indeed it is to be used by speaker
of many different languages, most of which have quite different rules
from those that english have currently.

One could point out that many writings follow perfectly the 'rules of
english', such as legal documents, but this not make the meaning any
more fluent.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T21:05:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com>`

```
On 13 Sep 2004 11:58:06 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:


>... there is no reason at all for Cobol to follow any
>'rules of english'.  It is not english, it is a contrived language
>with its own rules. It is not supposed to be judged alongside
>literature, or even formal writing. 

It's no good for poetry. I've tried writing  blank verse in Cobol --
iambic tetrameter. Words like EQUAL and GREATER mess up the feet.

>Indeed it is to be used by speaker
>of many different languages, most of which have quite different rules
>from those that english have currently.

In the Good Old Days, manuals and the CODASYL standard (? not sure
about that) touted Cobol's English-like grammar as a benefit. Now,
references to English are gone.

>One could point out that many writings follow perfectly the 'rules of
>english', such as legal documents, but this not make the meaning any
>more fluent.

At least they don't use Hungarian type prefixes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>It's no good for poetry. I've tried writing  blank verse in Cobol --
>iambic tetrameter. Words like EQUAL and GREATER mess up the feet.
>  
>
Well that sounds to me like the ultimate exercise in futility so far as 
COBOL is concerned.  Isn't that somewhat like flagellation -  I 'm 
thinking of the ragheads in S.Iraq beating themselves with chains and 
the adherents of Opus Dei in Spain.

All is not lost however - seeing  as you are dabbling in OO. Set up a 
Poetry super class with sub-classes. And just think what you can *DO* 
with in-line invocation and the wretched Vocabulary class :-)

Nah. Come to think of it - much nicer, during the last days of summer if 
you take your nice lady-friend down by the river and have another picnic 
- what was it quiche and a bottle of plonk ?  That's more up my street, 
even though I enjoy some poetry.
Actually my 'beloved' and I are off to the mountains on Friday to do 
just that - sit alongside a large pond (Forget-Me-Not Pond in our 
Kananaskis Provincial Park), hear the rush of the exceedingly cold but 
crystal clear Elbow River some 200 yards away and look up at the 
glorious blue-grey backdrop of the mountains, and fingers crossed, a 
clear blue sky. Chances are there will only be the two of us at this 
local beauty spot.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T20:28:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> In the Good Old Days, manuals and the CODASYL standard (? not sure
> about that) touted Cobol's English-like grammar as a benefit. Now,
> references to English are gone.

You are wrong. I strongly doubt that anyone claimed that Cobol was
designed to use  'English grammar' as you appear to claim.  What they
have said is that, for example: "COBOL was designed ... using an
English-like command syntax."

In fact Cobol doesn't have a 'grammar' at all, let alone the English
one, it has a syntax (which is very much a subset of grammar), and not
that of English but a highly stylised and contrived one of its own.

That is: the Cobol syntax is like English.

Not: Cobol is like English grammar.
 

> At least they don't use Hungarian type prefixes.

You may wish to note that 'Hungarian notation' (prefixes) have nothing
to do with the Hungarian language.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-14T04:48:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oKu1d.1332535$6p.229903@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com...
> Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> English-like command syntax."
<snip>

From page XVII-1 of the '85 Standard (my "high-lighting" of text),

"1. THE DEVELOPMENT OF COBOL
 1.1 ORGANIZATION OF COBOL EFFORT

On May 28 and 29, 1959, a meeting was held for the purpose of considering both 
the desirability and the feasibility of establishing a common language for 
programming of computers in business data processing applications. This meeting 
was attended by representatives from users, both in private industry and in 
government, computer manufacturers, and other interested parties. It was agreed 
that the language must be open-ended and capable of accepting change and 
amendment, that it should be problem-oriented and machine-independent, and that 
it should use

  >>> simple English or pseudo-English <<

and avoid symbolism as far as possible. The Conference on DAta Systems Languages 
(CODASYL) developed out of this meeting."
```

> I agree; the fact that COBOL grammar is not English grammar does not, to me,
> require or imply that COBOL grammar does not exist.

It is apparent that some aspects of English grammar have been copied into CoBOL
grammar.   Other aspects, and even additional words have moved away from English
grammar and vocabulary.

I'm glad CoBOL doesn't accept double negatives as negative the way some spoken
languages do.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 35)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-14T19:43:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XRH1d.1363614$6p.234182@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <oKu1d.1332535$6p.229903@news.easynews.com> <217e491a.0409141110.db9baaa@posting.google.com> <ci7gi4$pkf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
The quote that I replied to was a reply to Robert's statement,

"In the Good Old Days, manuals and the CODASYL standard (? not sure
about that) touted Cobol's English-like grammar as a benefit. Now,
references to English are gone."

I think that statement from the history section of the '85 Standard implies (or 
maybe I just infer) that the original design *DID* think it was a "benefit" to 
be "English-like" and to that extent I agree with RW (whether you call that 
grammar or otherwise).

I think it is true that:

A) COBOL uses a grammar (for the procedure division) that is similar but NOT 
identical to a VERY small subset of English grammar.  IMHO, the "grammar" of the 
other 3 divisions has little to do with English

B) COBOL most definitely has a "grammar" by any commonly held definition of 
grammar that I have ever heard of. (I know that RW has referenced Chomsky 
before, so you might want to look at:
  http://encyclopedia.thefreedictionary.com/Chomsky%20grammar

C)  There was an original design criterion to make COBOL "English like" but that 
in each successive revision of the Standard this has become less and less 
important  (c.f. other thread on INVOKE for example)
```
