###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 45)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-17T09:01:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <dr4gk0t7cs6utfac6lc8nalv6v1bour21i@4ax.com> <10kgcc3bs5nuv24@corp.supernews.com> <k5sgk01dr0sklr5vdmvt5j8srh1pat7alf@4ax.com> <ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 16 Sep 2004 16:43:12 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >You can even write your own function to do this today, if it's
important
> >> >enough to you.
> >>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >User-written *intrinsic* functions, in standards-compliant COBOL, 30+
years
> >ago?   How so?
>
> Callable programs are functionally equivalent.

No, they are not.  They come close if you use all the features of
2002-standard COBOL save the definition of a function itself, but they're
still not quite there.

As I see it there are two criteria for a "function".  The first is that it
return a value.  The second is that the function-name may be used as an
operand with the intent that the value of the function is used where the
name appears.  .

"Callable programs" in standard COBOL were introduced in ANSI X3.23-1974,
which I grant was approved "30+ years ago" -- specifically, 30 years, four
months and seven days ago.   Neither '74 nor '85 COBOL has a clause that
allows a "callable program" to return a value.  One may modify a
*parameter*, but that is neither syntactically nor functionally equivalent.

The RETURNING clause of the PROCEDURE DIVISION header and the RETURNING
clause of the CALL statement were introduced in ISO/IEC 1989:2002.  This
meets *one* of the requirements for a user-written function being
"functionally equivalent" to a callable program.

The ability to use the name of a function to represent the value returned -- 
as an operand -- was also introduced in ISO/IEC 1989:2002.

Had "callable programs" been functionally equivalent to user-written
functions back in '74, they'd have had the ability to return values back
then and they would have been permitted as operands back then.  Guess what.
They didn't, they weren't, and they still aren't.  Pigs don't have wings.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 46)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-17T18:06:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <dr4gk0t7cs6utfac6lc8nalv6v1bour21i@4ax.com> <10kgcc3bs5nuv24@corp.supernews.com> <k5sgk01dr0sklr5vdmvt5j8srh1pat7alf@4ax.com> <ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 09:01:36 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com...
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>*parameter*, but that is neither syntactically nor functionally equivalent.

It is functionally equivalent. We've been doing it for 30 years.
There is no functional difference between these two:

move foo(a) to b

call 'foo' using a b

That syntax is so ingrained that since CALL RETURNING became available
*as an extension*, it's been widely ignored. I've tried using
RETURNING, especially for termination status code. Other programmers
say that's not 'the Cobol way' or 'not what I was taught in school',
and change it back to parameter(1). Then they move it to RETURN-CODE
(another extension) before STOP RUN.

What if a callable program or function needs to return TWO things? Do
you think programmers will put them into a structure and tediously
move the components out? I'll bet they pass two parameters. 

>The ability to use the name of a function to represent the value returned -- 
>as an operand -- was also introduced in ISO/IEC 1989:2002.

It was introduced in 1989 .. for standard-defined functions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 47)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-17T11:26:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <dr4gk0t7cs6utfac6lc8nalv6v1bour21i@4ax.com> <10kgcc3bs5nuv24@corp.supernews.com> <k5sgk01dr0sklr5vdmvt5j8srh1pat7alf@4ax.com> <ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com...
> It is functionally equivalent. We've been doing it for 30 years.
> There is no functional difference between these two:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> call 'foo' using a b

There is, however, a functional difference between
    compute c = foo(a) * foo(d)
and
    call 'foo" using a temp-1.  call 'foo' using d temp-2.  compute c =
temp-1 * temp-2.

>
> That syntax is so ingrained that since CALL RETURNING became available
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> (another extension) before STOP RUN.

So the only reason for *not* using a vendor extension is because it's not
*traditional*?  Most of the sites I know that avoid vendor extensions as a
matter of policy do so because they're not *portable*.

As our implementations of COBOL don't list RETURN-CODE among the reserved
words, I haven't a clue what special meaning it has.

> What if a callable program or function needs to return TWO things? Do
> you think programmers will put them into a structure and tediously
> move the components out? I'll bet they pass two parameters.

What if a mechanism defined to return a *single* thing has to return *two*
things?   Maybe it's your choice of tool that's incorrect here?  If what you
need is a hammer, it probably wouldn't be a good idea to grab a bassoon ...

> It was introduced in 1989 .. for standard-defined functions.

Oh.  Excuse me.  I should have repeated "user-defined".  I thought it was
reasonably clear that we were talking about whether or not *user-defined*
callable programs were equivalent to *user-defined* functions, since there
are no *standard-defined* callable programs that I know of.   Silly me.
    -Chuck Stevens.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 48)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-17T21:00:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<10kgcc3bs5nuv24@corp.supernews.com> <k5sgk01dr0sklr5vdmvt5j8srh1pat7alf@4ax.com> <ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 11:26:38 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>temp-1 * temp-2.

01 temp-1 pic x(37).
01 temp-2 pic x(37).

call "foo" using a temp-1
call "foo" using d temp-2
compute c = numval-f(temp-1) * numval-f(temp-2)

>So the only reason for *not* using a vendor extension is because it's not
>*traditional*?  Most of the sites I know that avoid vendor extensions as a
>matter of policy do so because they're not *portable*.

In reality, Cobol programs are generally more portable than people
think. I've ported many systems. In the old days it was from one
mainframe to another; more recently, from IBM mainframe (& AS/400) to
Unix and Windows. More than half the time, no change at all is
required. 

A common 'incompability' is the default file organization. On IBM
mainframes it is 'plain vanilla' sequential; on other platforms we
want it to be line sequential. Under Micro Focus, one need not change
SELECT statements. A compiler option changes it across the board. 

Typically, more changes are required to embedded SQL than to Cobol
code. IBM lets them get away with stuff that should never have been
allowed .. lazy programmer stuff like INSERT INTO "table" VALUES
(:record-name) rather than INSERT INTO "table" (col_name, col_name) ..
They're saying INSERT but they're thinking WRITE (indexed record).
Blah.

>As our implementations of COBOL don't list RETURN-CODE among the reserved
>words, I haven't a clue what special meaning it has.

By what *standard* mechanism do your programs return an exit status to
the operating system?

>> What if a callable program or function needs to return TWO things? Do
>> you think programmers will put them into a structure and tediously
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>need is a hammer, it probably wouldn't be a good idea to grab a bassoon ...

And if one needs a file copy utility written, it wouldn't be a good
idea to let an applications programmer write it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 49)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-17T14:40:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cifll1$9fm$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<10kgcc3bs5nuv24@corp.supernews.com> <k5sgk01dr0sklr5vdmvt5j8srh1pat7alf@4ax.com> <ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 11:26:38 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> compute c = numval-f(temp-1) * numval-f(temp-2)

What's your point, and how does it relate to the functional equivalence of
user-written *called programs* programs and user-written *functions* used as
operands?

> >So the only reason for *not* using a vendor extension is because it's not
> >*traditional*?  Most of the sites I know that avoid vendor extensions as
a
> >matter of policy do so because they're not *portable*.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> required.

Like the COBOL-60 code our B6500 compiler accepted, namely, "COMPUTE R = N
MOD D"?
Or "FIND MASTERRECORD VIA NAMES AT FIRSTNAME = "JOHN" AND LASTNAME =
"SMITH"?

OK, if you say so ...

> A common 'incompability' is the default file organization. On IBM
> mainframes it is 'plain vanilla' sequential; on other platforms we
> want it to be line sequential. Under Micro Focus, one need not change
> SELECT statements. A compiler option changes it across the board.

It does, huh?    Sure about that?

> Typically, more changes are required to embedded SQL than to Cobol
> code. IBM lets them get away with stuff that should never have been
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Blah.

And how do you include SQL code *in standard COBOL*?

> >As our implementations of COBOL don't list RETURN-CODE among the reserved
> >words, I haven't a clue what special meaning it has.
>
> By what *standard* mechanism do your programs return an exit status to
> the operating system?

What mechanism is it *required* by the standard for a program to return exit
status to the operating system?  In our environment execution is either
successful or it is not, and it's the operating system that makes that
determination.

If the program terminates normally by executing a STOP RUN, it terminated
normally.  If it encountered some sort of arithmetic fault that wasn't
handled by an ON SIZE ERROR, or the compiler generated garbled object code,
or the progream had a fatal I/O error with neither a declarative nor a FILE
STATUS applicable, or the program deliberately committed suicide through
some other means, guess what:  the program didn't terminate normally.

In our environment an "abend" is called a "DS", as I recall standing for
"Discontinue Service".  The operating system is capable of distinguishing
among faults (F[ault]-DS) which includes arithmetics as well as garbled
code; I/O errors (I[/O]-DS); suicides (P[rorammatic]-DS); failure to provide
resources (R[esource]-DS); and even "discontinued after scheduling but prior
to execution" (Q[ueue]-DS).   If the program ran OK, it ran OK and the
operating system treats it as if it ran OK.  What sort of
*standards-mandated* task status would you have it report?

> >What if a mechanism defined to return a *single* thing has to return
*two*
> >things?   Maybe it's your choice of tool that's incorrect here?  If what
you
> >need is a hammer, it probably wouldn't be a good idea to grab a bassoon
...
>
> And if one needs a file copy utility written, it wouldn't be a good
> idea to let an applications programmer write it.

This has *what* to do with your point about functions being equivalent to
called programs?

A *function*, which returns a single thing, is probably the wrong tool to
use if you need to produce *two* things.   A *called program* is not
functionally equivalent to a *function* unless the called program produces a
single value and is usable as a sending operand in a program.  If a
mechanism doesn't lend itself to implementation as a user-written function,
then it's probably not a good idea to attempt implementing the mechanism as
a user-written function.  That does not take away from the fact that
user-written functions can be used in semantic contexts in which called
programs cannot.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 14:40:49 -0700, Chuck Stevens wrote:

>
>And how do you include SQL code *in standard COBOL*?

   As an extension .... 

   For CODASYL-Databases, CODASYL-COBOL had a "standard" way to do it. 

   UCS COBOL for Unisys OS/1100 (Clearpath IX) does it with "Embedded SQL" or
ESQL for RDMS at compile time this way: 

   EXEC SQL. 
       <some SQL statement>
  END-EXEC. 


Yours, 

L. Willms
------------------------------ all rights reserved ------------------------
```

```
>> In reality, Cobol programs are generally more portable than people

I said "more portable than people think." Sure, there is odd syntax

Discontinue Service is not in the standard. Suppose a program does an
I/O that returns FILE STATUS 9$. Not knowing what that means, the
program displays "Something is wrong" and terminates. How does it
terminate?
```

- **References:** `<ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <cifll1$9fm$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <6q1ok0h85td2qib4n9jf745hciek7v4fn1@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:6q1ok0h85td2qib4n9jf745hciek7v4fn1@4ax.com...

> Discontinue Service is not in the standard. Suppose a program does an
> I/O that returns FILE STATUS 9$. Not knowing what that means, the
> program displays "Something is wrong" and terminates. How does it
> terminate?

I've seen many a program that forces an arithmetic fault (for example does a
division by zero with neither a SIZE ERROR nor an associated declarative)
after displaying a message.  That's not the way I'd choose, but it is a
standard way of doing it.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 52)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T17:01:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cin2d2$b99$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <cifll1$9fm$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <6q1ok0h85td2qib4n9jf745hciek7v4fn1@4ax.com> <cimtpm$25ki$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

On 20-Sep-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> I've seen many a program that forces an arithmetic fault (for example does a
> division by zero with neither a SIZE ERROR nor an associated declarative)
> after displaying a message.  That's not the way I'd choose, but it is a
> standard way of doing it.

One difficulty with the design of CoBOL is that we use up a lot of CPU cycles
checking for things that would hardly ever happen.   This used to be more
important than it is now.    Our design says that X will never be zero, but we
check before dividing by zero.   We check bounds.   We do a lot of checking so
that we don't abort.

Error tossing seems to be an afterthought to the design of the language, it is
clumsy and non-intuitive.    Current program language philosophy tends to be
that we design lots of error-catching layers that allow us to have exceptions
and handle them in a much more integrated manner.   But they can go further as
well.   And in the future, programs will need to be able to handle mistakes
better - with the environment being the world, programming will be like walking
- we hit a bump or a pot-hole and hardly notice as we handle the unexpected.  
Without having to check for every possible exception ahead of time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 51)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T11:16:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cin6qf$2bph$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <cifll1$9fm$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <6q1ok0h85td2qib4n9jf745hciek7v4fn1@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:6q1ok0h85td2qib4n9jf745hciek7v4fn1@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 14:40:49 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >    call 'foo" using a temp-1.  call 'foo' using d temp-2.  compute c
=
> >> >temp-1 * temp-2.
> >>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >What's your point, and how does it relate to the functional equivalence
of
> >user-written *called programs* programs and user-written *functions* used
as
> >operands?
>
> I thought the functional difference was precision of temporary
> intermediate data items. If that's not it, what is the difference?

That's *one* functional difference, but there are others -- the fact that
the results have to be stored and retrieved (unless the compiler does a
logic flow analysis and determines that temp-1 and temp-2 are used *only* in
this location, which an implementor is free to do) being but one.

> I said "more portable than people think." Sure, there is odd syntax
> that hypothetically *might* be in old programs. One can dream all
> sorts of nightmares .. or foster them if he's billing by the hour. In
> the real world of programs I've worked on, those fears were unfounded.

The MOD operator example was available in COBOL-60 and COBOL-68 for over
thirty years, and very widely used.   Function REM was added to COBOL85 (and
COBOL74) precisely because what the RDIV operator did wasn't exactly the
same as what the standard required.

The FIND syntax example I used is central to a number of LARGE international
banking networks running today.

> >And how do you include SQL code *in standard COBOL*?
>
> You use a  pre-compiler.

Should I have written "within" instead of "in"?    And what *standard* COBOL
does the pre-compiler produce?


    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
If MOD was so widely used, why did they delete it?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 53)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-21T08:45:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cipiac$tku$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <cifll1$9fm$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <6q1ok0h85td2qib4n9jf745hciek7v4fn1@4ax.com> <cin6qf$2bph$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <irhuk0lg8felfd76gojhbt4dvic86d919n@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:irhuk0lg8felfd76gojhbt4dvic86d919n@4ax.com...
> On Mon, 20 Sep 2004 11:16:46 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >thirty years, and very widely used.   Function REM was added to COBOL85
(and
> >COBOL74) precisely because what the RDIV operator did wasn't exactly the
> >same as what the standard required.
>
> If MOD was so widely used, why did they delete it?

The pre-1974 COBOL compilers for the three major Burroughs architectures
were not compatible with one another.  When the 1974 standard came out, a
committee was formed to develop a *corporate* standard to ensure
inter-machine portability of source code.

The MOD *arithmetic* operator, which directly caused the emission of the
RDIV *machine* operator, was *not* included in Burroughs Standard COBOL74
because the other compilers didn't have it and it was regarded as too
machine-specific to include.

When we began the process of discontinuing support for the old COBOL(68)
compiler back in the late 1980's, the user community for that product had no
direct migration path for the MOD operator, so we introduced Function REM
(to generate the RDIV operator) as well as Function MOD (to generate the
almost-but-not-quite-the-same results that would be required by the
not-yet-adopted Intrinsic Function amendment to the '85 standard).  We also
added Function DIV at the same time (for the arithmetic operator DIV,
corresponding to the machine opeator IDIV, and producing the results
mandated by that same amendment).

We didn't exactly delete the MOD arithmetic operator we dropped support
(officially as of 12/31/1999, but with several individual customers
initiating CER's extending support; I think the last of these expired last
year) for the compiler that allowed it.

> Was there once an RDIV in Cobol? I don't remember that.

I don't know of one.  There has always been a RDIV operator in the Unisys
MCP architecture.  What in some environments is called a "machine
instruction" is referred to by folks who deal in Unisys MCP architecture as
an "operator".

> If the program were being ported today, file IO would be translated to
> SQL anyway. FIND is easier to translate than READ.

Who said anything about file IO?  This is a data base access, not an I/O
statement (though the syntax does bear *some* similarities to READ).

Why would a customer who is happy with the performance, reliability and
recoverability of his DMSII data base and the application programs that
access it translate the access methodology to SQL?

> >> >And how do you include SQL code *in standard COBOL*?
> >>
> >> You use a  pre-compiler.
> >
> >Should I have written "within" instead of "in"?    And what *standard*
COBOL
> >does the pre-compiler produce?
>
> Many CALL statements and a few MOVEs.

OK, that's fair.  Is the library containing the programs that are the
targets of the CALLs portable and/or ported to all architectures (in other
words, are the libraries and their interfaces standardized) so that the
preprocessor produces the same COBOL code for all architectures?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

```
On Tue, 21 Sep 2004 08:45:16 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:irhuk0lg8felfd76gojhbt4dvic86d919n@4ax.com...

>> If MOD was so widely used, why did they delete it?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>inter-machine portability of source code.
[remainder deleted for brevity]

Thank you for the history.

>> If the program were being ported today, file IO would be translated to
>> SQL anyway. FIND is easier to translate than READ.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>access it translate the access methodology to SQL?

Oh, I thought it was reading an indexed file. The premise was porting
code to a different platform. If the FIND syntax is no longer
supported, there must an equivalent in newer DMS.

>> >> >And how do you include SQL code *in standard COBOL*?
>> >>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>preprocessor produces the same COBOL code for all architectures?

In Oracle, the answer is yes. It has to be because the pre-compiler
doesn't know which compiler will be used. The disadvantage is issuing
a CALL when it needs an address rather than using ADDRESS OF, which
would produce inline code. Getting addresses is by far the most common
activity in generated code.
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:7hk0l0tk305v34v6jkdu4glt93mmg1omus@4ax.com...

> Oh, I thought it was reading an indexed file. The premise was porting
> code to a different platform.

Exactly.

> If the FIND syntax is no longer
> supported,

Who said anything about FIND *no longer* being supported?

> there must an equivalent in newer DMS.

There may or may not be.  Unisys DMSII continues to evolve in power,
flexibility, accessibility.  The problem is finding an equivalent
*environment* that provides similar robustness, power, maintainability and
recoverability to that afforded by Unisys DMSII, both currently and
historically.   What syntax you decide to use in your COBOL programs, it
seems to me, is secondary to the quality and reputation of the data base
engine, and Unisys DMSII is very high quality indeed.   Other data bases
have come a long, long way over the past three decades, I grant.  But I
don't think they're there yet.

> In Oracle, the answer is yes. It has to be because the pre-compiler
> doesn't know which compiler will be used. The disadvantage is issuing
> a CALL when it needs an address rather than using ADDRESS OF, which
> would produce inline code. Getting addresses is by far the most common
> activity in generated code.

That's one.  I thought we were talking about *arbitrary* SQL service
environments.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
On Tue, 21 Sep 2004 21:52:11 GMT, Robert Wagner wrote:

>
>It's not clear what you mean by arbitrary. If the SQL is talking
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>because there's no standard for runtimes.

   When the compiler provides an ODBC client driver, i.e. a facility to send
SQL commands to an ODBC server, the compiler could do it by itself without an
preprocessor, isn't it? I would prefer that. 

   I had worked with ESQL in COBOL using the UCOB, i.e. COBOL-85, compiler
for Unisys OS/1100 (Clearpath IX), and that did not require a preprocessor.
The compiler called the RDMS runtime system to check the validity of the
commands and table references. 

   Since an ODBC client is initially detached from the database server, the
compiler could work the same way, don't you think so? 


Yours, 
L. Willms
```

_(thread depth: 58)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-22T13:36:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d2v2l09nknmk3q9itef08ht88s1kvpm6fb@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <cifll1$9fm$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <6q1ok0h85td2qib4n9jf745hciek7v4fn1@4ax.com> <cin6qf$2bph$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <irhuk0lg8felfd76gojhbt4dvic86d919n@4ax.com> <cipiac$tku$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <7hk0l0tk305v34v6jkdu4glt93mmg1omus@4ax.com> <cipum2$163d$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <bs31l0trqdtdk7ipvih5cd8bt9dk58md8u@4ax.com> <yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i4g5ur3.pminews@news.t-online.de>`

```
On Wed, 22 Sep 2004 10:35:15 +0200 (MES), "L?ko Willms"
<lw-nospam@t-online.de> wrote:

>On Tue, 21 Sep 2004 21:52:11 GMT, Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>compiler could work the same way, don't you think so? 

The Oracle preprocessor has an option (SEMANTICS) to do server
verification at (pre)compile time. It appears to be using the PREPARE
command. 

Sure, the compiler COULD integrate SQL, but many don't because:

1. It's not required by the Cobol standard.
2. They don't want to deal with database idiosyncracies. Many people
refuse to believe that SQL is Standardized. They think there are
syntactical differences between brands. The bad guy here is Oracle,
which held onto SQL-92 (with extensions) long after the others had
adopted SQL-99. 

IBM and Unisys have an incentive to integrate SQL -- they want to lock
you into their database.
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message 
news:v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 11:26:38 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
<snip>
>
>>As our implementations of COBOL don't list RETURN-CODE among the reserved
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Robert,
  I don't personally know of any COBOL compiler that supports Return-Code 
*except* for those that are now (or were once) targetted at IBM mainframe 
environments (porting or development).

The 2002 Standard has enhanced the Stop <RUN> statemen to allow one to convey 
information back to the "operating system" - this includes whether the 
termination was or was not successful - and some information value.

See
  Stop with Normal/Abnormal Status
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 22:03:37 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message 
>news:v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com...
>> On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 11:26:38 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
>> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>>>As our implementations of COBOL don't list RETURN-CODE among the reserved
>>>words, I haven't a clue what special meaning it has.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>termination was or was not successful - and some information value.

For 40+ years before the 2002 standard, we didn't sit on our hands
waiting for permission. We used extensions to the standard. If the
standard had specified how to do it, we would have done it that way.

Returning an exit status is so basic, I can't believe it wasn't
addressed before 2002.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 51)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-18T11:41:49-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 22:03:37 GMT, "William M. Klein"
> <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
[snip]
> >The 2002 Standard has enhanced the Stop <RUN> statemen to allow one to
convey
> >information back to the "operating system" - this includes whether the
> >termination was or was not successful - and some information value.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> standard had specified how to do it, we would have done it that way.

The COBOL standard is a specification for a computer
programming language. Conformance to the language
specification is a requirement for implementors. The
standard cannot impose requirements on the operating
system.

The 2002 enhancements to the STOP statement have effect
only "if such a capability exists within the operating system."

> Returning an exit status is so basic, I can't believe it wasn't
> addressed before 2002.

It was addressed before 2002, by applications programmers
using whatever means was at their disposal.

Consider the effect of 'returning an exit status' to MS-DOS
or Windows. It's meaningless!
```

- **References:** `<cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Sat, 18 Sep 2004 11:41:49 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com...

>> Returning an exit status is so basic, I can't believe it wasn't
>> addressed before 2002.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>or Windows. It's meaningless!

13.3.7.1 Terminate Program Execution

---------- MS-DOS  ---------------------------------------------------

Function (ah):          4Ch
Entry parameters:       al- return code
Exit parameters:        Does not return to your program

This is the function call normally used to terminate your program. It
returns control to the calling process (normally, but not necessarily,
DOS). A return code can be passed to the calling process in the al
register. Exactly what meaning this return code has is entirely up to
you. This return code can be tested with the DOS "IF ERRORLEVEL return
code" command in a DOS batch file. All files opened by the current
process will be automatically closed upon program termination.


--------- MS-DOS  ---------------------------------------------------

Return a Dos error code on exit

At times you may want to return a Dos ErrorLevel when closing your VB
application. This can be necessary, for example, if the EXE is meant
to be called from a batch file. Exiting the program with an error code
is really simple, and requires only a call to the ExitProcess API
function:

Private Declare Sub ExitProcess Lib "kernel32" (ByVal uExitCode As
Long)

' Exit with ErrorLevel set to 9
ExitProcess 9

The following Ms-Dos batch error runs the Text.Exe application, and
then tests whether its error level is 2, 1, or zero. Note that exit
codes must be tested in reverse order, from the highest value downward

REM a Ms-Dos batch file
CD c:\MyApp
Text.Exe
If Errorlevel 2 Goto ExitCodeIsTwo
If Errorlevel 1 Goto ExitCodeIsOne

REM process here a null exit code
REM ...
GOTO EndBatch

:ExitCodeTwo
REM process here exit code equals to two
REM ...
GOTO EndBatch

:ExitCodeOne
REM process here exit code equals to one
REM ...

:EndBatch
ECHO goodbye.


--------- Windows ---------------------------------------------------

ExitProcess

The ExitProcess function ends a process and all its threads.


VOID ExitProcess(
  UINT uExitCode
);

Parameters
uExitCode 
[in] Exit code for the process and all threads terminated as a result
of this call. Use the GetExitCodeProcess function to retrieve the
process's exit value. Use the GetExitCodeThread function to retrieve a
thread's exit value. 

------- Scripting Languages ------------------------------------------
Windows Script Host   

ExitCode Property
Returns the exit code set by a script or program run using the Exec()
method.

Executables set an exit code when they finish running. This conveys
the status information when a process ends. Often, it is used to send
an error code (or some other piece of information) back to the caller.
If the process has not finished, the ExitCode property returns 0. The
values returned from ExitCode depend on the application that was
called
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:vhsok0htrik0vr0dsr69u09jqu6o2fol9s@4ax.com...

>

> Exactly what meaning this return code has is entirely up to
> you.
[snip]

Exactly! Unless the applications programmer (or developer)
provides, outside COBOL, something to give meaning to
'this return code', it's meaningless.

Programmers, using standard COBOL, could return exit
status to the user in the form of a message, which was a
means at their disposal prior to 2002.
```

```
On Sat, 18 Sep 2004 15:03:06 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>'this return code', it's meaningless.

The return code situation is the same on zOS and Unix. Apparently you
consider their return codes meaningless too.

>Programmers, using standard COBOL, could return exit
>status to the user in the form of a message, which was a
>means at their disposal prior to 2002.

Batch jobs aren't run by users; they are run by automated schedulers
or scripts. Decisions about what to do next are based on return code.
Success/failure log entries are based on return code.

As for interactive users, programmers could not send a message to
Windows users via standard Cobol. Before OO features were added in
2002, the standard doesn't support communication with the Windows GUI.
```

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>On Sat, 18 Sep 2004 15:03:06 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
>wrote:
…[62 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Still doesn't Robert. The Standard  doesn't acknowledge GUIs - they are 
'extensions' just like leaving out 'Procedure Division' from an OO 
Method is an 'extension' - although I'd rather call that a 'deletion' 
:-) . Way back when, M/F had 'extensions' - then along comes COBOL 2002  
- and yet some of those M/F features are still now 'extensions' - 
perhaps an "Extension on an extension'. At this point in time any use of 
Collections is an 'extension' - they don't yet exist so far as J4 is 
concerned !

I blindly tried to follow the upcoming COBOL 2???? Standard before it 
got its imprimatur, so ignored the following :-

Class-id.   MySomething inherits from Base
                 Data is private.

Class-id.   MySomething2 inherits from MySomething
                 with Data.

1 - Must compile MySomething first which generates an 'inclusive' 
copyfile containing W/S info.
2 - Now compile MySomething2 which picks up on that copyfile.

No need in MySomething2 to write :-

    invoke super "blahblah..."

In MySomething2,  I can make DIRECT reference to a collection sat as 
os-List in the W/S of MySomethng.. Kinda neat - I will be using as 
appropriate. Portability ? WHEN ?

(Just think of those Micro Focus pic x(4) comp-5's popping up all over 
the place - being returned from access to OO classes. *Don't you dare* 
suggest I change my code to say pic s9(09) comp-5. Did it once and the 
whole thing blew up. Thank God I had the global search from the IDE - I 
could pick them out and went back and changed them back to pic x(4) comp5.)
```

- **References:** `<cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <vhsok0htrik0vr0dsr69u09jqu6o2fol9s@4ax.com> <10kp1lj9896i646@corp.supernews.com> <d72pk05fqms0eu8gg3hi3mngt6qo87p80f@4ax.com> <RH03d.463375$gE.285383@pd7tw3no>`

```
On Sat, 18 Sep 2004 20:15:45 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>>As for interactive users, programmers could not send a message to
>>Windows users via standard Cobol. Before OO features were added in
>>2002, the standard doesn't support communication with the Windows GUI.
>>
>Still doesn't Robert. The Standard  doesn't acknowledge GUIs 

It doesn't need to acknowledge GUIs, nor communications, security,
encryption, spell checking, etc. All we need are object handling and
Invoke. 

> they are 
>'extensions' just like leaving out 'Procedure Division' from an OO 
>Method is an 'extension' - although I'd rather call that a 'deletion' 

They shouldn't be extensions to Cobol; they should be system services
available to all. If every compiler had to separately supply them, it
would be a waste of time, barrier to entry and source of non-standard
behavior.

> Way back when, M/F had 'extensions' - then along comes COBOL 2002  
>- and yet some of those M/F features are still now 'extensions' - 
>perhaps an "Extension on an extension'. At this point in time any use of 
>Collections is an 'extension' - they don't yet exist so far as J4 is 
>concerned !

User-written classes aren't called 'extensions'. If you don't like
Micro Focus' Collection class (I don't), write your own (good idea).

If you don't like the way a Cobol verb works, write a method that
works the way you prefer. That's not an 'extension'.

>I blindly tried to follow the upcoming COBOL 2???? Standard before it 
>got its imprimatur, so ignored the following :-
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>appropriate. Portability ? WHEN ?

What's so remarkable about inheriting factory data?

>(Just think of those Micro Focus pic x(4) comp-5's popping up all over 
>the place - being returned from access to OO classes. *Don't you dare* 
>suggest I change my code to say pic s9(09) comp-5. Did it once and the 
>whole thing blew up. Thank God I had the global search from the IDE - I 
>could pick them out and went back and changed them back to pic x(4) comp5.)

Comp-x seems more appropriate to pic x, stylistically. The only
difference between comp-x and comp-5 is that comp-x is stored
small-endian. One clock for an xchg is no big deal.
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:r77pk01pfm5kvcdd7j42d25d01cjad2hbl@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 18 Sep 2004 20:15:45 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
> wrote:
[snip]
> >(Just think of those Micro Focus pic x(4) comp-5's popping up all over
> >the place - being returned from access to OO classes. *Don't you dare*
> >suggest I change my code to say pic s9(09) comp-5. Did it once and the
> >whole thing blew up. Thank God I had the global search from the IDE - I
> >could pick them out and went back and changed them back to pic x(4)
comp5.)
>
> Comp-x seems more appropriate to pic x, stylistically. The only
> difference between comp-x and comp-5 is that comp-x is stored
> small-endian. One clock for an xchg is no big deal.

Micro Focus COBOL 3.2 Language Reference, page 2-45.
"A COMPUTATIONAL-X item is always stored using the
same storage scheme as BINARY items, that is with the
high-order bytes stored at the lowest addresses and
successively lower-order bytes at successively higher
addresses."

Mr Wagner, the description given by Micro Focus for its
comp-x is synonoymous with 'big-endian'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Procedural v OO Collections

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-09-19T19:41:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-19T16:17:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409191517.1122a172@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <vhsok0htrik0vr0dsr69u09jqu6o2fol9s@4ax.com> <10kp1lj9896i646@corp.supernews.com> <d72pk05fqms0eu8gg3hi3mngt6qo87p80f@4ax.com> <RH03d.463375$gE.285383@pd7tw3no> <r77pk01pfm5kvcdd7j42d25d01cjad2hbl@4ax.com> <Q873d.466123$gE.71712@pd7tw3no> <1t9rk0djtr8s683k27b9cka3ab1cjau97c@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> Morons post _opinions_. 

It is interesting then that you had earlier posted that people here
didn't like the _opinions_ that you posted.

> In the case of Micro Focus' Collection
> classes, I posted verifiable _facts_ about their slow speed and also
> verifiable code that runs 50 times faster. 

However, your code was not even close to be a collection class, or
even anything to do with classes at all.  Generality adds overheads. 
The issue with collection classes isn't about machine performance but
is about reusability and cost of programmer development time.

3 or 4 decades ago a small computer cost several times one
programmer's annual salary - the first one that I used cost 50 times
what they paid me for a year. Today the slowest desktop PC costs less
than what I get paid for a day and yet it outperforms my first several
thousand times.

You still want to hand code for speed just as you did decades ago
using pointers that were 'what C did in the 70s'.  Realistically,
except for some small areas of code, the cost of this is too high
compared to just using tools that do the job without caring about
machine overhead.

> One fact trumps a fist-full of opinions .. at least in the world of
> reason.

Yes, but your code wasn't a collection class that can just be used to
manage the objects, it has to be coded for each requirement.

It is like your 'tree' where you haven't yet written the rotation.  I
can do the task (if I needed to) with a SORT and a SERACH ALL, 20
years ago I may have thought it worth while (as I in fact did) to code
up trees in C.  Now with disks access hundreds of times faster (due to
caching) I probably would just use an isam file or an SQL table.

Of course the writers of MySQL or PostgreSQL will be needing to use
the most efficient code, but for most applications to 'best' solution
is the one that uses the least programmer time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

```
On 19 Sep 2004 16:17:36 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

>> In the case of Micro Focus' Collection
>> classes, I posted verifiable _facts_ about their slow speed and also
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>even anything to do with classes at all. 

It could easily be turned into a collection class. 

> Generality adds overheads. 
>The issue with collection classes isn't about machine performance but
>is about reusability and cost of programmer development time.

True, but sounds apolegitic.

>3 or 4 decades ago a small computer cost several times one
>programmer's annual salary - the first one that I used cost 50 times
>what they paid me for a year. Today the slowest desktop PC costs less
>than what I get paid for a day and yet it outperforms my first several
>thousand times.

Yep, you got that right.

>You still want to hand code for speed just as you did decades ago
>using pointers that were 'what C did in the 70s'.  Realistically,
>except for some small areas of code, the cost of this is too high
>compared to just using tools that do the job without caring about
>machine overhead.

I'm talking about small areas of code. Specifically, the ones heavily
used in an OO application such as Collection class.

>It is like your 'tree' where you haven't yet written the rotation.  I
>can do the task (if I needed to) with a SORT and a SERACH ALL, 20
>years ago I may have thought it worth while (as I in fact did) to code
>up trees in C.  Now with disks access hundreds of times faster (due to
>caching) I probably would just use an isam file or an SQL table.

That may be the practical solution, but I dislike using file systems
to organize transient data. I feel the programmer should know enough
to manage the structure on his own.

>Of course the writers of MySQL or PostgreSQL will be needing to use
>the most efficient code, but for most applications to 'best' solution
>is the one that uses the least programmer time.

You think like an applications programmer; I'm trying to think like a
tool builder.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 61)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T11:33:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409201033.3bad2702@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <vhsok0htrik0vr0dsr69u09jqu6o2fol9s@4ax.com> <10kp1lj9896i646@corp.supernews.com> <d72pk05fqms0eu8gg3hi3mngt6qo87p80f@4ax.com> <RH03d.463375$gE.285383@pd7tw3no> <r77pk01pfm5kvcdd7j42d25d01cjad2hbl@4ax.com> <Q873d.466123$gE.71712@pd7tw3no> <1t9rk0djtr8s683k27b9cka3ab1cjau97c@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409191517.1122a172@posting.google.com> <i8rsk0tmk9oqol6rnqai0qu7m1no7ae03e@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> It could easily be turned into a collection class. 

How 'easily' please show.
 
> I'm talking about small areas of code. Specifically, the ones heavily
> used in an OO application such as Collection class.

Well then, show how that is done with a collection class.
 
> That may be the practical solution, but I dislike using file systems
> to organize transient data. I feel the programmer should know enough
> to manage the structure on his own.

And, I would feel, especially if I was paying, that a programmer
should use whatever tool has been created to do the task in the most
reliable manner using the least amount of code/time.  Any programmer
that want to piss around with unstable tree rotations and manually
fixing up pointer problems should be working somne where else,
probably in a language that actually supports development using those
mechanisms.

> You think like an applications programmer; I'm trying to think like a
> tool builder.

Cobol is a tool for application programmers.  If you want to parade as
a tool builder then stop copying what was already done 30 years ago
and can be read in any C programming book found in the 2nd hand book
shop and show how modern programming can be done.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 62)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T22:48:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8viuk0ps1uogme0k00r5e8h921jp4e35gh@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <vhsok0htrik0vr0dsr69u09jqu6o2fol9s@4ax.com> <10kp1lj9896i646@corp.supernews.com> <d72pk05fqms0eu8gg3hi3mngt6qo87p80f@4ax.com> <RH03d.463375$gE.285383@pd7tw3no> <r77pk01pfm5kvcdd7j42d25d01cjad2hbl@4ax.com> <Q873d.466123$gE.71712@pd7tw3no> <1t9rk0djtr8s683k27b9cka3ab1cjau97c@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409191517.1122a172@posting.google.com> <i8rsk0tmk9oqol6rnqai0qu7m1no7ae03e@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409201033.3bad2702@posting.google.com>`

```
On 20 Sep 2004 11:33:04 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Well then, show how that is done with a collection class.

It's not worth the effort. CLC posters are more interested in banter
than discussing code. I will when I need it for a project.
 
>> That may be the practical solution, but I dislike using file systems
>> to organize transient data. I feel the programmer should know enough
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>mechanisms.

If Cobol didn't support pointers, the programs wouldn't have compiled
and ran.  There is no "manually fixing up" in the programs and my tree
rotations (actually Rolfe's) are not "unstable". 

>> You think like an applications programmer; I'm trying to think like a
>> tool builder.
>
>Cobol is a tool for application programmers.  

The standard doesn't say that.

>If you want to parade as
>a tool builder then stop copying what was already done 30 years ago
>and can be read in any C programming book found in the 2nd hand book
>shop and show how modern programming can be done.

The notion that all this was known in the '70s is a common misbelief.
In the comments of trees.cbl, I said:

" Although rotations were known in the '70s, using them to
 transform a tree to a list was first described by Stout & Warren in
 1986."

and 

" The [balancing] algorithm used was first described (almost) by A.
Colin Day in 1976, then refined by Rolfe in 2002."

As I've said before, I believe in alternating between top-down and
bottom-up, converging on a solution in the middle. The programs I
posted laid a bottom-up foundation, which can now be packaged in OO.
If I'd tried using OO from the beginning, I'd have methods invoking
methods invoking other methods. All that invoking would make it 50
times slower.
```

- **References:** `<10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <vhsok0htrik0vr0dsr69u09jqu6o2fol9s@4ax.com> <10kp1lj9896i646@corp.supernews.com> <d72pk05fqms0eu8gg3hi3mngt6qo87p80f@4ax.com> <RH03d.463375$gE.285383@pd7tw3no> <r77pk01pfm5kvcdd7j42d25d01cjad2hbl@4ax.com> <Q873d.466123$gE.71712@pd7tw3no> <1t9rk0djtr8s683k27b9cka3ab1cjau97c@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409191517.1122a172@posting.google.com> <i8rsk0tmk9oqol6rnqai0qu7m1no7ae03e@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409201033.3bad2702@posting.google.com> <8viuk0ps1uogme0k00r5e8h921jp4e35gh@4ax.com>`

```
In article <8viuk0ps1uogme0k00r5e8h921jp4e35gh@4ax.com>,
 Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:

> The notion that all this was known in the '70s is a common misbelief.
> In the comments of trees.cbl, I said:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Colin Day in 1976, then refined by Rolfe in 2002."

Adelson-Velskii and Landis described the idea of balancing by rotation 
as far back as 1962.

For details see "An algorithm for the organization of information", 
Georgii M. Adelson-Velskii and Evgenii M. Landis, 1962.
```

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>On 20 Sep 2004 11:33:04 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
>
…[86 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Reference your last - let's see when I'm done :-)     
May still be slow, but I doubt your factor of 50.

Seriously - because I can't understand - you got your 'oodemo' to work, 
right ? The bugger wouldn't work for me until I changed your comp(s) to 
comp-5(s). Robert to my mind you committed a cardinal sin. If a vendor 
or third-party gives you the syntax for a method :-

        invoke <someObject> <method-name>  using <aaaaa> returning <bbbbbb>

you had better ensure that 'aaaaa' and 'bbbbbb' are the same format as 
the original coder intended !

You also had 'Perform destory-data' - didn't the compiler kick that out 
with 'missing Paragraph-Name' ?

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 65)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:d72pk05fqms0eu8gg3hi3mngt6qo87p80f@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 18 Sep 2004 15:03:06 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> consider their return codes meaningless too.

Mr Wagner, I said nothing of 'zOS and Unix'.
Did you intend something other than what may be commonly
understood by 'exit status'?

> >Programmers, using standard COBOL, could return exit
> >status to the user in the form of a message, which was a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Success/failure log entries are based on return code.

In my experience, as a user, MS-DOS batch jobs are run
by users and '[d]ecisions about what to do next' may be
based on something other than 'return code'. I am unaware
of any 'log' on MS-DOS or Windows.

> As for interactive users, programmers could not send a message to
> Windows users via standard Cobol. Before OO features were added in
> 2002, the standard doesn't support communication with the Windows GUI.

Mr Wagner, using the CALL statement to send a message
to Windows is not contrary to standard COBOL. Furthermore,
programming for Windows did not, in my experience, require OO.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 56)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-19T14:33:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c16rk01ptdocb60mi0lo9bp6vg1vjs9th9@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <vhsok0htrik0vr0dsr69u09jqu6o2fol9s@4ax.com> <10kp1lj9896i646@corp.supernews.com> <d72pk05fqms0eu8gg3hi3mngt6qo87p80f@4ax.com> <10kpce1pm3fsb15@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Sat, 18 Sep 2004 17:58:10 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:d72pk05fqms0eu8gg3hi3mngt6qo87p80f@4ax.com...
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>understood by 'exit status'?

When you said exit status was meaningless on DOS and Windows, the
implication was it had meaning on other platforms. 

In fact, it has no more meaning on other platforms than it does on
Windows. A programmer can arbitrarily pick any number other than zero.
There is no standard for assigning numbers on any platform.

>> >Programmers, using standard COBOL, could return exit
>> >status to the user in the form of a message, which was a
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>of any 'log' on MS-DOS or Windows.

In my experience with Unix, I've often run batch programs from the
command line. In my experience with TSO, ditto. Please show why the
same logic doesn't  apply to those platforms.

>> As for interactive users, programmers could not send a message to
>> Windows users via standard Cobol. Before OO features were added in
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>programming for Windows did not, in my experience, require OO.

It IS possible to do GUI by calling the Windows API directly, although
I doubt that's in your experience. How do you do it from Cobol? You'd
have to develop your own listboxes, slider bars, etc.

Most real world GUIs (outside VB, Java and .NET) are done via the
Microsoft Foundation Class library (MFC, formerly AFX). The word Class
should offer a clue that it's OO. Using it requires more than a CALL,
it requires the ability to inherit classes from outside Cobol.
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:c16rk01ptdocb60mi0lo9bp6vg1vjs9th9@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 18 Sep 2004 17:58:10 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >> >"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in
message
> >> >> >news:ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com...
> >> >>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> implication was it had meaning on other platforms.

Mr Wagner, I said, in effect, that the 'returning [of] an exit
status' to MS-DOS or Windows was meaningless.

However, 'returning an exit status', in the form of a text messge,
to a user may be meaningful to that user--certainly to a developer--
when the application platform is MS-DOS or Windows.

> In fact, it has no more meaning on other platforms than it does on
> Windows. A programmer can arbitrarily pick any number other than zero.
> There is no standard for assigning numbers on any platform.

I would hope the programmer picks a reasonable text message
when returning exit status to a user.

> >> >Programmers, using standard COBOL, could return exit
> >> >status to the user in the form of a message, which was a
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> same logic doesn't  apply to those platforms.

My limited experience with Unix, nearly twenty years ago,
was to use Unix in a manner similar to MS-DOS. I have no
experience with [IBM's (?)] TSO. I cannot respond to your
request.

> >> As for interactive users, programmers could not send a message to
> >> Windows users via standard Cobol. Before OO features were added in
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> have to develop your own listboxes, slider bars, etc.

The practice, years ago, was to use the Windows SDK to
create the resources for the GUI, then write the COBOL
program to be equivalent to a C program doing the same
thing. No OO.

With Micro Focus, one could use Dialog System. No OO.
Or Flexus SP2. No OO.

I experimented successfully with each, became disabled,
then stopped trying.

> Most real world GUIs (outside VB, Java and .NET) are done via the
> Microsoft Foundation Class library (MFC, formerly AFX). The word Class
> should offer a clue that it's OO. Using it requires more than a CALL,
> it requires the ability to inherit classes from outside Cobol.

Only if one uses MFC directly, Mr Wagner.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Nonsence about MFC

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-19T20:04:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409191904.5ee110e0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <vhsok0htrik0vr0dsr69u09jqu6o2fol9s@4ax.com> <10kp1lj9896i646@corp.supernews.com> <d72pk05fqms0eu8gg3hi3mngt6qo87p80f@4ax.com> <10kpce1pm3fsb15@corp.supernews.com> <c16rk01ptdocb60mi0lo9bp6vg1vjs9th9@4ax.com> <10krkkm13so20c1@corp.supernews.com> <tcurk0t4jd0tqdh72snl18iu9tgn4vgoca@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> >With Micro Focus, one could use Dialog System. No OO.
> >Or Flexus SP2. No OO.
> 
> Same for Fujitsu's PowerCobol. I could be wrong, but I strongly
> suspect all three are written in C++ and are using MFC. 

Yes, you could well be completely wrong.  

According to Bob Wolfe, posting on this very group (so it can be
determined easily for those that prefer to use facts rather than
opinions):

""" sp2.dll screen handler is written in C """

Both SP2 and Dailog system date from before MFC was available.  This
makes your claim unsupportable.  Even a cursory glance at facts should
have shown you that it wasn't viable.

> I say that
> because controls look and behave identically. Not sorta-kinda the
> same.

You don't seem to know much about MFC, or indeed Windows at all.  MFC
is just a thin OO (some might say: sorta OO) wrapper around the
standard Windows controls. They all look the same because they are
_Windows_, regardless of whether they are MFC.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nonsence about MFC

_(thread depth: 60)_

- **From:** "Lorne Sunley" <lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T03:48:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JMPlV5b5VFnN-pn2-74mIYz8d26xd@S01060080c8f879e3.wp.shawcable.net>`
- **References:** `<cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <vhsok0htrik0vr0dsr69u09jqu6o2fol9s@4ax.com> <10kp1lj9896i646@corp.supernews.com> <d72pk05fqms0eu8gg3hi3mngt6qo87p80f@4ax.com> <10kpce1pm3fsb15@corp.supernews.com> <c16rk01ptdocb60mi0lo9bp6vg1vjs9th9@4ax.com> <10krkkm13so20c1@corp.supernews.com> <tcurk0t4jd0tqdh72snl18iu9tgn4vgoca@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409191904.5ee110e0@posting.google.com>`

```
On Mon, 20 Sep 2004 03:04:12 UTC, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 
> 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> _Windows_, regardless of whether they are MFC.

The original dialog system (char and gui) was written in COBOL and 
used the PANELS (char) or PANELS2 (gui) interface to call standard Win
or PM (that's OS/2 for the OS deprived) controls. You could do your 
own calls to panels2 to do GUI stuff without requiring dialog system. 
The panels and panels2 modules were also written in COBOL. In those 
days Microfocus really ate their own dogfood.

Dialog system was a nice cross platform system for OS/2 and Window up 
Workbench 4.0, after that the NetExpress products went (mostly) 
Windows only. We wrote the Grandmaster II and Grandmaster Suite 
Payroll products using dialog system.

The controls, of course looked like whatever the controls looked like 
as provided by Windows or OS/2. Same code that was run on OS/2, 
Windows 3.1 and Windows 95 always  picked up the "native" look for the
OS.

If you want to do the work you can write calls to the base API on 
Windows or OS/2 to use the standard controls and dialogs. Just call 
the appropriate API, the same way you do in C or C++ (more or less 
according to the language syntax).

Adn once the OO code was available for MF COBOL you could call Dialog 
system from within OO stuff the same way you could call it before.
```

- **References:** `<ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <vhsok0htrik0vr0dsr69u09jqu6o2fol9s@4ax.com> <10kp1lj9896i646@corp.supernews.com> <d72pk05fqms0eu8gg3hi3mngt6qo87p80f@4ax.com> <10kpce1pm3fsb15@corp.supernews.com> <c16rk01ptdocb60mi0lo9bp6vg1vjs9th9@4ax.com> <10krkkm13so20c1@corp.supernews.com> <tcurk0t4jd0tqdh72snl18iu9tgn4vgoca@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409191904.5ee110e0@posting.google.com> <qgqsk05alujt6otko9la731sato3vrufuq@4ax.com>`

```
On Mon, 20 Sep 2004 06:08:48 UTC, Robert Wagner 
<robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:

<snip> 

> The Windows API doesn't support things like ListBox, ComboBox and
> scroll bars. That functionality is supplied by MFC (and VB and Swing).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

WHAT, where do you get this misinformation from.

That is totally untrue

List boxes, combo boxes, containers, tab lists etc are standard  
Windows controls. They have nothing to do with MFC, other than MFC 
calls the standard controls. The three controls you reference were 
available in Windows 3.x

You really have to do some rersearch before you spout off this kind of
nonsense.

Not to mention that Swing is a set of Java classes that has nothing to
do with MFC or Windows controls.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nonsence about MFC

_(thread depth: 61)_

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-22T15:25:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ds53l0dlju5ngg175s7kk6451phpf59ar3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <vhsok0htrik0vr0dsr69u09jqu6o2fol9s@4ax.com> <10kp1lj9896i646@corp.supernews.com> <d72pk05fqms0eu8gg3hi3mngt6qo87p80f@4ax.com> <10kpce1pm3fsb15@corp.supernews.com> <c16rk01ptdocb60mi0lo9bp6vg1vjs9th9@4ax.com> <10krkkm13so20c1@corp.supernews.com> <tcurk0t4jd0tqdh72snl18iu9tgn4vgoca@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409191904.5ee110e0@posting.google.com> <qgqsk05alujt6otko9la731sato3vrufuq@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:

>On 19 Sep 2004 20:04:12 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
>
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bob Wolfe: please ring in with the answer. 

All of the above are Win32 controls.

....but way back in the early 1990's many of our customers wanted
bigger, better, faster or fancier type controls.  Well......it seemed
that writing custom controls ourselves was a yeoman's task and
possibly an exercise in futility.  Obviously, everyone would have very
unique needs for various types of controls.  Therefore, we decided
that Microsoft's VBX control approach showed promise and began
supporting that.

Eventually, with the advent of the 32 bit version of Windows,
Microsoft introduced 32 bit OLE and OCX (Active X) controls, which
were even better.

So our VBX support evolved into OCX and OLE automation support.  This
approach seems to have worked very well as it offers the opportunity
for a programmer to use one of tens (perhaps hundreds) of thousands of
unique user interface and non-user interface controls.  It really
opened up a huge set of possibilities for enhancing one's COBOL
program.

The OLE automation also allows for the control of Microsoft Office
applications which seems to be of great interest as well.



Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>""" sp2.dll screen handler is written in C """

Just wanted to confirm Richard's comment.  He is correct.  sp2 was
originally written in the C language when we first started writing the
software back in 1983.

Obviously back then it wasn't a dynamic link library...rather it was
an executable in DOS and a linkable object file in UNIX and UNIX
derivatives.

When we first released the dynamic link library version for Windows,
we continued to use the C language, because being a system software
product (low level screen handling tool), it just made good sense for
us to use C.

The programming interface to control the dll is COBOL....the dll
itself is C.

>Both SP2 and Dailog system date from before MFC was available.

This is also correct.  Dialog dates all the way back to the late
1980's (for DOS character mode).  sp2 was first released as a
production software product for DOS back in 1986, for UNIX in 1987,
for VMS in 1987, GUI (Sun Open Look) in 1989, Windows 3.0 in 1990.

>makes your claim unsupportable.  Even a cursory glance at facts should
>have shown you that it wasn't viable.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>_Windows_, regardless of whether they are MFC.


Bob Wolfe
```

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-19T16:34:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409191534.170806eb@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <vhsok0htrik0vr0dsr69u09jqu6o2fol9s@4ax.com> <10kp1lj9896i646@corp.supernews.com> <d72pk05fqms0eu8gg3hi3mngt6qo87p80f@4ax.com> <10kpce1pm3fsb15@corp.supernews.com> <c16rk01ptdocb60mi0lo9bp6vg1vjs9th9@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> It IS possible to do GUI by calling the Windows API directly, although
> I doubt that's in your experience. How do you do it from Cobol? You'd
> have to develop your own listboxes, slider bars, etc.

It certainly is within _my_ experience. My first Cobol Windows GUI
program was developed for Windows 3.0 nearly 15 years ago.  It used
entirely standard Windows listboxes, slider bars, etc, did graphics
painting and printing.  Over the next decade I developed an extensive
entirely graphics application in Cobol.

> Most real world GUIs (outside VB, Java and .NET) are done via the
> Microsoft Foundation Class library (MFC, formerly AFX). The word Class
> should offer a clue that it's OO. Using it requires more than a CALL,
> it requires the ability to inherit classes from outside Cobol.

Many 'real world GUIs' are not Windows at all, for example Macintosh,
Gnome, KDE, and have nothing to do with MFC.  Even within Windows
there is no requirement to use MFC which is merely a thin wrapper
around the Windows GUI components, they not being particularly OO at
all.

MFC is used mainly for C++ but most large developments would bypass
this and develop a more functional, higher level, library, or buy one.
```
