###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-21T14:43:22-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10l0tkgt7vi7146@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <vhsok0htrik0vr0dsr69u09jqu6o2fol9s@4ax.com> <10kp1lj9896i646@corp.supernews.com> <d72pk05fqms0eu8gg3hi3mngt6qo87p80f@4ax.com> <10kpce1pm3fsb15@corp.supernews.com> <c16rk01ptdocb60mi0lo9bp6vg1vjs9th9@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409191534.170806eb@posting.google.com> <%ZL3d.462234$M95.116615@pd7tw1no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:%ZL3d.462234$M95.116615@pd7tw1no...
[snip]
> Richard,
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> only became available with C++.

I didn't use list boxes; at least, not really. I did modify
the example setup program from Visual Basic 1.0 (DOS)
to install the software I maintained. I used a list box (and
check boxes, etc.) in that software to allow the user to
configure the installation. But, neither the COBOL nor C
applications used list boxes.

Mr Gavan, with all respect, I think OO terminology has
muddled your thinking. A list is a homogeneous set of items.
A collection is a, possibly, heterogeneous set of items. That
is, lists and collections are not synonymous.

Lists are a feature of both COBOL and C. In COBOL
they may be stored in tables; in C they may be stored in
arrays. In both they may be stored in files.

Collections are a feature of both COBOL and C. Once
again they may be stored in tables or arrays or files; but
carry a type identifier to distinguish the items.

In OO, the means for distinguishing items is the class of
the object; therefore, the same storage mechanism is used,
without further consideration, for storing objects (items) of
both lists and collections. This, effectively, removes the
distinction between the two.

'remove distinction' -> 'obscure' -> 'confuse' -> 'muddle'
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-21T12:38:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409211138.6ece56c1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <vhsok0htrik0vr0dsr69u09jqu6o2fol9s@4ax.com> <10kp1lj9896i646@corp.supernews.com> <d72pk05fqms0eu8gg3hi3mngt6qo87p80f@4ax.com> <10kpce1pm3fsb15@corp.supernews.com> <c16rk01ptdocb60mi0lo9bp6vg1vjs9th9@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409191534.170806eb@posting.google.com> <%ZL3d.462234$M95.116615@pd7tw1no>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote 

> Funnily enough was thinking of posting this one to Rick, but perhaps you 
> can both indicate what you did. Prior to N/E while still with DOS, using 
> M/F's Screen  Section  

I never used 'SCREEN SECTION' except for a few test programs, never
liked it.  I did however use massive amounts of ACCEPT/DISPLAY AT
since MF CIS Cobol on CP/M and MP/M and other 8 bit machines, some of
which is still being used, though further developed of course. I did
my own screen colouring.

> Now I recall a message from you many moons ago that you 'limited' the 
> contents of a 'page' going into the Listbox. Out of curiosity how did 
> both you and Rick handle Lists(collections) 

In general I never used real 'listboxes', never liked them.  For SP/2
I would fake a list box by having a repeat group of 10 items and fill
this each time the user indicated up or down outside the current list.
Usually this was filled from a search or a file.  It is only necessary
to keep an array of 'page starts' so stepping back is easy.

This means that the latest data is always shown - if someone adds a
record it gets shown on the page whereas a listbox is filled once
(usually).  There is also no problem with limits, one can show 'next
page' forever, and the user doesn't have to wait, for example, for all
the data to be searched to see the first results which may happen on
an extensive search.

Of course, where it is more approriate, I do use other mechanisms.

Quite often for input fields where there is a list of viable options
from Customer code to 'status' (as in recent question) a function key
will produce a child window with a pageable list (10 at a time)
starting at the contents of the  input field (where appropriate).  For
example if the Customer code is numeric (one client uses phone number)
and a number is input it will list numerically from there, if alpha is
input then it will list alphabetically on customer alpha key (defaults
to name), if '=xxx' is input it will search using the word file, but
again the first 10 will be shown until 'pagedown'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 55)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T08:24:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cimsnu$24rf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <vhsok0htrik0vr0dsr69u09jqu6o2fol9s@4ax.com> <10kp1lj9896i646@corp.supernews.com> <d72pk05fqms0eu8gg3hi3mngt6qo87p80f@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:d72pk05fqms0eu8gg3hi3mngt6qo87p80f@4ax.com...

> Batch jobs aren't run by users; they are run by automated schedulers
> or scripts.

In our case, WFL.

> Decisions about what to do next are based on return code.
> Success/failure log entries are based on return code.

No, they're most likely based on the value of the STATUS task attribute
associated with the task.  If a task terminated abnormally, the reasons can
be found in the HISTORY, HISTORYCAUSE and HISTORYREASON task attributes.
Although a program can set the STATUS attribute to cause a program
termination (e.g., "CHANGE ATTRIBUTE STATUS OF MYSELF TO VALUE (DSED)" in
COBOL or "MYSELF.STATUS := VALUE (DSED)" in ALGOL) what the particular
numbers are aren't of concern to the COBOL programmer, or, for that matter,
the WFL programmer who is looking at the task variable.  A COBOL program, by
the way, can "fire off" another COBOL program asynchronously, monitor its
progress, and take action upon its successful or unsuccessful termination.
The COBOL standard doesn't provide for that either.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message 
news:cimsnu$24rf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
>
> "Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
> news:d72pk05fqms0eu8gg3hi3mngt6qo87p80f@4ax.com...
>
<snip>
>   A COBOL program, by
> the way, can "fire off" another COBOL program asynchronously, monitor its
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Chuck (or I or Thane) have mentioned it in other threads, but "asynchronous" 
processing was something that WG4 asked to be included in the 2008 Standard *if* 
it could be developed in time - and not delay other features.  (Chuck can 
probably quote the exact wording of this "request").  To the best of my 
knowledge (and I could be mistaken on this) there have been no papers submitted 
(yet) reflecting work on this development.

Again, I may have missed either a change in the request or the progress, but 
this is my current understanding.

P.S.  IBM mainframes can already do this *easily* for CICS programs and via PL/I 
or Assembler for more complex applications.  Micro Focus has a "CHAIN" statement 
that does *minimal" asynchronous processing - and other vendors may have their 
own extensions as well (just as Chuck reports that Unisys does)
```

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message

"asynchronous"
> processing was something that WG4 asked to be included in the 2008
Standard *if*
> it could be developed in time - and not delay other features.  (Chuck can
> probably quote the exact wording of this "request").  To the best of my

submitted

No, that reflects the current status.  And even if papers had been submitted
on the subject, it's very unlikely that J4 would have had the resources to
develop them sufficiently for the 2008 standard; as it is, we'll be hustling
to get the mandatory elements done in time.

> P.S.  IBM mainframes can already do this *easily* for CICS programs and
via PL/I
> or Assembler for more complex applications.  Micro Focus has a "CHAIN"
statement
> that does *minimal" asynchronous processing - and other vendors may have
their
> own extensions as well (just as Chuck reports that Unisys does)

I'd say the asynchronicity available on Unisys MCP systems is rather more
than "minimal", though I concur it does involve Unisys extensions (USAGE
EVENT items that can be CAUSEd, RESET, queried and WAITed for; USAGE LOCK
items that may be LOCKed and UNLOCKed, and of course USAGE TASK items that
can be ATTACHed to and DETACHed from external procedures that can be
PROCESSed or RUN as well as have its attributes CHANGEd.).   I've thought
about proposing something to J4 based on the Unisys MCP syntax as a starting
point for discussion, and may still after the current standard is ready to
go, but I don't have the time to deal with it right now.

    -Chuck Stevens

n MCP system
>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
>I'd say the asynchronicity available on Unisys MCP systems is rather more

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>go, but I don't have the time to deal with it right now.

   ACOB (ASCII COBOL, i.e. COBOL-74) for OS/1100 i.e. ClearPath IX also
provides an Asynchronous Processing module with an "Asynchronous Control
System" (ACS). 

   This is geared rather at managing separate threads running concurrently,
than reacting on events. For event processing, COBOL already provides the
DECLARATIVES in the PROCEDURE DIVISION. 

Yours, 
L. Willms
```

```

"L�ko Willms" <lw-nospam@t-online.de> wrote in message
news:yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i4g5ej2.pminews@news.t-online.de...

>    ACOB (ASCII COBOL, i.e. COBOL-74) for OS/1100 i.e. ClearPath IX also
> provides an Asynchronous Processing module with an "Asynchronous Control
> System" (ACS).

In Unisys MCP COBOLs, it's not a "module", it's simply verbs and data items.

>    This is geared rather at managing separate threads running
concurrently, ...

ditto ...

> than reacting on events. For event processing, COBOL already provides the
> DECLARATIVES in the PROCEDURE DIVISION.

The "events" I was referring to are user-defined events, in which one part
of an asynchronous process CAUSEs a USAGE EVENT item visible to another
process that is doing a "complex WAIT" that includes that USAGE EVENT item
among its arguments; when the event "happens" the second process wakes up.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
>P.S.  IBM mainframes can already do this *easily* for CICS programs and via PL/I
```

- **Message-ID:** `<10kpce2pk6vm417@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <dv%2d.20475$lb5.1486651@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:dv%2d.20475$lb5.1486651@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Meaningless?  Why?  (I've been using it for several years).

I addressed its meaninglessness in my reply to Mr Wagner;
however, returning 'exit status' to a user is not necessarily
meaningless for MS-DOS or Windows.

I too have used the RETURN-CODE special register, in
Micro Focus, 'to pass a value to ... the execution environment'.
[Quote from Micro Focus 3.2 LRM] I did so, in part,
because users ran the application in a DOS box on Windows,
with 'Close on exit' checked. Messages sent to users were
discarded by Windows when it closed the DOS box. The
execution environment was a C program that detected the
RETURN-CODE for runtime errors and kept the window
from closing until the message was acknowledged by the user.
```

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>

- **In-Reply-To:** `<10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> Consider the effect of 'returning an exit status' to MS-DOS
> or Windows. It's meaningless!

Is that not how my old BBS software used to let me write "errorlevel" 
checking batch files?  Maybe these days it's meaningless, but 
back-in-the-day, it was a quite useful, and without exception, every 
software package I used to run the BBS during that time (FrontDoor, 
RemoteAccess, FastEcho, BinkleyTerm, Maximus, Squish, MainDoor) would 
set an errorlevel when they exited.  BinkleyTerm could even be 
configured to exit with a custom errorlevel at a given time - handy for 
scheduling the nightly maintenance and weekly defrag.
```

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:%d83d.24$Z36.7@fe40.usenetserver.com...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> checking batch files?

I don't know? It seems to me that 'old BBS software'
written to return 'exit status' to MS-DOS for the purpose
of controlling batch files is not the same type of software
written to return 'exit status' to a user for the purpose of
immediate error resolution.

In the latter case, 'returning an exit status' to MS-DOS
is meaningless; because MS-DOS can't do anything
to resolve the error.
```

```
My point was that you're engaging in what's come to be known here as a 
"sweeping generality" - just because _you_ don't see a use for it 
doesn't mean that it's not useful.  Rather than just say "you're wrong", 
I thought I would give an example of how they would be useful.

Wasn't it you that said that you called your COBOL from C, and looked at 
the exit status to achieve some functionality?  Doesn't sound 
meaningless to me.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 55)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-19T16:00:25-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10krpcvbeb0oi3b@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <%d83d.24$Z36.7@fe40.usenetserver.com> <10kqcsd8k0aj52f@corp.supernews.com> <rhk3d.2589$Z36.732@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:rhk3d.2589$Z36.732@fe40.usenetserver.com...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> > "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> I thought I would give an example of how they would be useful.

Not 'sweeping generality'. '[E]xit status' is a generally
useful term that can mean a number of different things,
depending on context. One use of 'exit status' may be
'return code'; that is, 'returning an exit status' to
MS-DOS. Another use of 'exit status' may be a text
message to a user.

On Novemeber 2, 2004 (US general election), a
number of people will be engaged in exit polling. What
might 'exit status' mean in that context?

> Wasn't it you that said that you called your COBOL from C, and looked at
> the exit status to achieve some functionality?  Doesn't sound
> meaningless to me.

No, I said '[t]he execution environment was a C program'.
I actually used '_spawnl' which is closer to entering a
command at a DOS prompt. 'x1' and 'x2' are the same
as would be entered at a DOS prompt.

    RCode = _spawnl( _P_WAIT, "x0", "x1", "x2", NULL );

And again no. I looked at a 'return code' with the intent of
preventing batch files and Windows from eliminating the 'exit
status' (text message) before the user could record it.

Furthermore, when the C program terminated, it returned
zero to MS-DOS; because, ... well ... zero had as much
meaning as anything else.
```

```
for others.

> On Novemeber 2, 2004 (US general election), a
> number of people will be engaged in exit polling. What
> might 'exit status' mean in that context?

I'd say someone is stretching the term "exit polling" to fit their 
argument.  :)

>>Wasn't it you that said that you called your COBOL from C, and looked at
>>the exit status to achieve some functionality?  Doesn't sound
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> status' (text message) before the user could record it.

Ah - I see how you're using the term.  But, above, you said that the 
term "exit status" could refer to return code as well.  Still think it's 
meaningless?

> Furthermore, when the C program terminated, it returned
> zero to MS-DOS; because, ... well ... zero had as much
> meaning as anything else.

At that point, you're right - you didn't attach any meaning to it.  What 
if that program was but one in a sequence of steps, and if any of the 
steps died, you wanted to abandon the rest of the process?  Couldn't you 
have had the C program return a "1" or "99" or something like that, and 
had the .bat or .cmd file coded to do so?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 57)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T11:28:39-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10kttrlfgtu38b1@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <%d83d.24$Z36.7@fe40.usenetserver.com> <10kqcsd8k0aj52f@corp.supernews.com> <rhk3d.2589$Z36.732@fe40.usenetserver.com> <10krpcvbeb0oi3b@corp.supernews.com> <Ipo3d.675$vl.361@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:Ipo3d.675$vl.361@fe40.usenetserver.com...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> > "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> for others.

I think it reasonable to consider that my comment was
directed toward COBOL programming and not toward
... gag ... gasp ... batch file programming. <g>

Having said that, it is true that I have not written every
possible COBOL application and, therefore, cannot
deny that others might find uses for a return-code on
MS-DOS or Windows.

I would never have considered sending a return-code
to MS-DOS with the intent of controlling a batch file,
since, from the beginning, batch files were the problem,
not the solution.

[snip]
> >>Wasn't it you that said that you called your COBOL from C, and looked at
> >>the exit status to achieve some functionality?  Doesn't sound
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> meaningless?

To send it to MS-DOS or Windows? Yes; but maybe
that is just me.

> > Furthermore, when the C program terminated, it returned
> > zero to MS-DOS; because, ... well ... zero had as much
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> had the .bat or .cmd file coded to do so?

Some parts of the COBOL application did execute
'a sequence of steps' and, in the case of failure, the
application had all information necessary to resume
processing at the correct step; once the error was
resolved. Indeed, if the application was restarted
without separate resolution, the application wouild
assist the user in resolving the problem.

I think resumption in the middle of a batch file would
have been impossible. The batch file could not
possibly assist the user. So, no, *I* couldn't have
the C program do any other than what *I* had it do.

In fact, the first COBOL program loaded was
essentially the 'script' for executing the application.
Where, for example, DOS utilities were used, they
were executed from within that COBOL program.
```

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:txI3d.11868$vl.5112@fe40.usenetserver.com...
> Rick Smith wrote:
[snip]
> > I think it reasonable to consider that my comment was
> > directed toward COBOL programming and not toward
> > ... gag ... gasp ... batch file programming. <g>
>
> COBOL can't be used by batch files?  :)

In my experience, where a program displays a message
to inform the user of a cause for the program's termination,
regardless of programming laanguage, the use of batch
files is unsafe.

They could be made safer by always including, something
like (and if I recall correctly):
    IF ERRORLEVEL 1 PAUSE
after every command.

> > Having said that, it is true that I have not written every
> > possible COBOL application and, therefore, cannot
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Batch files are the problem?  I'm guessing you're a Windows guy, right?

No! More than ten years and best I can say is that
Solitaire has never crashed the system.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 57)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-09-22T07:51:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9HLnf53eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <10krpcvbeb0oi3b@corp.supernews.com> <Ipo3d.675$vl.361@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
.    Am  19.09.04
schrieb  lxi0007@netscape.net (LX-i)
    auf  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  Ipo3d.675$vl.361@fe40.usenetserver.com
  ueber  Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

   On passing exit codes from a PC program under DOS or Windows:

l> At that point, you're right - you didn't attach any meaning to it.
l> What if that program was but one in a sequence of steps, and if any of
l> the steps died, you wanted to abandon the rest of the process?
l> Couldn't you have had the C program return a "1" or "99" or something
l> like that, and had the .bat or .cmd file coded to do so?

   The fact is that in most cases, I dare to estimate, 99% of all  
cases, the return code (or exit code) of a program is not used.

   You would only do it in elaborated .BAT or .CMD files where e.g.  
the linker should only be called when the compiler had not found any  
errors.

   But situations like this are superseded by interactive GUI IDEs.


Yours,

corsaire, corsaire et demi [auf einen Schelmen anderthalben]."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 58)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-09-22T14:05:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cis0s7023ps@news3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <10krpcvbeb0oi3b@corp.supernews.com> <Ipo3d.675$vl.361@fe40.usenetserver.com> <9HLnf53eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```

In article <9HLnf53eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) writes:
> 
>    The fact is that in most cases, I dare to estimate, 99% of all  
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> errors.

make, and many similar build systems, relies on exit codes.  And there
are many scripting / batch processing facilities - yes, for PCs too -
besides .BAT and .CMD files.  Not all the world is CMD.EXE.

>    But situations like this are superseded by interactive GUI IDEs.

Nonsense.  While many developers may like IDEs (why, I have no idea),
many, many project builds are automated.  If you're doing automatic
nightly integration builds for testing, for example, it doesn't make
sense to have some QA tester sitting there clicking IDE buttons to
kick them off.

For that matter, all the IDEs I've used in recent years have the
capability to invoke external programs, do so for a variety of tasks,
and pay attention to their exit codes.

I suspect your 99% is completely bogus, though I have no idea how
anyone would go about measuring it.  (First, describe a representative
sample of all programs.)  Certainly it's bogus in the case of software
development tools.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 58)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cVJ4d.25923$vl.19443@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
Lueko Willms wrote:
> .    Am  19.09.04
> schrieb  lxi0007@netscape.net (LX-i)
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>    But situations like this are superseded by interactive GUI IDEs.

Sure, for manual user programs.  I've been dabbling in Linux (actually, 
using it exclusively now for a couple of weeks - only had to boot to WXP 
to get some files off the NTFS system - haven't gotten around to getting 
that going under Linux yet), and working some with shell scripts.

<opinion>For system administration automation, I don't think anything 
will ever top a simple batch file or shell script for its sheer 
simplicity and effectiveness.</opinion>  Wouldn't it be great if we 
could count on programs to still allow us to write them?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 55)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T02:54:10-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10ksvn81r578uc1@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <%d83d.24$Z36.7@fe40.usenetserver.com> <10kqcsd8k0aj52f@corp.supernews.com> <h9q3d.7712$bL1.225735@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:h9q3d.7712$bL1.225735@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> > "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cobol program.

It would never have occurred to me to load and execute
a separate COBOL executable where, for example, a
fatal error in that executable should not terminate the entire
application; so I would not have considered any but CALL.

Since I have not written every possible COBOL application,
I cannot deny that in such an arrangement, returning an exit
status (return-code) to a COBOL program, the exit status
would be meaningless.

It would still be true that the COBOL program receiving
a fatal error return-code would need to terminate to
preserve the text of the runtime error, since the COBOL
program can't do anything to resolve the error.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 57)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T10:22:17-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10ktpvbc54aeic9@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <%d83d.24$Z36.7@fe40.usenetserver.com> <10kqcsd8k0aj52f@corp.supernews.com> <h9q3d.7712$bL1.225735@news20.bellglobal.com> <10ksvn81r578uc1@corp.supernews.com> <_3z3d.7944$bL1.321193@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:_3z3d.7944$bL1.321193@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> > "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> could resolve the error.

Mr Tees, I will use 'calling program' because you did;
but we must both understand that a CALL statement
was not used to load and execute the 'called progam'.

As I see it, there are two cases for MS-DOS:

1. A fatal runtime error displays a message on the console
and supplies 255 as its return-code.

2. A fatal runtime error displays a message on the console
and supplies a specific error number as its return-code.

I believe the first case is true of Micro Focus but cannot
locate a reference and have not tested it.

In the first case, all fatal errors having the same value,
there is not enough information for the 'calling program'
to resolve the problem; but application exceptions may
be returned and resolved.

In the second case, there may be enough information for
the 'calling program' to make some decisions; but this
prevents the use of return-code for other purposes, such
as application exceptions.

If the first case is true, then the 'calling program' cannot
resolve any problem which is also a fatal error.
```

_(thread depth: 58)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T12:21:15-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<arD3d.8081$bL1.369214@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<10ktpvbc54aeic9@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <ZaJ2d.1540642$6p.261759@news.easynews.com> <ga8nk0tgaon5kcek3425uk5m713m58ugfh@4ax.com> <10kolsfqpvcps00@corp.supernews.com> <%d83d.24$Z36.7@fe40.usenetserver.com> <10kqcsd8k0aj52f@corp.supernews.com> <h9q3d.7712$bL1.225735@news20.bellglobal.com> <10ksvn81r578uc1@corp.supernews.com> <_3z3d.7944$bL1.321193@news20.bellglobal.com> <10ktpvbc54aeic9@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
>>But that is precisely why you would do it ... so the calling program
>>could resolve the error.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Agreed.  I should have said "executed program".

> As I see it, there are two cases for MS-DOS:
> 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Well, what you are enforcing on the programmer is one monolithic piece 
of code. There are several instances where I use a Cobol executable 
program (that is, and EXE module) from within a second piece of cobol. 
There is a need to be able to communicate more than a 255 or -1 in those 
cases.

Conversion of files is a typical case.  I get a 39 status code in the 
main program.  I want to run a second, stand-alone-program that checks 
the file causing the error. I need to know how it did.

There are several other instances where I do similar things ... code 
generation, for example.  I need to know if a program compiled and 
linked before I try to run it.

Lastly, I need to be able to run MF cobol from withing Fujitsu Cobol, 
and vice versa.  Either that or keep two complete versions of every 
single software tool I ever wrote in Cobol.

Donald
```

```
On Mon, 20 Sep 2004 16:46:38 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>
>"Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
>news:arD3d.8081$bL1.369214@news20.bellglobal.com...

>> > As I see it, there are two cases for MS-DOS:
>> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>> > locate a reference and have not tested it.

Try the Micro Focus manual at:
http://supportline.microfocus.com/documentation/books/nx31sp1/lrpubb.htm

Look under GOBACK RETURNING integer

>> > In the first case, all fatal errors having the same value,
>> > there is not enough information for the 'calling program'
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>monolithic piece of code'.

The limit of 255 different return codes was because the MS-DOS API
received the code in the byte-sized AL register. 

That has nothing whatever to do with monolithic code. I've written
MS-DOS applications having several dozen dynamically called programs.
```

```
> On Mon, 20 Sep 2004 16:46:38 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Look under GOBACK RETURNING integer

Mr Wagner, this is only remotely related to the discussion.
Specifically, if, for example, a runtime error 114 occurs,
what value, if any, will be reported to the operating system?

The Micro Focus COBOL 3.2 Error Messages manual
does not state that any value will be reported to the
operating system, only that a message will be displayed
and the program terminated.

> >> > In the first case, all fatal errors having the same value,
> >> > there is not enough information for the 'calling program'
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> There is a need to be able to communicate more than a 255 or -1 in
those
> >> cases.
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> MS-DOS applications having several dozen dynamically called programs.

By 'monolithic', I understood Mr Tees to be suggesting
'all code controlled by a single executable file' whether
that is done by static or dynamic linking. The opposite
being separate executable files. At least, that was the
context of the discussion.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 49)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-17T15:22:01-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cifo2m$b3p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<10kgcc3bs5nuv24@corp.supernews.com> <k5sgk01dr0sklr5vdmvt5j8srh1pat7alf@4ax.com> <ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com...

> A common 'incompability' is the default file organization. On IBM
> mainframes it is 'plain vanilla' sequential; on other platforms we
> want it to be line sequential. Under Micro Focus, one need not change
> SELECT statements. A compiler option changes it across the board.

Bill Klein pointed out privately.  that I responded erroneously.  I mentally
omitted the introductory phrase "Under Micro Focus" in this citation and
thought it was a more general statement than IBM-to-Micro-Focus migration.

In our environment, the default organization is indeed "plain vanilla"
sequential, but since a great deal of device and organization independence
is built into the I/O system the distinction can easily be overridden at the
source level on a file-by-file basis; as part of the compilation statement;
at execution time, or even as a permanent change (no, not a patch) to the
compiled object code itself after compilation.  The latter three are
accomplished through the capabilities of WFL.   I think I'd be uncomfortable
with a blanket compiler option; if we were to do something like that I'd
prefer some sort of preprocessor that produced a modified source file for
compilation.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 15:22:01 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>sequential, 

Plain vanilla means one thing in the mainfrane world and something
else in the non-mainframe world. 

>but since a great deal of device and organization independence
>is built into the I/O system the distinction can easily be overridden at the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>accomplished through the capabilities of WFL.  

IBM offers similar capability, especially since the release of SMS
(Tivoli outside mainframes). JCL can override compiler defaults.

> I think I'd be uncomfortable
>with a blanket compiler option; if we were to do something like that I'd
>prefer some sort of preprocessor that produced a modified source file for
>compilation.

I have no problem with it. Line sequential IS the default (most
common) format on non-mainframe platforms. Record sequential is an
oddball excepetion. It seems arrogant for mainframers to impose their
norms on everyone else, as though THEY set standards. Those days are
long gone.
```

```
Now I *know* for sure you have made one of your wildly inaccurate

I just checked N/E V 4.0 - which is very close to COBOL 2002 and any 
syntactical reference to LINE SEQUENTIAL shows a little 'balloon' (MF) - 
it *is* an M/F extension. Similarly checking the FDIS1989.PDF (COBOL 
2002), a search on Adobe doesn't bring up one single reference to 'Line

Standard.
```

- **References:** `<cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com> <cifo2m$b3p$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9g5nk0184nfu5rp4v30bnim8tjfe166qjp@4ax.com> <YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no>`

```
On Sat, 18 Sep 2004 04:23:20 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>statements. SEQUENTIAL IS THE DEFAULT.

I said that. But what does Sequential mean? The standard's definition
is vague. It could be interpreted to mean either mainframe style or
ASCII-delimited style. To distinguish between them, most compilers add
extensions Record and Line. 

Most programmers  code those extensions. I prefer to leave  programs
unchanged, instead define the default with a compiler option. The
choice is a function of the platform, not the individual file.

>I just checked N/E V 4.0 - which is very close to COBOL 2002 and any 
>syntactical reference to LINE SEQUENTIAL shows a little 'balloon' (MF) - 
>it *is* an M/F extension. Similarly checking the FDIS1989.PDF (COBOL 
>2002), a search on Adobe doesn't bring up one single reference to 'Line 
>Sequential'.

You'll be pleased with the purity of my programs. They don't say Line
Sequential either.

>Sure I use it and *will* continue to use it - but it is *not* in the 
>Standard.

So delete it from your programs.
```

```
Here:

>>A common 'incompability' is the default file organization. On IBM
>> mainframes it is 'plain vanilla' sequential; on other platforms we
>> want it to be line sequential. Under Micro Focus, one need not change
>> SELECT statements. A compiler option changes it across the board.

And here, with clarification restored to show I was referring to most
common file format, not the Cobol default.

>>>> Line sequential IS the default (most
>>>>common) format on non-mainframe platforms.
```

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>On 18 Sep 2004 10:20:59 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>>
No sir. No sir. No !. Your were discussing C-O-B-O-L . You never once 
alluded to 'other' languages. Your original text to Chuck :-

I have no problem with it. Line sequential IS the default (most
common) format on non-mainframe platforms. Record sequential is an
oddball excepetion. It seems arrogant for mainframers to impose their
norms on everyone else, as though THEY set standards. Those days are
long gone.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 56)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T16:22:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9g5nk0184nfu5rp4v30bnim8tjfe166qjp@4ax.com> <YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no>`

```

On 18-Sep-2004, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> I have no problem with it. Line sequential IS the default (most
> common) format on non-mainframe platforms. Record sequential is an
> oddball excepetion. It seems arrogant for mainframers to impose their
> norms on everyone else, as though THEY set standards. Those days are
> long gone.

So which is most common format in all CoBOL programs?

I've only worked with various mainframes, minis, and with PCs.  The PCs used
line-sequential, the minis and mainframes I worked with did not.    The reason
mainframers think that their norms are standard is because their norms were
standard from before PCs and even mini computers came along.   So it actually is
saying "old standards are the norm until something takes over and predominates".
  Have PC standards for CoBOL taken over from these old standards?    I haven't
seen any evidence from my side of standards moving up.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 57)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T11:22:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cin75i$2btv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9g5nk0184nfu5rp4v30bnim8tjfe166qjp@4ax.com> <YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 18-Sep-2004, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> So which is most common format in all CoBOL programs?

I believe that the set "all COBOL programs" is primarily made up of legacy
COBOL programs running on systems intended as enterprise servers.  And given
that, I believe files in which I/O operations take place in at least
record-size "chunks" instead of one-character "chunks" or "chunks" delimited
by line terminators of some description make up the *overwhelming* majority
of programs running today.  There are COBOL applications running on PC's,
and there are applications running on Unix servers, that don't meet these
criteria.  But there's a lot of "big iron" out there running a whole lot of
COBOL code that neither know anything about, nor care about, "line
sequential".

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 58)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T22:48:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b0muk0hmi1jrdefeaigh75c8tjinvdcmc8@4ax.com>`

```
On Mon, 20 Sep 2004 11:22:41 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
>news:cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>sequential".

If they don't care about line sequential, they must not care about
comminicating with 90% of the computer world.

Unix and PC platforms don't care what language the application was
written in. They cache big chunks of disk data, often whole files, in
memory. If the application calls for it a byte at a time, its coming
from memory, not off a disk.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-21T09:00:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cipj6t$u34$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9g5nk0184nfu5rp4v30bnim8tjfe166qjp@4ax.com> <YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cin75i$2btv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b0muk0hmi1jrdefeaigh75c8tjinvdcmc8@4ax.com>`

```
I wrote:

> >I believe that the set "all COBOL programs" is primarily made up of
legacy
> >COBOL programs running on systems intended as enterprise servers.  And
given
> >that, I believe files in which I/O operations take place in at least
> >record-size "chunks" instead of one-character "chunks" or "chunks"
delimited
> >by line terminators of some description make up the *overwhelming*
majority
> >of programs running today.  There are COBOL applications running on PC's,
> >and there are applications running on Unix servers, that don't meet these
> >criteria.  But there's a lot of "big iron" out there running a whole lot
of
> >COBOL code that neither know anything about, nor care about, "line
> >sequential".

Robert Wagner responded:

> If they don't care about line sequential, they must not care about
> comminicating with 90% of the computer world.

I can understand the confusion here; I failed to observe English grammar
rules in the last sentence of my paragraph.  *Perhaps* it would have been
clearer had I written "... that neither knows ... nor cares ...".

How much of the code dealing with, say, a production control
distribution/invoicing or credit card transaction database is actually doing
the "communicating with 90% of the computer world" and how much of it is
actually dealing with the internal manipulation of the data?   At some
point, I believe, the application designer on an enterprise server is going
to realize that once the editing is done the most efficient way to pass
information around *in the system* is in clumps, not in single characters.

> Unix and PC platforms don't care what language the application was
> written in. They cache big chunks of disk data, often whole files, in
> memory. If the application calls for it a byte at a time, its coming
> from memory, not off a disk.

If the application "calls" for it a byte at a time, that application is
going to be spending a much higher percentage of time "calling" for data a
byte at a time than if it were "calling" for it a record, or a transaction,
at a time, ISTM.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 60)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-21T17:33:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kbp0l0ps63c6qlil7svg3nnff1mvgn7bd7@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9g5nk0184nfu5rp4v30bnim8tjfe166qjp@4ax.com> <YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cin75i$2btv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b0muk0hmi1jrdefeaigh75c8tjinvdcmc8@4ax.com> <cipj6t$u34$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Tue, 21 Sep 2004 09:00:29 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>I wrote:
>
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>information around *in the system* is in clumps, not in single characters.

Line sequential IS passed in record-sized clumps. Parsing is done
inside the file system. On many machines, it's done with a single
machine-language instruction.

In shops where I've worked recently, data stored inside the system was
100% database. Disk files ('Cobol files') were used only for interface
with other systems, and were always line sequential.

>> Unix and PC platforms don't care what language the application was
>> written in. They cache big chunks of disk data, often whole files, in
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>at a time, ISTM.

True. The case that started this thread was a utility that needed to
copy RECORD sequential files in which the record length was unknown at
compile time. Had the organization been line sequential, it would have
read a record at a time. My suggestion to dynamically build a File
Control Block before opening the file was brushed aside. The only
solution left was reading and writing one-byte records. That's not
something I'd do in a production program, only in a one-shot.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 61)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ciq1uc$186g$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9g5nk0184nfu5rp4v30bnim8tjfe166qjp@4ax.com> <YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cin75i$2btv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b0muk0hmi1jrdefeaigh75c8tjinvdcmc8@4ax.com> <cipj6t$u34$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kbp0l0ps63c6qlil7svg3nnff1mvgn7bd7@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:kbp0l0ps63c6qlil7svg3nnff1mvgn7bd7@4ax.com...

> Line sequential IS passed in record-sized clumps. Parsing is done
> inside the file system. On many machines, it's done with a single
> machine-language instruction.

But it takes *no* machine-language instructions to determine how long a
record is if how long it is is determined at compile time.

>
> In shops where I've worked recently, data stored inside the system was
> 100% database. Disk files ('Cobol files') were used only for interface
> with other systems, and were always line sequential.

Well, that explains everything that's worth knowing about data processing, I
guess.  ;-)

> True. The case that started this thread was a utility that needed to
> copy RECORD sequential files in which the record length was unknown at
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> something I'd do in a production program, only in a one-shot.

Well, I guess I'm too used to being able to use the DEPENDENTSPECS file
attribute.

I know about "File Parameter Blocks" created by the Unisys MCP compilers,
and I know about "File Information Blocks" created by the Unisys MCP from
the File Parameter Blocks and from any attribute equations associated with
the task initiation, but what's a File Control Block, and how do I
dynamically build one before opening a file?   For that matter, what do I do
with it on my Unisys MCP system once I've got it built?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
On Tue, 21 Sep 2004 13:11:55 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>record is if how long it is is determined at compile time.

The time saved is more than offset by time to read trailing spaces ..
at DISK speed.

Worse, it takes human time to figure out the record length in a file
that came from outside, change the FD and recompile the program.

Fixed length creates an arbitrary maximum, for example 80 bytes on
'free form' Cobol source. 

>> True. The case that started this thread was a utility that needed to
>> copy RECORD sequential files in which the record length was unknown at
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>attribute.

How does my PC read your disk catalog?
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:se81l0hgonmcl8dtromrloq8vlh51homkj@4ax.com...

> The time saved is more than offset by time to read trailing spaces ..
> at DISK speed.

That depends on how synchronous your I/O subsystem is, I'd suggest.

> Worse, it takes human time to figure out the record length in a file
> that came from outside, change the FD and recompile the program.

That depends on what "outside" means.

> Fixed length creates an arbitrary maximum, for example 80 bytes on

laptop) ...

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 64)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-23T06:59:18+00:00

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9g5nk0184nfu5rp4v30bnim8tjfe166qjp@4ax.com> <YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Mon, 20 Sep 2004 16:22:01 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>
>On 18-Sep-2004, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>So which is most common format in all CoBOL programs?

It depends on the application and shop standards. In general business
shops where I've worked, the only format for permanently stored data
is database. They used line sequential csv for exchange with outsiders
and Word for printed reports. It's easy to stuff variables into a Word
template.

In data warehousing, the most common format is database, with XML used
for exchange, HTML for display. Exchange is via network. CDs and tapes
are relics of the past.

CSV (comma-delimited) is supported by STRING and UNSTRING. The others
are not supported by standard Cobol (XML will be in 2006). 

Mapping between Cobol types and database types is easy, with the
notable exception of dates. That problem was solved by the addition of
TYPEDEF in the 2002 standard (Micro Focus and Fujitsu already have
it).

Generally, the file format most commonly used by Cobol programs I've
worked on is 'something outside Cobol'. Number two is line sequential.

>I've only worked with various mainframes, minis, and with PCs.  The PCs used
>line-sequential, the minis and mainframes I worked with did not.    The reason
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>seen any evidence from my side of standards moving up.   

 PC Cobol hasn't become dominant, but non-mainframe technologies now
dominate the computer industry .. including Cobol's former stronghold:
general business in medium to large companies. Fewer than ten percent
of full-time programmers are working in Cobol.
```

- **References:** `<cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9g5nk0184nfu5rp4v30bnim8tjfe166qjp@4ax.com> <YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1pcuk09fc58birlncs7nmdg4b73hq7eo01@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message 
news:1pcuk09fc58birlncs7nmdg4b73hq7eo01@4ax.com...
> On Mon, 20 Sep 2004 16:22:01 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
> wrote:
<snip>
> PC Cobol hasn't become dominant, but non-mainframe technologies now
> dominate the computer industry .. including Cobol's former stronghold:
> general business in medium to large companies. Fewer than ten percent
> of full-time programmers are working in Cobol.
>

Robber the only *VALID* conclusion from the statement (that I won't even agree 
is true)

 Fewer than ten percent  of full-time programmers are working in Cobol.

Is

COBOL jobs are not predominant.

  ***

Making an assumption  about what TECHNOLOGY is dominate in the computer industry 
from what JOBS are dominate in the industry is a wild (in my opinion) leap of 
assumption.

The simple fact is that for many (but I don't have the statistics to tell you 
what percentage) of "workstation interface applications" there are MAINFRAME 
applications/programs that interface with the data.  In many cases, these are 
existing ("legacy") applications that require little or no maintenance - much 
less new development.  But if you took away the "mainframe technology", then the 
chances are:

 - you wouldn't see any utility bills (hard-copy or online)
 - credit card transactions
 - banking transactions
 - insurance claims processing

or MANY many other types of "general business as we know it today"

  ***

In general,
  Robert would you PLEASE stop making conclusions from what types of jobs you 
see (especially jobs advertised on US-or-English-speaking websites) other than 
what jobs are advertised there.

I would certainly guess (and this is a PERSONAL guess) that getting a COBOL 
mainframe development job - searching via the web, might be difficult and might 
not lead to the HIGHEST paying job available via a web search.  But guessing 
what development is being done on mainframes (or via mainframe COBOL) from this 
limited database is simply an erroneous piece of logic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 58)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T14:21:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cinhki$2j17$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9g5nk0184nfu5rp4v30bnim8tjfe166qjp@4ax.com> <YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1pcuk09fc58birlncs7nmdg4b73hq7eo01@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:1pcuk09fc58birlncs7nmdg4b73hq7eo01@4ax.com...

> CSV (comma-delimited) is supported by STRING and UNSTRING. The others
> are not supported by standard Cobol (XML will be in 2006).

What's currently being termed "any-length elementary items" is also planned
for the 2008 (not 2006) standard; the current version of the proposal for
this feature is J4 document 04-0151 and may be found at the J4 website.
Current design plans include both PREFIXED BY and DELIMITED BY clauses, and
support way beyond that provided by STRING and UNSTRING.  The details of
this capability are still being refined and discussed.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T23:57:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u6ruk0lsk50970ood1d0mttmuv5uc3e6qp@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9g5nk0184nfu5rp4v30bnim8tjfe166qjp@4ax.com> <YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1pcuk09fc58birlncs7nmdg4b73hq7eo01@4ax.com> <cinhki$2j17$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Mon, 20 Sep 2004 14:21:22 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>this capability are still being refined and discussed.

On second thought, CSV is not supported by UNSTRING because UNSTRING
doesn't honor quotes. For example: 

unstring foo delimited by ',' into word(1), word(2) ...

Where foo contains

Common Latin quotation: "Veni, Vedi, Vici"

would emit four strings rather than two.
```

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:u6ruk0lsk50970ood1d0mttmuv5uc3e6qp@4ax.com...
> On Mon, 20 Sep 2004 14:21:22 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >What's currently being termed "any-length elementary items" is also
planned
> >for the 2008 (not 2006) standard; the current version of the proposal for
> >this feature is J4 document 04-0151 and may be found at the J4 website.
> >Current design plans include both PREFIXED BY and DELIMITED BY clauses,
and
> >support way beyond that provided by STRING and UNSTRING.  The details of
> >this capability are still being refined and discussed.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Common Latin quotation: "Veni, Vedi, Vici"

That's not a common Latin quotation.  In fact, I can't find "Vedi" in any
Latin dictionary available to me.

On the other hand, Julius Caesar delivered the quotation "Veni, vidi, vici"
after conquering Anatolia, and that particular quotation has enjoyed some
currency since then.

> would emit four strings rather than two.

Really?  I'd have expected three.  If I make the delimiter ", " instead of
"," I even get the three words snuggled up against the high-order boundaries
of the destinations!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Pardon the typo and misspelling. I should have said:

Common Latin quotation, "Veni, Vidi, Vici"

returns four strings rather than two.
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:nop0l0t94ocsj779audmjh2t688q8rau3l@4ax.com...
> Pardon the typo and misspelling. I should have said:
>
> Common Latin quotation, "Veni, Vidi, Vici"
>
> returns four strings rather than two.

... And no matter how I slice it, whether the delimiter is "," or ", ", I
get three strings, with either COBOL74 or COBOL85:

NEXT+ ...1....*....2....*....3....*....4....*....5....*....6....*....7..
000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000300 DATA DIVISION.
000400 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000500 77  FOO PIC X(16) VALUE "VENI, VIDI, VICI".
000600 01  A-TABLE.
000700     03  WORD PIC X(5) OCCURS 4.
000800 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000900 MAIN-PARAGRAPH.
001000     MOVE ALL "@" TO A-TABLE.
001100     UNSTRING FOO DELIMITED BY ", " INTO
001200          WORD (1), WORD (2), WORD (3), WORD (4).
001300     DISPLAY WORD (1), ";" WORD (2), ";",
001400             WORD (3), ";", WORD (4).
001500     STOP RUN.
#DISPLAY COMPLETE

C W COBOL74;E;
#COMPILING 9912
#ET=0.6 PT=0.1 IO=0.1
#RUNNING 9920
#9920 DISPLAY:VENI ;VIDI ;VICI ;@@@@@.
#ET=0.0 PT=0.0 IO=0.0

C W COBOL85;E;
#COMPILING 9932
#ET=1.5 PT=0.1 IO=0.1
#RUNNING 9933
#9933 DISPLAY:VENI ;VIDI ;VICI ;@@@@@.
#ET=0.0 PT=0.0 IO=0.0

What exactly is it that you're doing differently to get four elements of
WORD, rather than three,  affected by the stated "unstring foo deliminted by
',' into word(1) word(2)..."?  Under which rule do you believe this should
only affect the first two elements of WORD?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 63)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-21T21:52:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iv11l0p4ftj8b405nm68uto7e2707vr1d1@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1pcuk09fc58birlncs7nmdg4b73hq7eo01@4ax.com> <cinhki$2j17$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <u6ruk0lsk50970ood1d0mttmuv5uc3e6qp@4ax.com> <cipjt9$uq7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <nop0l0t94ocsj779audmjh2t688q8rau3l@4ax.com> <cipver$16gi$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Tue, 21 Sep 2004 12:29:31 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:nop0l0t94ocsj779audmjh2t688q8rau3l@4ax.com...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>get three strings, with either COBOL74 or COBOL85:

>What exactly is it that you're doing differently to get four elements of
>WORD, rather than three,  affected by the stated "unstring foo deliminted by
>',' into word(1) word(2)..."?  Under which rule do you believe this should
>only affect the first two elements of WORD?

The CSV files contains the entire line:

Common Latin quotation, "Veni, Vidi, Vici"

word(1) 'Common Latin quotation'
word(2) ' "Veni'
word(3) ' Vidi'
word(4) ' Vici"'

My point was that UNSTRING cannot be used to parse a CSV because
UNSTRING does not honor quotes. According to the rules of CSV,
anything inside quotes should be parsed to a single string .. with
quotes removed.  The same problem occurs when parsing a command line
with file name(s) containing spaces e.g. MyProg "My File.txt".

The fix would be this syntax:
UNSTRING ... DELIMITED BY PAIRED QUOTE OR ',' or ', '
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ UNSTRING, CSV and ASCII-RS, ASCII-US (was: If you were inventing CoBOL...)

_(thread depth: 64)_

- **From:** "L���ko Willms" <lw-nospam@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 2004-09-22T10:19:22+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i4g54a1.pminews@news.t-online.de>`
- **References:** `<YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1pcuk09fc58birlncs7nmdg4b73hq7eo01@4ax.com> <cinhki$2j17$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <u6ruk0lsk50970ood1d0mttmuv5uc3e6qp@4ax.com> <cipjt9$uq7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <nop0l0t94ocsj779audmjh2t688q8rau3l@4ax.com> <cipver$16gi$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <iv11l0p4ftj8b405nm68uto7e2707vr1d1@4ax.com>`

```
On Tue, 21 Sep 2004 21:52:09 GMT, Robert Wagner wrote:

>The CSV files contains the entire line:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>word(4) ' Vici"'

   Ah, that was misunderstood. 

>My point was that UNSTRING cannot be used to parse a CSV because
>UNSTRING does not honor quotes. According to the rules of CSV,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>UNSTRING ... DELIMITED BY PAIRED QUOTE OR ',' or ', '

   That would be a good enhancement to the COBOL Standard. 

   Let me state as a side remark, that I don't like this "industry standard"
of terminating records by CR/LF (DOS, Windows), LF (Unix) or CR (Mac); the
ASCII standard defined characters such as RS i.e. Record Separator, and for
subdivisions of a record such as in a CSV file, ASCII defines the US or Unit
Separator. *)

   It would be much better to use those non-printable characters instead of
commas or semicolons which then need some special treatment because they may
occur within a printable string. 

   But ... who am I do advise sanity when the whole world is going insane? 

Yours, 
L. Willms
------------------------------ all rights reserved ------------------------
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 62)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-21T21:48:05-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-8E74B4.21480521092004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9g5nk0184nfu5rp4v30bnim8tjfe166qjp@4ax.com> <YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1pcuk09fc58birlncs7nmdg4b73hq7eo01@4ax.com> <cinhki$2j17$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <u6ruk0lsk50970ood1d0mttmuv5uc3e6qp@4ax.com> <cipjt9$uq7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <nop0l0t94ocsj779audmjh2t688q8rau3l@4ax.com>`

```
In article <nop0l0t94ocsj779audmjh2t688q8rau3l@4ax.com>,
 Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:

> Pardon the typo and misspelling. I should have said:
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Only if used in a trivial example.  To parse a CSV using UNSTRING you 
have to account for the rules of the CSV, e.g:


   Parse-CSV-Row.

      Move CSV-Row to Row-Remainder

      Perform until Row-Remainder = spaces

         Unstring Row-Remainder
            delimited by ','
            into Next-Token Row-Remainder
         End-Unstring

         If Next-Token (1:1) = '"'
            Unstring Next-Token
               delimited by '"'
               into trash-1 Next-Token trash-2
            End-Unstring
         End-If

         Perform Emit-Next-Token      

         Move spaces to Next-Token

      End-Perform

      Exit.

Or something like that...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 63)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-22T03:47:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sqs1l0lmv2vi6101eoqhlun6g0i8ve762d@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1pcuk09fc58birlncs7nmdg4b73hq7eo01@4ax.com> <cinhki$2j17$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <u6ruk0lsk50970ood1d0mttmuv5uc3e6qp@4ax.com> <cipjt9$uq7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <nop0l0t94ocsj779audmjh2t688q8rau3l@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-8E74B4.21480521092004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
On Tue, 21 Sep 2004 21:48:05 -0400, Joe Zitzelberger
<joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

>In article <nop0l0t94ocsj779audmjh2t688q8rau3l@4ax.com>,
> Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>have to account for the rules of the CSV, e.g:

Not at all close.

>   Parse-CSV-Row.
>      Move CSV-Row to Row-Remainder
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>         End-Unstring

Unstring doesn't clear receiving fields. You have junk at the end of
Row-Remainder and junk in Next-Token on the first iteration.

>         If Next-Token (1:1) = '"'
>            Unstring Next-Token
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>         End-If

That strips the leading quote while leaving junk in the last byte.
What about the rest of the string inside quotes?

>         Perform Emit-Next-Token      
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>      Exit.

You don't write Exit at the end of a paragraph.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 64)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-22T12:37:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cisk9j$30rs$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1pcuk09fc58birlncs7nmdg4b73hq7eo01@4ax.com> <cinhki$2j17$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <u6ruk0lsk50970ood1d0mttmuv5uc3e6qp@4ax.com> <cipjt9$uq7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <nop0l0t94ocsj779audmjh2t688q8rau3l@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-8E74B4.21480521092004@knology.usenetserver.com> <sqs1l0lmv2vi6101eoqhlun6g0i8ve762d@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:sqs1l0lmv2vi6101eoqhlun6g0i8ve762d@4ax.com...

> >         Unstring Row-Remainder
> >            delimited by ','
> >            into Next-Token Row-Remainder
> >         End-Unstring

ISO/IEC 1989:2002, page 548, 14.8.44, UNSTRING statement, general rule 18
says the results are undefined.  This is explicitly corroborated on page
695, Undefined language element list, item 65.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(thread depth: 63)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-22T04:13:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vst1l01fre1nu9dq6tvkh7pbsshpebjlem@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <1pcuk09fc58birlncs7nmdg4b73hq7eo01@4ax.com> <cinhki$2j17$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <u6ruk0lsk50970ood1d0mttmuv5uc3e6qp@4ax.com> <cipjt9$uq7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <nop0l0t94ocsj779audmjh2t688q8rau3l@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-8E74B4.21480521092004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
On Tue, 21 Sep 2004 21:48:05 -0400, Joe Zitzelberger
<joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

>   Parse-CSV-Row.
>      Move CSV-Row to Row-Remainder
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>Or something like that...

Parse-CSV-Row.
move 1 to CSV-Pointer
perform until CSV-Pointer greater than length(CSV-Row)
    move spaces to Next-Token 
    if  CSV-Row (CSV-Pointer:1) = '"' or "'"
        add 1 to CSV-Pointer
        unstring CSV-Row delimited by CSV-Row(CSV-Pointer-1:1)
            into Next-Token with pointer CSV-Pointer
        unstring CSV-Row delimited by ', ' or ',' 
            into trash with pointer CSV-Pointer
   else
        unstring CSV-Row delimited by ', ' or ','
            into Next-Token with pointer CSV-Pointer
   end-if
   perform Emit-Next-Token
end-perform.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ UNSTRINGing a CSV line (was: If you were inventing CoBOL...)

_(thread depth: 64)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-09-23T11:00:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9HPy3$tPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-8E74B4.21480521092004@knology.usenetserver.com> <vst1l01fre1nu9dq6tvkh7pbsshpebjlem@4ax.com>`

```
.    Am  22.09.04
schrieb  robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests (Robert Wagner)
    auf  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  vst1l01fre1nu9dq6tvkh7pbsshpebjlem@4ax.com
  ueber  Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

>> Or something like that...

RW> Parse-CSV-Row.
RW> move 1 to CSV-Pointer
RW> perform until CSV-Pointer greater than length(CSV-Row)
RW>     move spaces to Next-Token

        move zero to item-number

RW>     if  CSV-Row (CSV-Pointer:1) = '"' or "'"
RW>         add 1 to CSV-Pointer
RW>         unstring CSV-Row
RW>             delimited by CSV-Row(CSV-Pointer-1:1)

*>   I would change "Next-Token" to a table item:

RW>             into Next-Token
                into CSV-item(item-number)

*>   and add TALLYING:
                   TALLYING IN item-number

RW>             with pointer CSV-Pointer
RW>         unstring CSV-Row delimited by ', ' or ','
RW>             into trash with pointer CSV-Pointer
RW>    else
RW>         unstring CSV-Row
RW>             delimited by ', ' or ','
                into CSV-item(item-number)
                   tallying in item-number
RW>             with pointer CSV-Pointer
RW>    end-if
RW> end-perform.

   Also, the delimiter should be made into a variable "CSV-delimiter"  
e.g., so that it can be set to comma, semikolon, TAB, US, or whatever.

   I also think one should use someting like

    INSPECT CSV-item[CSV-Pointer]
       TALLYING CSV-Pointer FOR LEADING SPACES CSV-delimiter

   to skip over the leading spaces of each item.

   Wouldn't it be possible to implement this as an object CLASS?


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de

Herrschaft anstrebt."            - Leo Trotzki         (27. Januar 1932)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: UNSTRINGing a CSV line (was: If you were inventing CoBOL...)

_(thread depth: 65)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-23T15:30:52+00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: UNSTRINGing a CSV line

_(thread depth: 68)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-09-23T23:31:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9HU3-FyPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <9HQ1QY0eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <j4e6l01ait5eplet99sk7jar962lglatrv@4ax.com>`

```
.     Am  23.09.04
 schrieb  robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests (Robert Wagner)
     bei  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
      in  j4e6l01ait5eplet99sk7jar962lglatrv@4ax.com
   ueber  Re: UNSTRINGing a CSV line

>> RW>>>         unstring CSV-Row
>> RW>>>             delimited by CSV-Row(CSV-Pointer-1:1)
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>                   TALLYING IN item-number

RW> Should be: TALLYING IN item-length

   No, you are confusing TALLYING IN and COUNT IN

   TALLYING counts the number of items moved to CSV-item(item-number)  
(in this example), while COUNT IN counts the number of characters  
examined for the single move, i.e. the length of Next-Token.

   like this:

   UNSTRING CSV-Line
      DELIMITED BY CSV-Line[CSV-Pointer - 1:1]
        INTO CSV-item-text(item-number)
           COUNT IN CSV-item-length(item-number)
        TALLYING IN item-number


   where CSV-item-text and CSV-item-length would be two items  
subordinate to a group item with an OCCURS clause long enough for a  
large number of items, e.g. like this:

   01 CSV-item-table.
      02 CSV-item OCCURS 200 TIMES.
         03 CSV-item-length  PIC 9(04) COMP.
         03 CSV-item-text    PIC X(256).




Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

"Die Interessen der Nation lassen sich nicht anders formulieren als unter
dem Gesichtspunkt der herrschenden Klasse oder der Klasse, die die
Herrschaft anstrebt."            - Leo Trotzki         (27. Januar 1932)
```

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> >   Wouldn't it be possible to implement this as an object CLASS?
> 
> Yes. In that case, we're discussing code inside the unpack method.

For which I would not use UNSTRING at all for CSV data. If it is in a
callable routine or a class there is no point in trying to get
UNSTRING to do things that it is unsuitable for, it is easier and more
flexible to just step through the data.  It also allows the processing
of 'escape' codes where these are required.
```
