- **References:** `<unhrj05jdogr3n5d06lgnra6u135o4aggp@4ax.com> <Rk22d.439344$gE.57805@pd7tw3no> <ahcjk05jobfdngjd08hvj1i86v78sii7qt@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409161457.7aeb0e76@posting.google.com> <cpekk01lv8pnt26v38npruklf6nf3oh0cl@4ax.com> <ass2d.28238$%S.7408@pd7tw2no>`

```
On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 03:01:26 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>>Rubbish. One generalized program can do it all.
>>  
>OK ! I really like your programming style. Now put your money where your 
>mouth is.

I did two months ago, and again in reply to Richard (same thread).

> Bearing in mind the questioner is talking Procedural, and 
>before you commit, take a look at the RM/Manuals on-line - make sure it 
>fits Liant - now let us see you go for it !  

Every Cobol compiler can read and write 1-byte records.

>Also remember, he doesn't 
>want to bugger around reformatting files, conversions or whatever. He 
>hasn't said, but I'll say it for him, while he's looking for a vanilla 
>answer, like it or not he has to change the coding of all his Business 
>programs that would be calling your FileProgram

We're all talking about slightly different things here. My and
Richard's programs copy a file,  converting fields to display. That
plus a text editor and reverse conversion is a crude way to update a
file. 

My approach could be (and has been) enhanced to produce a General File
Maintenance Routine. Writing it again is probably unnecessary because
others have done it better. Three that I'm familiar with are Toad
($60), Msquery (comes with Excel) and File-Aid for DB2 (n/a). There
are dozens more. Add an ODBC driver and you're in business. 

>No short-cuts now. You did say *ALL* - so ISAM, Relative, Sequential and 
>LineSequential.

Liant has an ODBC driver for all their formats.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is a General File Maintenance Routine possible?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-17T21:07:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409172007.6ddc9b6a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<unhrj05jdogr3n5d06lgnra6u135o4aggp@4ax.com> <Rk22d.439344$gE.57805@pd7tw3no> <ahcjk05jobfdngjd08hvj1i86v78sii7qt@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409161457.7aeb0e76@posting.google.com> <cpekk01lv8pnt26v38npruklf6nf3oh0cl@4ax.com> <ass2d.28238$%S.7408@pd7tw2no> <c9qlk0h8qvsrjb1q2205rl8n9kfc08ta3n@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

I'll add what I think you really meant:

> Every Cobol compiler can [compile programs that] read and write 1-byte records.

This may be superficially true, though there may be an environment
where there is a minimum record size.  However, you are assuming that
using a one byte (or some other size) is actually useful in
environments that you have never used.

With MF C-ISAM or Level II ISAM file formats the data files are just
fixed length blocks with CR/LF or x00/LF terminators and opening the
data file as a 1 byte or some other fixed size gives access to the raw
bytes without worrying about actual file parameters.  I have actually
done this, though I used 128 byte and a sliding window.

Some environments that I know of in which this is _NOT_ useful:

   Microfocus FILETYPE"3" (default for DOS/Windows)
   MicroFocus FILETYPE"5" or "6" (Btrieve)
   Microfocus DATACOMPRESS
   Fujitsu ISAM

The data file of these is _NOT_ just contiguous fixed length records.
   
It will also not be useful where sequential files are not just
consecutive blocks of fixed record size, for example if it uses a file
header or record headers/trailers. The only environment that know of
that would fail on this count is MF Cobol on DRX, but it is obsolete. 
Others may also do this, probably on mainframes.

Your program may well work with MF and C-ISAM, but once again you
extrapolate this single data point to _every_ in a completely
incompetent manner.

The requirement was for RM.  I haven't worked with RM files for some
years, in fact since the mid-80s.  I recall that pre '85 was
relatively simple, much like Level II format.  RM Cobol '85 changed
the format completely.  From memory it was more like FILETYPE"3" with
record headers.

> My approach could be (and has been) enhanced to produce a General File
> Maintenance Routine.

It may even work - in a very limited way with the limited systems that
you have used.  Not with RM though, or Fujitsu, or MF using default
Windows options.

> Writing it again is probably unnecessary because others have done it better.

That is just deflection.  You claimed you could do it (the
requirement) all in one program because you were a 'Real Programmer'. 
I know that you can't, or at least can't in a portable Cobol way that
will meet the requirement.

If you want to be thought of as a passably competent programmer by
others you will stop making unsupportable claims about what you think
you can do.

Oh, yes, and you will actually read the requirements before sounding
off about how clever your solution is when it is not even close to
doing the actual job.
```

```
On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 01:16:46 GMT, Robert Wagner
<robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:

>On 16 Sep 2004 16:11:07 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
>
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>Rubbish. One generalized program can do it all.
Can you please quote a COBOL compiler that given the following
structure used when the file is first created
           SELECT  FICH-CLI  ASSIGN TO RANDOM F-CLI
                             ORGANIZATION    INDEXED
                             ACCESS MODE     DYNAMIC
                             RECORD KEY      KEY-CLI-1
                             ALTERNATE RECORD KEY-CLI-2
                             FILE STATUS IS  FILE-STATUS.
..........
       FD FICH-CLI
              LABEL RECORDS STANDARD.
       01 REG-CLI.
      *CONSTITUICAO DO FICHEIRO DE CLIENTES PARA DESPACHANTES
          05  KEY-CLI-2.
              10  CL-TIPO1    PIC  9.
              10  CL-NOME     PIC  X(45).
              10  KEY-CLI-1.
                  15  CL-TIPO PIC  9.
                  15  CL-NUM  PIC  9(6)     COMP.
          05  DES-CLI.
              10  CL-MORA     PIC  X(45).
              10  CL-CPOST    PIC  9(4)     COMP.
              10  CL-CONTRIB  PIC  X(10).
              10  FILLER      PIC  X(40).

then allows a program with this changed definition
           SELECT  FICH-CLI  ASSIGN TO RANDOM F-CLI
                             ORGANIZATION    INDEXED
                             ACCESS MODE     DYNAMIC
                             RECORD KEY      KEY-CLI-1
                             FILE STATUS IS  FILE-STATUS.
..........
       FD FICH-CLI
              LABEL RECORDS STANDARD.
       01 REG-CLI.
      *CONSTITUICAO DO FICHEIRO DE CLIENTES PARA DESPACHANTES
          05  KEY-CLI-2.
              10  CL-TIPO1    PIC  9.
              10  CL-NOME     PIC  X(45).
              10  KEY-CLI-1.
                  15  CL-TIPO PIC  9.
                  15  CL-NUM  PIC  9(6)     COMP.
          05  DES-CLI.
              10  CL-MORA     PIC  X(45).
              10  CL-CPOST    PIC  9(4)     COMP.
              10  CL-CONTRIB  PIC  X(10).
              10  FILLER      PIC  X(40).
to sucessefuly open and process the file.


>
>>I have actually done this sort of thing.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>without creating 70 'programs'.



Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

```
On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 07:47:24 +0100, Frederico Fonseca
<real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com> wrote:


>Can you please quote a COBOL compiler that given the following
>structure used when the file is first created
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>                             FILE STATUS IS  FILE-STATUS.

>then allows a program with this changed definition
>           SELECT  FICH-CLI  ASSIGN TO RANDOM F-CLI
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>                             FILE STATUS IS  FILE-STATUS.

>to sucessefuly open and process the file.

You're right. Alternate keys cannot be omitted because updates would
mess up indexes. A utility would have to include them when
constructing a file definition at execution time. They are probably
in the file header. If not, it would have to parse source code.
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-17T17:36:05+01:00

- **References:** `<unhrj05jdogr3n5d06lgnra6u135o4aggp@4ax.com> <Rk22d.439344$gE.57805@pd7tw3no> <ahcjk05jobfdngjd08hvj1i86v78sii7qt@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409161457.7aeb0e76@posting.google.com> <cpekk01lv8pnt26v38npruklf6nf3oh0cl@4ax.com> <qq1lk0tv5dmsgsfns62v5sr1v4f4r6n46r@4ax.com> <5urlk01s0hf0g3ummigickib27g1htjcp6@4ax.com>`

```
On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 15:00:43 GMT, Robert Wagner
<robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:

>On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 07:47:24 +0100, Frederico Fonseca
><real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com> wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>in the file header. If not, it would have to parse source code.
I am right, but you missed the point.

My change code very well have been increasing the size of the key.
The final effect would be the same. e.g. the program would not be able
to process the file because the file definition on the program did not
mach the physical file definition (as stored on the file itself.)
And this is the real problem.
In order to have a generic program reading an indexed file (as
indexed, not as a binary 1 byte file), the select and the file
definition would need to be the same in ALL files.
This would be a plain stupid thing to do.



Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-17T17:11:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<886mk0ptoflajt9lmqc6nk78e70de0jhe1@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<unhrj05jdogr3n5d06lgnra6u135o4aggp@4ax.com> <Rk22d.439344$gE.57805@pd7tw3no> <ahcjk05jobfdngjd08hvj1i86v78sii7qt@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409161457.7aeb0e76@posting.google.com> <cpekk01lv8pnt26v38npruklf6nf3oh0cl@4ax.com> <qq1lk0tv5dmsgsfns62v5sr1v4f4r6n46r@4ax.com> <5urlk01s0hf0g3ummigickib27g1htjcp6@4ax.com> <1d4mk01g1p5ujsdg46dlih9ce7c952jco6@4ax.com>`

```
On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 17:36:05 +0100, Frederico Fonseca
<real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com> wrote:

>On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 15:00:43 GMT, Robert Wagner
><robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>>in the file header. If not, it would have to parse source code.

>I am right, but you missed the point.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>And this is the real problem.

You missed my point. You think the only way to communicate information
about keys is via SELECT. It is not. One can construct a file
description block at execution time before opening the file. 

Given that the information about keys is in the file header, the
program can read it using a random FD and put it into the file
description before opening the indexed FD.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is a General File Maintenance Routine possible?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-09-18T04:58:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MfP2d.37616$%S.31980@pd7tw2no>`

- **References:** `<unhrj05jdogr3n5d06lgnra6u135o4aggp@4ax.com> <Rk22d.439344$gE.57805@pd7tw3no> <ahcjk05jobfdngjd08hvj1i86v78sii7qt@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409161457.7aeb0e76@posting.google.com> <cpekk01lv8pnt26v38npruklf6nf3oh0cl@4ax.com> <qq1lk0tv5dmsgsfns62v5sr1v4f4r6n46r@4ax.com> <5urlk01s0hf0g3ummigickib27g1htjcp6@4ax.com> <1d4mk01g1p5ujsdg46dlih9ce7c952jco6@4ax.com> <886mk0ptoflajt9lmqc6nk78e70de0jhe1@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 17:36:05 +0100, Frederico Fonseca
><real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com> wrote:
…[64 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I'm not directly interested, having since made the switch to DBs and SQL 
- but I think my OO file templates may be close to what you are saying. 
Care to spell out the above for a 'Procedural' with a bit of code ?

To avoid individual developers having to re-invent the wheel I did 
submit a paper to J4 - Could we perhaps have reference modification or 
'offsets' if you like, specified for ISAM PrimeKeys and Alternate Keys. 
That went down the tubes with a lot of other stuff not considered worth 
the effort.

Back to another point you made about 'Mainframes arrogantly imposing on 
us PC-ers'. For sure, and I made the point some years ago - there should 
be two Standards - Mainframe and PCs. Once I wrote something to the 
effect, be nice if we had those F/J and M/F COBOL_FILE routines in the 
Standard. Howard,  (Mr. Mainframer - "You may need them but I don't")..

Unfortunately 'imposition' is the real world - we PC-ers are still the 
minority and are likely to remain so for COBOL for a long time. Now I 
didn't dream this up, because I have never looked at an ISO(WG4) 
document - in fact they are probably 'Confidential'. So I found 
following in an ANSI(J4) document - subsequent to COBOL 2002 - take the 
current wish-list and 'prioritize'. Chuck says "Not so. Prioritizing was 
done by ISO(WG4)".

So ask yourself who, using PCs, are the ISO members from the European 
countries and N. America ? Not too many - time involvement and cost. So 
who rules - the mainframes. Therefore currently one of J4's top 
priorities is Dynamic Tables, seen as an enhancement for mainframes, but 
which also can certainly be used on PCs. Much replication of what is 
available in Collections. Who cares about OO and Collections ? IBM 
doesn't  :-

Class-id.   MyIBMClass  inherits from JavaBase.
 
Now to be fair, sort of running parallel to Dynamic Tables *are* 
Collections - first written up (some six month's work, by my reckoning) 
By Aussie Russell Clarke back in '96. It went into a cryogenic state 
until Bill K. "resurrected* it in 2001. So from '96 until to date 2003 - 
oh hummm.

I told you that you were a *newbie* when it came to the Standards.

Mainframes *still* rule.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is a General File Maintenance Routine possible?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-17T18:50:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_lG2d.1534267$6p.257572@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<unhrj05jdogr3n5d06lgnra6u135o4aggp@4ax.com> <Rk22d.439344$gE.57805@pd7tw3no> <ahcjk05jobfdngjd08hvj1i86v78sii7qt@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409161457.7aeb0e76@posting.google.com> <cpekk01lv8pnt26v38npruklf6nf3oh0cl@4ax.com> <qq1lk0tv5dmsgsfns62v5sr1v4f4r6n46r@4ax.com> <5urlk01s0hf0g3ummigickib27g1htjcp6@4ax.com>`

```
Actually, Robert

The IBM mainframe compilers are "notorious" for the fact that you don't need the 
exact same Alternate Record Key descriptions when doing Updates as you do when 
creating the files.

I *know* that you can create a file with 2 alternate keys - and then update (or 
read) the file with only one alternate key.  I have not tried creating it with 1 
alternate key - and then updating it with 0 alternate keys - but it wouldn't 
surprise me if this were accepted.

Originally, in the '85 Standard this would have caused a FS=39 - but later on it 
was "clarified" that it is up to each vendor WHICH "fixed file attributes" were 
to be checked to cause a FS=39.

I believe (but won't swear to it) that if one uses the LATEST Micro Focus MFEEE 
product, that this "behavior" has been emulated by MF - due to "user demand" 
*not* to check alternate keys for FS=39.
```

```
On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 18:50:34 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>I believe (but won't swear to it) that if one uses the LATEST Micro Focus MFEEE 
>product, that this "behavior" has been emulated by MF - due to "user demand" 
>*not* to check alternate keys for FS=39.

That's been in EXTFH for some time. See the KEYCHECK parameter in File
Handling/Configuration. The default is on.

"Specifies whether the File Handler checks that the key definition
defined in your application matches that of the indexed file you are
opening."
```

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> >I would start by defining an interface which includes a whole bunch of
> >standardised fields and has enough of them to hold the data of every
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> the rare event it doesn't exist, create one.

Because, if you had read the message, it is a standardised interface
between the maintenance program and the dozens of individual files. 
It will be used in the CALL filehandler USING ...

'Parsing a schema' doesn't necessarily achieve the interfacing, but
please do provide a solution if you wish.
 
> >Now you will need 200 programs that will read, rewrite, etc and will
> >move data between the record layout and the interface, one for each
> >file.
> 
> Rubbish. One generalized program can do it all.

That is something that you may wish to demonstrate.

> >I have actually done this sort of thing.
> 
> I have no doubt. Automated cut-and-paste programming at its worst.

Actually, at its best.  Program generators can be completely automated
using a make file so that it becomes a completely fix and forget. 
Change the fd copybook and the program is regenerated.

> >Now actually I wrote the template processor in Python because the
> >whole thing is about 20 lines of code in that language, but it could
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> without creating 70 'programs'.

What are you suggesting ? One program with 70 fd copybooks ?  Perhaps
you could show some code about how you would do it.

<shrug> I code up one template to do one simple thing and create a set
of parameters to reproduce that as many times as I need.  I don't need
to be a self-appointed 'Real Programmer', I can do it with little
effort and create simple code that works and that everyone can
understand.

The issue is productivity.  I can do it this way in 


Here is a simplified sample of a template:

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.                     %(programid)s.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT IN-File ASSIGN In-Name
           ORGANIZATION SEQUENTIAL
           FILE STATUS File-Status
           .
           COPY "%(filesa)s".
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  IN-File.
       01  IN-Record.
           03  IN-Data                 PIC X(%(recordsize)s).
           03  IN-CRLF                 PIC XX.
           COPY "%(filefd)s".
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  File-Status.
           03  FS1                     PIC X.
           03  FS2                     PIC X.
       01  IN-Name                     PIC X(80).
       01  %(filename)s                PIC X(80).
       01  In-Count                    PIC S9(8).
       01  Out-Count                   PIC S9(8).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
           MOVE ZERO           TO In-Count
                                  Out-Count
           DISPLAY "Program: %(programid)s"
           DISPLAY "Reading: %(inname)s"
           DISPLAY "Writing: %(outname)s"
           MOVE "%(inname)s"  TO IN-Name
           OPEN INPUT IN-File
           IF ( FS1 NOT = ZERO )
               DISPLAY "INPUT %(inname)s File failed"
               STOP RUN
           END-IF
           MOVE "%(outname)s"  TO %(filename)s
           OPEN OUTPUT %(outfile)s
           ...

Here is the Python code to create the programs, basically the program
sorts out the command parameters and then actions the convgen routine
which does all the work.

def convgen(configfile="convgen.cfg", testing="n"):

    config    = ConfigParser.ConfigParser()
    config.read(configfile)

    section   = 'generate'
    programs = config.options(section)

    for (program) in programs:

        tempname = config.get(section, program)
        print "Generating: %s from: %s" % (program, tempname)

        if ( config.has_section(program) ):
            params = config.options(program)
            paramblock = {}
            for (paramname) in params:
                paramvalue = config.get(program, paramname)
                if ( testing == "y" ):
                    print "%s=%s" % (paramname, paramvalue)
                paramblock[paramname] = paramvalue

            try:
                template = open(tempname, "r")
            except:
                print "Template: %s failed to open" % (tempname)
            else:
                text = template.read()
                template.close()

                generated = text % (paramblock)

                cobol = open(program, "w")
                cobol.write(generated)
                cobol.close()

That's it.  The whole template -> program is done by "text %
(paramblock)"
           
Here's a simple configuration file for one program. 

[generate]
prodconv.cbl=convgen.tem

[prodconv.cbl]
programid=prodconv
inname=TPROD.DAT
recordsize=1022
filesa=prod.sa
filefd=prod.fd
outfile=Prod
filename=PFName
outname=TPROD.ISM
outrecord=PRec
```

- **References:** `<unhrj05jdogr3n5d06lgnra6u135o4aggp@4ax.com> <Rk22d.439344$gE.57805@pd7tw3no> <ahcjk05jobfdngjd08hvj1i86v78sii7qt@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409161457.7aeb0e76@posting.google.com> <cpekk01lv8pnt26v38npruklf6nf3oh0cl@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409170010.39aad29c@posting.google.com>`

```
On 17 Sep 2004 01:10:50 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>please do provide a solution if you wish.

Two months ago I posted cob2csv.cbl, which parsed a copybook into a
structure in memory, a schema, then converted any file from sequential
to comma-delimited. A sample input looked like this:

        01  WWICTLG.
            10 CTLGOP-CO-NO              PIC X(3).
            10 CTLGCONTROL-SET-SEQ-NO    PIC S9(9) USAGE COMP.
            10 CTLGINVOICE-SEQ-NO        PIC S9(4) USAGE COMP.
            10 CTLGAP-CNTRL-ENTITY-CD    PIC X(4).
            10 CTLGINVOICE-TYPE-CD       PIC X(2).
            10 CTLGLEGACY-VENDOR-NO      PIC X(10).
            10 CTLGAP-VENDOR-NO          PIC X(30).
            10 CTLGAP-PO-NO              PIC X(30).
            10 CTLGAP-INVOICE-NO         PIC X(30).
            10 CTLGINVOICE-DT            PIC X(10).
            10 CTLGTOTAL-INVOICE-AM      PIC S9(11)V9(2) USAGE COMP-3.
            10 CTLGTOTAL-MERCH-AM        PIC S9(11)V99   USAGE COMP-3.
            10 CTLGTERMS-CD              PIC XX.
            10 CTLGCARRIER-CD            PIC X(10).
            10 CTLGCARRIER-PRO-NO        PIC X(10).
            10 CTLGSHIP-DT               PIC X(10).
            10 CTLGSHIP-VIA-CD           PIC X(3).
            10 CTLGSHIP-LOC-NO           PIC X(3).
            10 CTLGSHIP-WT               PIC X(10).
            10 x redefines CTLGSHIP-WT   PIC X(10).
            10 CTLGSHIP-UNITS-DC         PIC X(10).
            10 CTLGSHIP-TY               PIC X(2).

The normalized description looked like this:

     Lvl Name                           Pos Len Typ Occ
    1 10 ctlgop-co-no                     1   3   x   1
    2 10 ctlgcontrol-set-seq-no           4   4   b   1
    3 10 ctlginvoice-seq-no               8   2   b   1
    4 10 ctlgap-cntrl-entity-cd          10   4   x   1
    5 10 ctlginvoice-type-cd             14   2   x   1
    6 10 ctlglegacy-vendor-no            16  10   x   1
    7 10 ctlgap-vendor-no                26  30   x   1
    8 10 ctlgap-po-no                    56  30   x   1
    9 10 ctlgap-invoice-no               86  30   x   1
   10 10 ctlginvoice-dt                 116  10   x   1
   11 10 ctlgtotal-invoice-am           126   7 2 p   1
   12 10 ctlgtotal-merch-am             133   7 2 p   1
   13 10 ctlgterms-cd                   140   2   x   1
   14 10 ctlgcarrier-cd                 142  10   x   1
   15 10 ctlgcarrier-pro-no             152  10   x   1
   16 10 ctlgship-dt                    162  10   x   1
   17 10 ctlgship-via-cd                172   3   x   1
   18 10 ctlgship-loc-no                175   3   x   1
   19 10 ctlgship-wt                    178  10   x   1
   20 10 ctlgship-units-dc              188  10   x   1
   21 10 ctlgship-ty                    198   2   x   1
         Record length                  199
 
>> >Now you will need 200 programs that will read, rewrite, etc and will
>> >move data between the record layout and the interface, one for each
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>That is something that you may wish to demonstrate.

A generalized program cannot rely on the compiler to setup its file IO
at compile time; it must do its own setup at execution time. I've used
three approaches. One sets up a file description block at execution
time and calls Cobol's file system. That's especially easy with Micro
Focus because its EXTFH interface is well documented. With other
compilers, you have to figure it out by looking at generated code.
Write a program containing one of each file type, fixed and variable,
and procedural code to do every possible operation. Generated code
will reveal the contents of the file descriptor and call interface. 

A second approach uses ODBC drivers, dynamic SQL and an external
schema defining record layouts. Many people are unaware there are ODBC
drivers for flat files (cames standard with Windows) and many Cobol
indexed file formats.

A third approach is lower level than the first two. Defining a random
file with record length of one byte gives the program access to every
byte in the file. That's now cob2csv does it.

 select any-file assign to file-name
     organization is relative
     access is random
     relative key is relative-key
     file status is file-status.

fd  any-file.
01  file-byte pic x.

Read a record like this:

perform varying record-byte from 1 by 1 until record-byte > record-len
     read any-file
    move file-byte to record-byte (record-len)
    add 1 to relative-key
end-perform

For speed in a real-world program, I'd have two FDs -- one with
512-byte records (size of a disk sector) and the other with 1-byte
records, used to read the last partial sector. That's a technical
improvement; concept is the same.

The third approach may or may not work for indexed files. It does for
Micro Focus CISAM, the default form on Unix. And it does for indexed
copied to record sequential via the compiler-supplied tool. 

> Program generators can be completely automated
>using a make file so that it becomes a completely fix and forget. 
>Change the fd copybook and the program is regenerated.

Except when you forget to run the make file after changing the
copybook. Or you're in a sweltering mine shaft. 

>What are you suggesting ? One program with 70 fd copybooks ?  Perhaps
>you could show some code about how you would do it.

Cob2csv has code handling individual packed and binary fields. Its
field normalizations looks like this:

  05  input-number                   pic s9(15)
  05   redefines input-number
      10  input-number-byte occurs 15 pic  x(01)

  05  input-binary           comp-5  pic s9(18)
  05   redefines input-binary
      10  input-binary-byte occurs 8 pic  x(01)

  05  input-packed    packed-decimal pic s9(15)
  05   redefines input-packed.
      10  input-packed-byte occurs 8 pic  x(01)

 convert-number.
   move zero to input-number
   compute t = length of input-number - layout-length (l) + 1
   perform layout-length (l) times
       move input-byte (i) to input-number-byte (t)
       add 1 to i, t
   end-perform
   move input-number to work-number

 convert-binary.
   move zero to input-binary
   if  input-byte (i) greater than x'7F'
       move -1 to input-binary
   end-if
   compute t = length of input-binary - layout-length (l) + 1
   perform layout-length (l) times
       move input-byte (i) to input-binary-byte (t)
       add 1 to i, t
   end-perform
   move input-binary to work-number

 convert-packed.
   move zero to input-packed
   compute t = length of input-packed - layout-length (l) + 1
   perform layout-length (l) times
       move input-byte (i) to input-packed-byte (t)
       add 1 to i, t
   end-perform
   move input-packed to work-number

>Here is a simplified sample of a template:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>           COPY "%(filefd)s".

Not shown above is a second FD for the output file. All 70 FDs were
MANUALLY translated, and SELECTs were manually created. That's not an
automated solution. 

My conversion to csv could easily be changed to write fixed-length
display fields. The user would need only three things: the input file,
the copybook and my executable. When a new file is discovered, it will
work there too .. without updating a make file, manually creating two
copybooks and running a generate/compile.

What if the user is not a Cobol programmer? I could send him or her
one executable. You'd have to send a compiler, a make utility, a
Python interpreter, and a document explaining how to write copybooks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is a General File Maintenance Routine possible?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-17T12:46:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409171146.5ec5d280@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<unhrj05jdogr3n5d06lgnra6u135o4aggp@4ax.com> <Rk22d.439344$gE.57805@pd7tw3no> <ahcjk05jobfdngjd08hvj1i86v78sii7qt@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409161457.7aeb0e76@posting.google.com> <cpekk01lv8pnt26v38npruklf6nf3oh0cl@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409170010.39aad29c@posting.google.com> <dqklk017ki28a3fhb6cv7f8hja1e4tiapp@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> Two months ago I posted cob2csv.cbl, which parsed a copybook into a
> structure in memory, a schema, then converted any file from sequential
> to comma-delimited. A sample input looked like this:

> >That is something that you may wish to demonstrate.
 
> A generalized program cannot rely on the compiler to setup its file IO
> at compile time; it must do its own setup at execution time. I've used
> three approaches. One sets up a file description block at execution
> time and calls Cobol's file system. That's especially easy with Micro
> Focus because its EXTFH interface is well documented. 

The MF EXTFH was specifically implemented to give such flexability,
yes. Had the specification been for MF Cobol I may have suggested
using this.  Unfortunately the requirement was quite clearly for RM
Cobol.

> With other
> compilers, you have to figure it out by looking at generated code.
> Write a program containing one of each file type, fixed and variable,
> and procedural code to do every possible operation. Generated code
> will reveal the contents of the file descriptor and call interface. 

RM Cobol isn't 'generated code', it is byte code (last time I looked).
This is quite easy to decode, but only if you have the full
description of the byte code engine.
 
> A second approach uses ODBC drivers, dynamic SQL and an external
> schema defining record layouts. Many people are unaware there are ODBC
> drivers for flat files (cames standard with Windows) and many Cobol
> indexed file formats.

Just two little points you may have overlooked.  First it clearly
specified SCO Unix (probably OpenServer rather than UnixWare), and
secondly it required no end user cost.
 
> A third approach is lower level than the first two. Defining a random
> file with record length of one byte gives the program access to every
> byte in the file. That's now cob2csv does it.
 
>  select any-file assign to file-name
>      organization is relative
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> end-perform

Just two little, trivial really, problems. First your 'solution' may
work with C-ISAM files which are essentially fixed length data records
in the data file, but this is for RM.  Once again you take a single
data point and extrapolate without understanding that this doesn't
work.

The second problem is writing back into the ISAM file, a point you
have completely failed to cover and which is the essence of the whole
issue, or of both issues actually.  You have now travelled down a
complete dead-end.  Explain how you will insert a record and update
the index on an RM ISAM file, or a Fujitsu one.  Will you use your 1
byte file to fiddle the index ?

> The third approach may or may not work for indexed files. It does for
> Micro Focus CISAM, the default form on Unix. 

It is the default for _MicroFocus_ on Unix.

> And it does for indexed
> copied to record sequential via the compiler-supplied tool. 

> Except when you forget to run the make file after changing the
> copybook.

Well, actually it would be the same makefile as is used for every
other program in the system.  So if you forget to run the make then
the programs that use the file will also not be recompiled.

> Or you're in a sweltering mine shaft. 

I am not sure that the development system is down the mine, or on any
customer machine, I think that was also part of the requirements
statement.

> >What are you suggesting ? One program with 70 fd copybooks ?  Perhaps
> >you could show some code about how you would do it.
> 
> Cob2csv has code ...

Well you have certainly tried to show how clever you can be, it is
just a shame that all that code solves some other problems entirely
and doesn't address any of the problem at hand.

Specifically you haven't shown how you would write the data back to,
say, an RM ISAM file on SCO Unix.

> Not shown above is a second FD for the output file. All 70 FDs were
> MANUALLY translated, and SELECTs were manually created. That's not an
> automated solution. 

This was the file conversion from C-ISAM to Fujitsu.  The FDs were not
required to have anything at all done with them.  There was no 'manual
creation' they already existed.  Certainly for the maintenance
programs the FD would need to be parsed to create the MOVEs to the
interface and back.

> My conversion to csv could easily be changed to write fixed-length
> display fields. The user would need only three things: the input file,
> the copybook and my executable. When a new file is discovered, it will
> work there too .. without updating a make file, manually creating two
> copybooks and running a generate/compile.

And if a CSV was an appropriate result we would all say 'well done',
but actually it isn't.
 
> What if the user is not a Cobol programmer? I could send him or her
> one executable. You'd have to send a compiler, a make utility, a
> Python interpreter, and a document explaining how to write copybooks.

Now why would all that be required ?  The program runs at the
development site where the RM compiler and the source code is located
and the resulting executables are delivered to the end user along with
the executables for the system.

I am not selling a packaged solution to a developer, I am suggesting a
solution that they can implement, the executables from which are sent
to the end user.

I think that you have clearly missed all the requirements of the task
and have, as you have often done, made up a 'solution' to a completely
different task.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is a General File Maintenance Routine possible?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
On 17 Sep 2004 12:46:24 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

>> With other
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is a General File Maintenance Routine possible?

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-16T16:11:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409161457.1a8a4b98@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<unhrj05jdogr3n5d06lgnra6u135o4aggp@4ax.com> <Rk22d.439344$gE.57805@pd7tw3no> <ahcjk05jobfdngjd08hvj1i86v78sii7qt@4ax.com>`

```
sbelt@dpcsystems.com wrote

> What I'm trying to do is make it easier for us to support our
> customers.  This file maintenance routine would not be used directly
> by our customers but by our programmers - tracing down data problems,
> out of balance journals, etc.

I would start by defining an interface which includes a whole bunch of
standardised fields and has enough of them to hold the data of every
individual file.  eg:

      01  Data-Interface.
          03  DI-Key              PIC X(36).
          03  DI-Alternate        PIC X(36) OCCURS 5.
          03  DI-Flags            PIC X     OCCURS 30.
          03  DI-Words            PIC X(4)  OCCURS 30.
          03  DI-Texts            PIC X(36) OCCURS 40.
          03  DI-Values           PIC S9(10)V9999 OCCURS 50.
          03  DI-Dates            PIC X(8)  OCCURS 15.

Try to keep the number of field type down.

Set up a dictionary for each file that refers to these as F03, W12 etc
and also includes the field name as used in the FD for the file
(hopefully as a copybook).

Now you need one 'maintenance' program that will read the appropriate
dictionary and works with the interface.  It will dynamically call a
program to read, rewrite, delete etc the records of the file and will
display, allow amendment, etc.

Now you will need 200 programs that will read, rewrite, etc and will
move data between the record layout and the interface, one for each
file.

To get these 200 programs you will need a template skeleton program,
the copy books, the dictionary and a program that will create the
final 200 programs from these.

I have actually done this sort of thing. For example I needed about 70
file conversion programs which I had copybooks for. One skeleton
template, one control file that had a set of parameters, such as
program name, copybook name, and several other variables and a
template processor and it was all ready.

Now actually I wrote the template processor in Python because the
whole thing is about 20 lines of code in that language, but it could
easily be done in Cobol.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is a General File Maintenance Routine possible?

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-17T15:12:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a9D2d.11426$yp2.7936@newssvr30.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<unhrj05jdogr3n5d06lgnra6u135o4aggp@4ax.com> <Rk22d.439344$gE.57805@pd7tw3no> <ahcjk05jobfdngjd08hvj1i86v78sii7qt@4ax.com>`

```
> My original criteria for this project, which I did not put in my OP,
> was something like:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> into memory, change it in memory and write back to RM file.

Shawn,

One of the biggest issues for you is, "How will I build a data dictionary?"

Since you already have the Relativity SDK, you can use it as a toolkit to 
build an accurate dictionary.  You can the use the Relativity Designer's 
built-in reporting mechanism to export the physical schema (i.e. the file 
record layouts), and use that export as input to a program generator to 
generate maintenance programs.

An alternative source of information for building a data dictionary would be 
the allocation map in the listing file.  The Relativity route would be 
easier, since it would generate exactly the dictionary information you would 
be looking for, some of which would need to be inferred from an allocation 
map.   Relativity also allows you to enhance/supplement the dictionary 
information.

WRT "No additional deployment costs" you really should talk to the Liant 
sales folks about licensing terms specific to the described use.

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is a General File Maintenance Routine possible?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-17T17:26:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t7F2d.21990$VI3.7191@newssvr24.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<unhrj05jdogr3n5d06lgnra6u135o4aggp@4ax.com> <Rk22d.439344$gE.57805@pd7tw3no> <ahcjk05jobfdngjd08hvj1i86v78sii7qt@4ax.com> <a9D2d.11426$yp2.7936@newssvr30.news.prodigy.com> <f95mk0ln8psf9kmkiba5jhorppn05b19de@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message 
news:f95mk0ln8psf9kmkiba5jhorppn05b19de@4ax.com...
> Is either the Relativity schema or export compatible with your ODBC 
> driver?

Relativity is the brand name (registered trademark) for Liant's ODBC driver 
for RM/COBOL and Micro Focus COBOL data files.

Relativity has a Designer component which is used by the application 
developer to develop the catalog (very enhanced data dictionary) which is 
then deployed with the ODBC driver component.  The Designer can process 
COBOL source or the symbol tables embedded in RM/COBOL object files to 
produce the physical schema.  The application developer then uses the 
Designer to describe the mapping of COBOL record layouts/data items to SQL 
tables/datatypes.  This includes mapping data items containing date/time 
information (in a myriad of formats) to SQL dates, resolving multiple record 
types (redefinition in both the large - record level - and small scale), 
determining NULL representations where appropriate, normalizing OCCURS, etc.

A detailed explanation of all this may be found in an on-line white paper: 
http://www.liant.com/download/pdf/theoryweb.pdf.

For the truly self-abusive, you may find additional interest in the US 
Patent 5,826,076,
http://patft.uspto.gov/netacgi/nph-Parser?Sect1=PTO1&Sect2=HITOFF&d=PALL&p=1&u=/netahtml/srchnum.htm&r=1&f=G&l=50&s1=5,826,076.WKU.&OS=PN/5,826,076&RS=PN/5,826,076

>
> If yes, there are many query and reporting tools that could be used.
>

Yes that is the idea behind Relativity: provide ODBC (and JDBC) access to 
COBOL data files without the need for data warehousing, etc. so that all 
those ODBC- and JDBC-enabled tools may be used.

Shawn has expressed concern about the additional cost of goods sold if his 
company were to adopt Relativity.  While the deployment cost is fairly 
small, if you cannot justify any additional cost to the end-user, then the 
value proposition is not favorable to adoption.

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message 
news:5samk09shog31hem7ccpipiohi6bht8u6i@4ax.com...
[snip]
> IBM mainframers may be interested to know the ODBC driver also
> provides access to MVS files.

If this is meant to say that Relativity is available for MVS files on a 
mainframe, this is no longer true.  That product has been withdrawn from the 
market.  If you came to this conclusion from our marketing materials on the 
web, please tell me where (I didn't see any mainframe references in a quick 
review).

There *do* exist ODBC drivers for MVS files from other companies.  They are 
not as feature rich as Relativity (see below) in my estimation (but I 
stopped evaluating these products several years ago).

>
>>For the truly self-abusive, you may find additional interest in the US
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> schema format and tools that maintain it.

Of course, prior art is acknowledged in the patent disclosure and the 
twenty-three claims speak for themselves, assuming one can interpret 
patent-ese.  The advances of the art are significant in the area of dealing 
with, for lack of a better term, automatic normalization of typical COBOL 
data structures and data definition practices.

Tom Morrison
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is a General File Maintenance Routine possible?

- **From:** rjones0@hotmail.com (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2004-09-17T13:22:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6dd8322.0409171222.1f3dc1b0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<unhrj05jdogr3n5d06lgnra6u135o4aggp@4ax.com> <Rk22d.439344$gE.57805@pd7tw3no> <ahcjk05jobfdngjd08hvj1i86v78sii7qt@4ax.com>`

```
message snipped

Hello Shawn

To go off on a somewhat different tack, I would be very wary of
fiddling with files without using the programs designed for the
purpose in a production system.  Certainly, for accounting purposes
such as the journals you mentioned, an audit trail of all changes is
highly desirable, and an accountant might be very unhappy with changes
being applied in such an ad-hoc manner as you suggest.

Many organisations would also be generally unhappy about changes being
applied in such an unregulated manner and you would have to be careful
to ensure that the people using such tools have the appropriate
authorisation.

By all means buy, borrow or write general purpose update facilities
but try to restrict your use of them to test environments, they can be
very useful for tweaking test data to satisfy particular conditions. 
A print program for each file is often a useful asset, especially if
it also checks and reports fields with invalid data.

As others have mentioned there are several professional products that
will provide batch or online updating of files, using copybooks to
present the data in an easily manipulable form, some might be
shareware or free if you do the appropriate online searches.  Some
have even suggested ways of you doing this yourself.

Regards, Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is a General File Maintenance Routine possible?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-18T00:28:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ogvmk09079s3qpq8e83n80erfm4urvq0ka@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<unhrj05jdogr3n5d06lgnra6u135o4aggp@4ax.com> <Rk22d.439344$gE.57805@pd7tw3no> <ahcjk05jobfdngjd08hvj1i86v78sii7qt@4ax.com> <6dd8322.0409171222.1f3dc1b0@posting.google.com>`

```
On 17 Sep 2004 13:22:09 -0700, rjones0@hotmail.com (Robert Jones)
wrote:

>Hello Shawn
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>authorisation.

This is a reference to the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002, which was
passed post-Enron to prohibit destroying evidence in cases under
Federal investigation. 

It was never intended to affect error correction in day-to-day
operation of company systems, only to stop fraud in companies UNDER
INVESTIGATION. Read the Act if you don't believe me.

Merchants of FUD and bureaucratic overhead have perverted its intent
to embrace nearly all systems. They've turned Sarbanes-Oxley into a
money-making, job-creating Industry. 

Companies who fall for it are doing their stockholders a disservice.
```

- **From:** rjones0@hotmail.com (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2004-09-18T08:00:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6dd8322.0409180700.5a901c6a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<unhrj05jdogr3n5d06lgnra6u135o4aggp@4ax.com> <Rk22d.439344$gE.57805@pd7tw3no> <ahcjk05jobfdngjd08hvj1i86v78sii7qt@4ax.com> <6dd8322.0409171222.1f3dc1b0@posting.google.com> <ogvmk09079s3qpq8e83n80erfm4urvq0ka@4ax.com>`

```
Bottom posting

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message news:<ogvmk09079s3qpq8e83n80erfm4urvq0ka@4ax.com>...
> On 17 Sep 2004 13:22:09 -0700, rjones0@hotmail.com (Robert Jones)
> wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> Companies who fall for it are doing their stockholders a disservice.

No I do not know of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act, or have forgotten about
it, in any case I have never worked in the US, so its provisions are
not of direct interest to me, though I am sure several other countries
have similar legislation.

My comments were made regarding general accountancy practice. 
Accountants and auditors in the UK have a general responsibility to
ensure that financial systems are operating correctly and to prevent
and detect fraud and other malpractice, ideally long before it becomes
a significant problem necessitating government and or regulatory
investigation.  This has been a long standing requirement and predates
the use of computers.

I did not say that file editors should never be used, but that their
use should be sparing and indicated the preferability of using the
carefully designed correct procedures for changing data in a system. 
In any case provision of an audit trail of changes should be
considered when making them, even if it is only a manual log.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is a General File Maintenance Routine possible?

- **From:** Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net>

```
Right ON, Mr. Jones. That's what I thought when I finished my BS
major in Accounting. Then I was exposed to the real world, and to this
day, I wonder where the auditors went.

At our shop, long ago, we had internal auditors who created their own
data files as a means of auditing changes that were applied. I guess
a company has to be very very honest to do this, but it does Not cover
all the things that can and do happen.

Warren Simmons
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** rjones0@hotmail.com (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2004-09-19T04:03:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6dd8322.0409190303.56a373fb@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<unhrj05jdogr3n5d06lgnra6u135o4aggp@4ax.com> <Rk22d.439344$gE.57805@pd7tw3no> <ahcjk05jobfdngjd08hvj1i86v78sii7qt@4ax.com> <6dd8322.0409171222.1f3dc1b0@posting.google.com> <ogvmk09079s3qpq8e83n80erfm4urvq0ka@4ax.com> <6dd8322.0409180700.5a901c6a@posting.google.com> <eI%2d.463233$gE.105471@pd7tw3no>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:<eI%2d.463233$gE.105471@pd7tw3no>...
> Robert Jones wrote:
> 
…[75 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
Hello Jimmy,

No, I was never an accountant, I got a degree in Botany, which I think
means I am rather less ignorant about that than I was before.  Most of
my working life has been as an applications programmer, though that is
not what I am doing now.

I also thought that such accounting practices were fairly general
internationally if not univeral, but am unsure of my facts in that
matter.  However, despite what obviously does go wrong quite
frequently, it is also the case that many miscreants are and were
brought to book through legislation prior to the Sarbanes-Oxley act in
the US, though that was probably meant to close more loopholes.  I
don't know the details of that act so can't comment further.

Apart from accountancy practices, most, if not all, systems
programmers and managers are also well aware of the need for
appropriate controls, suitable for the particular circumstances of
their organisations and specific applications.

Another benefit of logging ad-hoc changes, is that it enables managers
to identify problem areas, so they can direct their attention to
rectifying faults, particularly those that recur frequently or have a
high impact on security, down time, etc.

Robert
```

```
Hi Robert,

My background is in accounting and business admin.  I'm one of
those who "morphed into a programmer " by asking too many
questions.

Everything you say here is correct.  What ever I did would have
to meet  AICPA standards.

This would be for testing purposes, minor corrections after a
power outage, debugging bad data, etc.

After reading everyone's ideas, I think I may have figured out
a way of doing this.  If it works out, I'll come back and let you
know.

Thanks everyone for you input and ideas.

Shawn
```

<a id="t-4427474de8"></a>
## Thread: Paragraph name flagged as unknown verb during compilation by MICROFOCUS — 19 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Paragraph name flagged as unknown verb during compilation by MICROFOCUS

- **From:** int_21h@yahoo.com (kigs)
- **Date:** 2004-09-08T03:16:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dabf410c.0409080216.5a45e951@posting.google.com>`

```
This problem is driving us crazy.  We have embedded SQL in our COBOL
program.  When the beginning of the PROCEDURE DIVISION looks like
this:

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
           EXEC SQL WHENEVER SQLWARNING CONTINUE END-EXEC.  
       MAINLINE.

The compiler will complain that MAINLINE is an unknown verb.  When we
comment out the EXEC SQL code before the paragraph name, it compiled
fine.  When we commented out the paragraph name, it compiled fine. 
When we insert any COBOL verb in between the EXEC SQL statement and
MAINLINE paragraph, it compiles fine.  Why is the compiler complaining
when the EXEC SQL statement is followed by the MAILINE paragraph?  I
tried this in mainframe and it didn't have any problem with it.  It's
only with MICROFOCUS.  Did anybody encounter this before?  Can anybody
here help us with this?

By the way, here's the directive we are using:
CASE
ANS85
VSC2
OSVS
NOTRUNC
DEFFILETYPE"NT"
DEFFILE
OUTDD"$MYDIR\OUT.TXT"
p(cobsql) csqlt=syb c2s verbose end-c -a -m p(cp) sy endp

Can anybody help me?

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Paragraph name flagged as unknown verb during compilation by MICROFOCUS

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-08T12:11:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UFC%c.13698$FV3.6918@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<dabf410c.0409080216.5a45e951@posting.google.com>`

```
> This problem is driving us crazy.  We have embedded SQL in our COBOL
> program.  When the beginning of the PROCEDURE DIVISION looks like
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> The compiler will complain that MAINLINE is an unknown verb.

Doesn't the first "thing" after "PROCEDURE DIVISION" need to be a paragraph
name?

When the EXEC SQL is expanded, it's probably a bunch of MOVE and CALL
statements without a paragraph name.....

So try this....

        PROCEDURE DIVISION.
        MAINLINE.
             EXEC SQL WHENEVER SQLWARNING CONTINUE END-EXEC.

.. which should result in the same execution path...


MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Paragraph name flagged as unknown verb during compilation by MICROFOCUS

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-08T13:38:21+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<chmur3$rrs$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<dabf410c.0409080216.5a45e951@posting.google.com> <UFC%c.13698$FV3.6918@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`

```
The Sybase precompiler, cobpre, will generate code from your

       EXEC SQL WHENEVER ...

statement. I *suspect* that the subsequent program structure, when passed to
the Micro Focus COBOL compiler, breaks the language rules as defined by one
of these directives :

ANS85
VSC2
OSVS

so I think Michael's suggestion would be the best approach.

SimonT.
```

#### ↳ Re: Paragraph name flagged as unknown verb during compilation by MICROFOCUS

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-08T17:07:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<noeuj09f8sbl4cubvcdss9c4shf26tlqrm@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<dabf410c.0409080216.5a45e951@posting.google.com>`

```
On 8 Sep 2004 03:16:14 -0700, int_21h@yahoo.com (kigs) wrote:

>This problem is driving us crazy.  We have embedded SQL in our COBOL
>program.  When the beginning of the PROCEDURE DIVISION looks like
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>here help us with this?

The culpret is the Pro*Cobol precompiler, not Micro Focus. Looking  at
the intermediate .cob file should reveal the answer.  I'd first
suspect a missing period or something else on the last line of
generated code.

Although Micro Focus doesn't require a paragraph name after the
procedure division header, Pro*Cobol probably does. Try putting
MAINLINE before the EXEC SQL.
```

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-09T13:04:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<dabf410c.0409080216.5a45e951@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0409081047.683d5095@posting.google.com> <dabf410c.0409081832.759e8f1c@posting.google.com>`

```
"kigs" <int_21h@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:dabf410c.0409081832.759e8f1c@posting.google.com...
> Thanks for the response guys.  But even though putting the paragraph
> name before the EXEC works fine, our PM wants the code to have the
> EXEC SQL WHENEVER... statement first, then the paragraph name.  He
> claims that it worked on the other work site, and it should work here
> as well.  I know better than to argue.

Maybe my attitude comes from being my own boss for eleven years, but ....

This PM apparently does not have sufficient education to understand that
WHENEVER may be specified multiple times within a program.

So what does he/she suggest would be the proper style/location if the
WHENEVER would need to change somewhere during the program run? Huh?

And, who gives a rat's butt where an "EXEC SQL" appears in the source code
as long as the software works correctly, is relatively efficient, and
maintainable?

Sheesh.
```

```
one's own experience/knowledge) is quite different than claiming to know 
everything.

How did it go - something like "when I was a teenager, I knew 
everything.  In my twenties I realized that I knew most things. (etc.) 
And the last, I don't know anything."  Or was it "The more I learn, the 
more I discover I don't know".
```

## Thread: Automatic detection of a COBOL dialect ? — 18 msgs

### Automatic detection of a COBOL dialect ?

- **From:** partoche@caramail.com (diesmo)
- **Date:** 2004-09-08T08:37:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcfbe153.0409080737.69d63d19@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello!

I am trying to enhance a javaCC parser for COBOL, which only accept
free format with "*>" comments.

I must say i have little knowledge about COBOL.
For now, I automatically detect fixed or free format, like this :
   - if the first line (without spaces) starts with a number, then it
is fixed,
   - else it is free format.

This seems to work (if the cols 1-6 in fixed format, when trimed,
always contains a number :) ).

For "variable" format, it is more tricky... Cols 1-6 may contain
anything, it seems.

Does anyone know a trick to distinguish a fixed format cobol program
from a variable format one ?
For example, if the column 1-6 only contains spaces, then it is
variable format ?
Or a way to determin the dialect (assuming each dialect uses only one
format)?
Or is it impossible, and the user must specify the format manually ?

Thanks in advance.

Regards,
diesmo.
```

#### ↳ Re: Automatic detection of a COBOL dialect ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-08T15:40:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LJF%c.992017$y4.174591@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<bcfbe153.0409080737.69d63d19@posting.google.com>`

```
I know of no compiler in which the user does NOT need to specify the format.  In 
all of them there is a "default" - but if they support multiple formats, then 
the programmer selects the format via a compiler option or directive.
```

#### ↳ Re: Automatic detection of a COBOL dialect ?

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-08T17:29:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mjfuj0t07v7uo40udotcq4qgq5vu855h56@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<bcfbe153.0409080737.69d63d19@posting.google.com>`

```
On 8 Sep 2004 08:37:32 -0700, partoche@caramail.com (diesmo) wrote:

>I am trying to enhance a javaCC parser for COBOL, which only accept
>free format with "*>" comments.

Free format permits comments with * in column 1.

>I must say i have little knowledge about COBOL.
>For now, I automatically detect fixed or free format, like this :
>   - if the first line (without spaces) starts with a number, then it
>is fixed,
>   - else it is free format.

A number would tell you for sure, but it's more likely to have spaces.
Programmers usually don't use numbers. It's also possible for the
first line to contain a compiler option such as one of the following
starting in column 7, usually, or 1. If you see one of these, you know
the format. $SET is Micro Focus; @OPTIONS is Fujitsu. Other compilers
will have similar options. IBM doesn't support free format.

$SET SOURCEFORMAT"FREE" 
@OPTIONS SRF(FREE,FREE)
$SET SOURCEFORMAT"FIXED"'
@OPTIONS SRF(FIX,FIX)

>Does anyone know a trick to distinguish a fixed format cobol program
>from a variable format one ?

Two passes.

>For example, if the column 1-6 only contains spaces, then it is
>variable format ?

It's probably fixed format, not not necessarily.

>Or a way to determin the dialect (assuming each dialect uses only one
>format)?
>Or is it impossible, and the user must specify the format manually ?

Compilers require the user to specify, either in the compile script or
at the top of the program. The default is fixed.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Automatic detection of a COBOL dialect ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-08T18:17:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<q1I%c.998035$y4.175691@news.easynews.com>`

```
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

True for some (not all) existing extensions to the '85 Standard

NOT true for free format reference format as defined in the 2002 ISO Standard
```

```
*>----------------------- editcustomers.cbl ------------------------- = 
Line 1 Identifier for my program/class

*>------------------------------------------------------------------- = 
denotes end of my program/class source

asterisk in  Column 1.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Automatic detection of a COBOL dialect ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-08T19:22:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YZI%c.409510$ic1.39042@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<bcfbe153.0409080737.69d63d19@posting.google.com> <mjfuj0t07v7uo40udotcq4qgq5vu855h56@4ax.com> <q1I%c.998035$y4.175691@news.easynews.com> <XsI%c.360869$gE.148484@pd7tw3no>`

```
Even for MF, can we say "compiler directive"???

"MFCOMMENT
Treats lines with an asterisk (*) in column 1 the same as comment lines but does 
not show them in the source listing.
    ...
Comments:
With MFCOMMENT, lines with an asterisk in column 1 are ignored by the Compiler 
and do not appear in the source listing (though they do appear in Animator). 
With NOMFCOMMENT, the asterisk forms part of the sequence number and has no 
special significance."
```

#### ↳ Re: Automatic detection of a COBOL dialect ?

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-08T18:30:02+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mkfuj05prmddmque02i9pena7r7cf92opo@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<bcfbe153.0409080737.69d63d19@posting.google.com>`

```
On 8 Sep 2004 08:37:32 -0700, partoche@caramail.com (diesmo) wrote:

>Hello!
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>always contains a number :) ).
This is an incorrect assumption.

If you have
ABCD   IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  testing.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
EFGH   DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01 V1            pic x(11) value "ABC-DEF-GHI".
       PROCEDURE       DIVISION.
       MAIN.
           DISPLAY V1.
it is fixed format and does not have any number on the 1-6 cols.

One thing that "might" work would be 
if position 7 on any line contains a letter other than "D" then it is
free format, or is wrongly coded.

Now you could still have a valid free format program that just
happened to only have "D"'s on position 7. Very unlikely though.

But if you are parsing a copybook on it's own (you didn't tell us what
your parser does), then it is possible that a freeformat copybook will
have only numbers on cols 1-6.



>
>For "variable" format, it is more tricky... Cols 1-6 may contain
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Or is it impossible, and the user must specify the format manually ?
Same vendor can use both free and fixed format. There are rules to
determine this.


>
>Thanks in advance.
>
>Regards,
>diesmo.



Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Automatic detection of a COBOL dialect ?

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-08T21:21:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pctuj0tnjgv090br05f0hfa4ia6nm1n49d@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<bcfbe153.0409080737.69d63d19@posting.google.com> <mkfuj05prmddmque02i9pena7r7cf92opo@4ax.com>`

```
On Wed, 08 Sep 2004 18:30:02 +0100, Frederico Fonseca
<real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com> wrote:

>One thing that "might" work would be 
>if position 7 on any line contains a letter other than "D" then it is
>free format, or is wrongly coded.

A Java jockey won't be happy with two passes. 

>But if you are parsing a copybook on it's own (you didn't tell us what
>your parser does), then it is possible that a freeformat copybook will
>have only numbers on cols 1-6.

I doubt diesmo has even thought about copybooks. It is semi-common for
copybooks to be fixed-form while the main program is free-form.  In
that case the main program says:

$SET SOURCEFORMAT"FIXED"
      copy foo.cpy.
$SET SOURCEFORMAT"FREE"

Exception to all of this -- if the compiler is Realia (semi-extinct),
the file extension tells the format. Cob means fixed; cbl means free.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Automatic detection of a COBOL dialect ?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** partoche@caramail.com (diesmo)

- **Message-ID:** `<bcfbe153.0409210714.5bc218ee@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bcfbe153.0409080737.69d63d19@posting.google.com> <mkfuj05prmddmque02i9pena7r7cf92opo@4ax.com> <pctuj0tnjgv090br05f0hfa4ia6nm1n49d@4ax.com> <Z-2dncRKTeiPCqLcRVn-rQ@giganews.com> <217e491a.0409091125.694ff849@posting.google.com> <r4q1k01bus7hnj0jrq17ssjf8b1ctej0da@4ax.com>`

```
Hello!

Thank you for your answers and comments.
Okay, it seems impossible to detect the format, so i can only try to
find a method that works "as often as possible".

Here is a listing of what is GENERALLY (not systematically) described
as "formats" (found on SIBER site).

Format of the Cobol source
===========================

* fixed
=======
The standard Cobol format. Columns 1 to 6 contain sequence number,
column 7 contains indicator, columns 8 to 72 contain Cobol code.

* free
=======
Free format. Column 1 contains indicator, columns 2 to 255 contain
Cobol code.

* fsc-free
===========
Fujitsu Cobol "free" format. There is no indicator area, all comments
start with "*>",  Cobol code may appear in any column.

* var
======
Variable format. Columns 1 to 6 contain sequence number, column 7
contains indicator,
columns 8 to 255 contain Cobol code.

 
The website then uses the following default values :
the format is determined by the dialect. 
   - For FSC Cobol default line format is variable, 
   - for ICobol it is free 
   - and for all other Cobol dialects default line format is fixed.

Heuristic :
===========
I think the method i provided is not too bad (and not too complicate):
   - if the $SETFORMAT directive is present then it's ok, else
   - if the first 6 columns of the file contains a sequence number,
assume it is fixed
   - else it is free (if the format is variable and the sequence
number is always empty, then the format is considered as free).
If people use proprietary "tricks" (* on column 1 with MFocus ;), or
variable format, or do not put sequence numbers, then they go to hell
and specify the format :) !

An optimization is to check the format if a find a dialect specific
keyword or construction...

So, here is a list of "popular" cobol dialects and their supported
formats :
ansi74 "1974 ANSI COBOL standard"              - fixed
ansi85 "1985 ANSI COBOL standard"              - free
osvs "IBM OSVS COBOL"                          - fixed
vsii "IBM VS COBOL II (revision 2, 3 and 4)"   - fixed
saa "IBM SAA COBOL (levels 1 and 2)"           - fixed
dosvs "IBM DOSVS COBOL"                        - fixed
mf "Micro Focus"                               - fixed
ms "Microsoft"                                 - fixed
xopen "X/OPEN COBOL"                           - fixed
rm "RM COBOL 74"                               - fixed
rm85 "RM COBOL 85"                             - free
univac "UNISYS COBOL"                          - fixed
wang "Wang VS COBOL"                           - free
icobol "ICobol"                                - fixed (based on
ANSI74)
acu "Acu COBOL"                                - fixed
fsc "Fujitsu COBOL"                            - free/variable
net "Fujitsu NetCOBOL"                         - free/variable

Is that correct ?

The variable format seems  not to be widely used :  
   only Fujistu COBOL uses it. true ?
If somebody knows other "popular" dialects to complete the list
(Object cobol for example?), that would help.


Thanks.

Regards,
diesmo.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Automatic detection of a COBOL dialect ?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-21T09:57:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cipmh7$10ga$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<bcfbe153.0409080737.69d63d19@posting.google.com> <mkfuj05prmddmque02i9pena7r7cf92opo@4ax.com> <pctuj0tnjgv090br05f0hfa4ia6nm1n49d@4ax.com> <Z-2dncRKTeiPCqLcRVn-rQ@giganews.com> <217e491a.0409091125.694ff849@posting.google.com> <r4q1k01bus7hnj0jrq17ssjf8b1ctej0da@4ax.com> <bcfbe153.0409210714.5bc218ee@posting.google.com>`

```

"diesmo" <partoche@caramail.com> wrote in message
news:bcfbe153.0409210714.5bc218ee@posting.google.com...
> Hello!
>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>

A few quibbles:

The right-hand end of the COBOL source area for "*fixed" was by convention
usually in Column 72, but is actually defined by the individual implementor,
as early as the '74 standard.  Your "*fixed" and "*var* seem to me to be one
and the same, as far as the COBOL standards go.

The distinction between Column 8 ("margin A") and Column 12 ("margin B"),
which you don't mention, was significant for '74 and '85 COBOL, but has been
erased in the '02 standard.
.
Your "*free" is not standard COBOL.  Personally I don't know of any
implementations that use this form.

What you describe as "*fsc-free" and unique to Fujitsu is actually what is
mandated as "free-form source format in ISO/IEC 1989:2002, the 2002 standard
for COBOL.   Note that the "indicators" are also floating in that format -- 
*> for comment indicator, >> for compiler directive. "- or '- for literal
continuation indicator, and >>D for debugging indicator.

The default format in 2002 standard COBOL is still fixed-form reference
format:  sequence (which need not be a number) in columns 1-6, indicator (if
fixed) in column 7, program-text area starting at Margin A in Column 8 and
extending (as for '74 and '85) however far the implementor defines.

There's also a Unisys MCP (descendant of the Burroughs Large System)
extension that they call "free"; let's call it "*mcp-free":
    columns 1-6 :  sequence number area
    columns 7-72:  program text area (debug indicator not allowed)
    columns 73-80: the traditional "identification" area.

Thus:


>    - if the first 6 columns of the file contains a sequence number,
> assume it is fixed

Needn't be a number in '85 COBOL or in fixed-form reference format in '02
COBOL.  The '74 standard indicates that it contains digits.

>    - else it is free (if the format is variable and the sequence
> number is always empty, then the format is considered as free).

Bad idea if blanks are legal in the sequence number area!

> So, here is a list of "popular" cobol dialects and their supported
> formats :

> ansi85 "1985 ANSI COBOL standard"              - free

No.  "*fixed", as I've clarified above.

You forgot

2002 ISO/IEC COBOL standard -- fixed or free as clarified above.


> univac "UNISYS COBOL"                          - fixed

I believe there are three dialects of OS2200 COBOL.  I don't know much about
them, but if you're going to take the trouble to enumerate a bunch of IBM
dialects, it might be appropriate to grant the Unisys folks the same
courtesy ...

And given the longevity of the Burroughs Large System and its descendants,
why not

Unisys MCP COBOL74    - *fixed (as redefined) or *mcp-free
Unisys MCP COBOL85    -  *fixed (as redefined) or *mcp-free


> The variable format seems  not to be widely used :
>    only Fujistu COBOL uses it. true ?

That's likely true.  Everybody's free to do whatever they want to do so long
as it's identified as a vendor extension, but one of the goals of writing
standard COBOL is to ensure that your COBOL is as specified in the standard,
and that includes the source formats!    Since free-form reference format
wasn't approved as standard COBOL until 12/1/2002, it's not a surprise that
a number of compilers implemented according to previous standards don't
include it!

Your "*fixed" and "*var", as described above, both fall within the
description of the "reference format" for the '74 and '85 standard and for
"fixed-form reference format" in the '02 standard, with "*var" having the
more accurate description, I think.

Your "*fsc-free" is virtually identical to the "free-form reference format"
defined in the '02 standard.   To get there you've got to have something
like ">> SOURCE FORMAT FREE" as the first line of the compilation group.
That line may be in either fixed form or free form reference format, by the
way.

But "*free", as you have described it, is a vendor extension and is not
standard COBOL.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
On Tue, 21 Sep 2004 09:57:10 -0700, Chuck Stevens wrote:

>> univac "UNISYS COBOL"                          - fixed
>
>I believe there are three dialects of OS2200 COBOL.

   @ACOB or ASCII COBOL conforming to ANS-COBOL-74 (with extensions, like
asynchronous processing)
   -->> Fixed format with margin R at col 72

   @UCOB or UCS COBOL, conforming to ANS-COBOL-85 (with extensions like
Embedded SQL, Embedded CODASYL DML)
   -->> Fixed format with margin R at col 72

   OO-COBOL or Object Oriented COBOL, conforming to ANS-COBOL-85 with
extensions like UCOB plus object oriented extensions based on a subset of the
2002 COBOL standard.
   --> Fixed format with margin R at col 72
or --> Free format with lines of maximal 80 characters


Yours,
L. Willms
```

## Thread: PowerCOBOL Hint. Hell, REVELATION! — 11 msgs

### PowerCOBOL Hint. Hell, REVELATION!

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-08T10:51:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HeudnRQXwZoIt6LcRVn-sA@giganews.com>`

```
Plagued with an intractable error message at the start of debugging "...
error in xxxxx.SVD....," and having exhausted all known "fondle-it-until it
works" techniques, we girded our loins, went not into our wives this night,
and started tearing the son-of-a-bitch apart.

Found it!

Before I tell you what the problem was, I've got to tell you there was much
dancing in the street. The vino flowed freely. There was joy throughout the
land! Now I'm going to share with you the disease that generated the above
symptom. Wait for it now....

A superfluous end-of-file smack in the middle of Working-Storage.

This particular EOF was possibly sucked in with a defective #INCLUDE
copy-book, but however the EOF got there it confused the hell out of the
debugger.*

Further, the miscreant EOF could not be removed or painted over! It clung
like a barnacle and nothing would dislodge the sucker - normal editing
procedures simply skipped over the damned thing (you evidently can't get rid
of a hole by digging it out).

The fix was to cut all above this errant EOF, pasting same into a dummy
procedure division area, doing the same with the area following the culprit.
Then delete the Working-Storage module, followed by re-creating same with
the stuff formerly saved in a holding area.

I apologize for being so effusive on this matter, but this particular puddle
has occupied the time of one programmer, off and on, for over three weeks.

Such a relief. I'm going to go have a lie down.

----
*One of our failed attempts at a solution involved ditching all the
#INCLUDEs and replacing them with the original copy-book texts. This failed,
no doubt, because the copy-book had the extra EOF or the extra EOF was
already firmly ensconced in the PC code.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PowerCOBOL Hint. Hell, REVELATION!

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-09T09:47:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Russell Styles wrote:
> Some editors will allow you to see this sort of thing.
>
> EDLIN was one - it required an option that I forget right now.
>
> Of course, who has EDLIN on their pc these days.

Oh we could SEE it; it was NOTICING it that was our fault. An EOF is shown
as a left-arrow which looks very much like a RETURN (broken left-arrow) and
we just didn't snap.

Then, once we did find the EOF, getting rid of the son-of-a-bitch required
jumping through several hoops.

Anyway, this exercise illustrates the maxim: "If you stare at something more
than five minutes, the problem is either trivial to solve or nigh-on
impossible."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PowerCOBOL Hint. Hell, REVELATION!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** epc8@juno.com (E P Chandler)
- **Date:** 2004-09-09T15:38:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0409091438.29df59ba@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<HeudnRQXwZoIt6LcRVn-sA@giganews.com> <ZqidnXu6R6hSfaLcRVn-ow@giganews.com> <5vGdncb6s4iS8N3cRVn-pA@giganews.com> <nbudnZmLCouADN3cRVn-oQ@giganews.com>`

```
"Russell Styles" <rws0203@comcast.net> wrote in message news:<nbudnZmLCouADN3cRVn-oQ@giganews.com>...

> To find a cntl/z, just "TYPE" the file.
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
 
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
> news:5vGdncb6s4iS8N3cRVn-pA@giganews.com...

> > Then, once we did find the EOF, getting rid of the son-of-a-bitch required
> > jumping through several hoops.

Two good candidates for removing embedded EOF marks (control-z) or
other unwanted characters from files under Win32 are

1) Unix "tr" - a GNU port can be found at
http://unxutils.sourceforge.net/
eg  
    tr -d \032 <foo.txt >foo.new

Note that \032 is *octal*.

2) gsar - a dos global search and replace utility - even though it is
restricted to 8.3 filenames, it will handle a group of long file names
via wildcards. gsar110.zip can be found on any one of the SimTel or
Garbo mirrors or through a google search.

eg
    gsar -s:x1a -r -o *.cob

Note that :x1a is *hex* and -r means replace with noting, ie delete.
-o is overwrite in place.

3) I've written a debug script (as part of a set of utilities) which
generates a filter program that strips out all control-z characters.
It makes dos calls but since it uses input and output redirection it
will handle long file names. Feel free to e-mail me if interested.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PowerCOBOL Hint. Hell, REVELATION!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** epc8@juno.com (E P Chandler)
- **Date:** 2004-09-09T20:41:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0409091941.1f45fe51@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<HeudnRQXwZoIt6LcRVn-sA@giganews.com> <5vGdncb6s4iS8N3cRVn-pA@giganews.com> <nbudnZmLCouADN3cRVn-oQ@giganews.com> <7f64e2ff.0409091438.29df59ba@posting.google.com> <chqmnc$f2c$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote in message news:<chqmnc$f2c$1@panix5.panix.com>...
> In article <7f64e2ff.0409091438.29df59ba@posting.google.com>,
> E P Chandler <epc8@juno.com> wrote:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

It's true that octal is a quirky Unix relic. (Perhaps someone has been
reading too many _dumps_?) However, gsar does accept 3 digit decimal
numbers as parameters:

gsar -s:026 -r -o *.cob

My concern was to find programs that are well known and that actually
handle files containing the MS-DOS EOF mark. (Reading redirected
standard input as binary rather than as a text stream is often a
problem on MS-DOS/Windows.) As obscure as Unix arcana is, I still
prefer it to JCL <grin>.
```
