_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-29T21:56:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YbG6d.2339590$6p.396746@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <184il0hcvljo98k04t2gn1v57172blit6b@4ax.com> <uabil092fbefvedp9qmm36bjcgar6d52i6@4ax.com> <10lj5hjqgv90v89@corp.supernews.com> <j07jl0t0qd5pf1euvamk59ksdgjaf9gkta@4ax.com> <cjc9lp$1eq0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <h3jjl0lj0nklv7e9rj0vi6co8cf9shhb1a@4ax.com> <cjf185$8aq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cjf3nm$pn6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cjf9g5$dnr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message 
news:cjf9g5$dnr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
> ... which is precisely why I suggested this methodology be in a user-defined
> Function whose arguments and behavior are well-described ...  ;-)
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Point of clarification (possibly just personal opinion),

The distinction that Chuck is making is a POST-2002 Standard distinction. 
Although the 2002 Standard introduced "user-defined functions", the following 
INTRINSIC funcitons were introduced at the same time.  These are just the ones 
that I think would have been easy to create "user-defined" functions for - but 
instead intrinsic ones were provided:

DATE-TO-YYYYMMDD
DAY-TO-YYYYDDD
E
EXP
EXP10
FRACTION-PART
NUMVAL-F
PI
SIGN
YEAR-TO-YYYY
```

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-29T22:39:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uhdml09pa5j8ep3hfh2o278jt0bmvinu5s@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <184il0hcvljo98k04t2gn1v57172blit6b@4ax.com> <uabil092fbefvedp9qmm36bjcgar6d52i6@4ax.com> <10lj5hjqgv90v89@corp.supernews.com> <j07jl0t0qd5pf1euvamk59ksdgjaf9gkta@4ax.com> <cjc9lp$1eq0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <h3jjl0lj0nklv7e9rj0vi6co8cf9shhb1a@4ax.com> <cjf185$8aq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cjf3nm$pn6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cjf9g5$dnr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Wed, 29 Sep 2004 14:29:41 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>Someone could also propose a standard Function TRIM, but in similar
>circumstances J4 has been reluctant to specify a *standard* function where a
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>category of potentially-marketable functions.

How altruistic, but the time for a Cobol-specific library has passed.
In the OO world, a Cobol program has access to TONS of classes
targeted to other languages .. many of them free. 

A better idea would be creating libraries in Cobol for the use of
other languages. An astute marketer wouldn't mention that they're
written in Cobol. If they're good enough, the subsequent revelation
would give Cobol a boost.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-29T19:19:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409291819.70bd9a13@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <184il0hcvljo98k04t2gn1v57172blit6b@4ax.com> <uabil092fbefvedp9qmm36bjcgar6d52i6@4ax.com> <10lj5hjqgv90v89@corp.supernews.com> <j07jl0t0qd5pf1euvamk59ksdgjaf9gkta@4ax.com> <cjc9lp$1eq0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <h3jjl0lj0nklv7e9rj0vi6co8cf9shhb1a@4ax.com> <cjf185$8aq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cjf3nm$pn6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cjf9g5$dnr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <uhdml09pa5j8ep3hfh2o278jt0bmvinu5s@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> A better idea would be creating libraries in Cobol for the use of
> other languages. 

I very doubt that it would be a 'better' idea. One problem would be
supplying the complete run-time environment for Cobol.  In many cases
this may be a chargable item and more costly than any revenue for
selling the library.

A second issue is that the strength of Cobol comes from the writing of
the source code, not from the use of libraries.  Selling compiled code
is of no benefit or added value compared to other languages.

> An astute marketer wouldn't mention that they're
> written in Cobol. If they're good enough, the subsequent revelation
> would give Cobol a boost.

An astute marketer wouldn't waste his time on your silly ideas.
```

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> >I very doubt that it would be a 'better' idea. One problem would be
> >supplying the complete run-time environment for Cobol.  In many cases
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Fujitsu has no runtime fee.

That may be true, but the runtime is still several megabytes that has
to be loaded and installed on the target machine.

Also, while the runtime is available for free distribution, this is
_only_ licenced to developers who have bought the Fujitsu compiler. 
If one is selling compiled components to developers to redistribute
with _their_ products they _DO_NOT_ have a distribution license for
the run-time.

 
> >A second issue is that the strength of Cobol comes from the writing of
> >the source code, not from the use of libraries.  Selling compiled code
> >is of no benefit or added value compared to other languages.
> 
> Take a look at ComponentOne:

How many of them are written in Cobol ?

Or putting it another way: why would someone buy a component, similar
to the ones available from that site, that are written in Cobol rather
than one written in C#, Java, or assembler ?

What advantage to the component buyer is its Cobol origin ? What
special things will it do that are not in some other Component written
in another language ?

What, to the compiled component, does its Cobolness add ?  (other than
several megabytes of runtime where required).

I know what the advantage to _you_, Robert, as the programmer is: you
don't know any other language, but what would be the advantage to the
customers ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-30T11:43:25-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cjhk4d$207n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <184il0hcvljo98k04t2gn1v57172blit6b@4ax.com> <uabil092fbefvedp9qmm36bjcgar6d52i6@4ax.com> <10lj5hjqgv90v89@corp.supernews.com> <j07jl0t0qd5pf1euvamk59ksdgjaf9gkta@4ax.com> <cjc9lp$1eq0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <h3jjl0lj0nklv7e9rj0vi6co8cf9shhb1a@4ax.com> <cjf185$8aq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cjf3nm$pn6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cjf9g5$dnr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <uhdml09pa5j8ep3hfh2o278jt0bmvinu5s@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:uhdml09pa5j8ep3hfh2o278jt0bmvinu5s@4ax.com...

> How altruistic, but the time for a Cobol-specific library has passed.
> In the OO world, a Cobol program has access to TONS of classes
> targeted to other languages .. many of them free.

That presumes that your OO COBOL implementation happens to have been
designed to access those class libraries.  Maybe it has.  Maybe it hasn't.
Talk to your implementor.

> A better idea would be creating libraries in Cobol for the use of
> other languages. An astute marketer wouldn't mention that they're
> written in Cobol. If they're good enough, the subsequent revelation
> would give Cobol a boost.

I think that depends on the implementation.  To pick an example:  the
benefits of something like an "edited-display" class are rather greater in
the specific environments in which hardware support for the COBOL concept of
"edited data items" is provided than they are for environments in which the
likes of PICTURE handling is done entirely in software.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **Date:** 2004-09-29T21:57:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ha8ml0hdp5mlk6oi04csisuhh8qlncf996@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <184il0hcvljo98k04t2gn1v57172blit6b@4ax.com> <uabil092fbefvedp9qmm36bjcgar6d52i6@4ax.com> <10lj5hjqgv90v89@corp.supernews.com> <j07jl0t0qd5pf1euvamk59ksdgjaf9gkta@4ax.com> <cjc9lp$1eq0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <h3jjl0lj0nklv7e9rj0vi6co8cf9shhb1a@4ax.com> <cjf185$8aq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Wed, 29 Sep 2004 12:08:53 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >news:j07jl0t0qd5pf1euvamk59ksdgjaf9gkta@4ax.com...

>> Applications provide the definition, formal or informal, of their own
>> application data. In this case, the definition reasonably disallows
>> two consecutive spaces within a string.
>
>Who said?

John Locke, in "The Rights of Man".

>The way I see it, this is a good example of a Dodgsonesque definition for
>the term "string".

I wasn't writing satire.

>> >What about null-delimited strings?  Comma-delimited strings?
>Tab-delimited
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>"string data item" in COBOL.

Ok, all are handled by the STRING verb.

> > >Why not use "inspect function reverse ... tallying ... leading spaces"
>to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>The code I saw used an inline PERFORM to do that.   

Look again. It contained two solutions: one used a PERFORM loop and
the other, inspect reverse .. tallying.

>> >> ODO is the standard COBOL way to delimit a string.
>> >
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>matter, for what's proposed for "any-length elementary items".

Some compilers are smart enough to detect one-byte occurrences and
generate code  the same as reference modification. 

Given ALLOCATE and BASED in the 02 standard, don't we already have
any-length elementary items?

>> >Personally, I think the appropriate way to detect the position of the
>last
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>outweighs "fast" in my book.

The language cannot guarantee there won't be two consecutive spaces;
that has to come from the application designer. The language _could_
make it easy to get rid of them by allowing variable length operands
on INSPECT .. REPLACING. In the SQL language, it's trivially easy:
REPLACE(foo, ' '+' ', ' '). I do it in a trigger so the programmer
can't forget.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-30T11:31:25-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cjhjdt$1vri$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <184il0hcvljo98k04t2gn1v57172blit6b@4ax.com> <uabil092fbefvedp9qmm36bjcgar6d52i6@4ax.com> <10lj5hjqgv90v89@corp.supernews.com> <j07jl0t0qd5pf1euvamk59ksdgjaf9gkta@4ax.com> <cjc9lp$1eq0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <h3jjl0lj0nklv7e9rj0vi6co8cf9shhb1a@4ax.com> <cjf185$8aq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ha8ml0hdp5mlk6oi04csisuhh8qlncf996@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:ha8ml0hdp5mlk6oi04csisuhh8qlncf996@4ax.com...
> >> Applications provide the definition, formal or informal, of their own
> >> application data. In this case, the definition reasonably disallows
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> John Locke, in "The Rights of Man".

Writing sometime before 1704 on the nature of strings in COBOL applications?
How prescient of him!

> >The way I see it, this is a good example of a Dodgsonesque definition for
> >the term "string".
>
> I wasn't writing satire.

Who said you were?  I was referring to the quotation "When I use a word, it
means just what I choose it to mean -- neither more nor less."   String may
mean something like "a collection of one or more of some sort of words which
are separated from one another by one and only one space" *to you*, but I
would argue that it doesn't mean that *to me*, and I would also argue that
it doesn't mean that to very many folk!


> Ok, all are handled by the STRING verb.

That does not make STRING a *noun* in COBOL.

> > > >Why not use "inspect function reverse ... tallying ... leading
spaces"
> >to
> >> >get the number of trailing blanks?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> the other, inspect reverse .. tallying.

Didn't see it, hence "the code I saw".  Did I not write exactly what I
meant?

> Some compilers are smart enough to detect one-byte occurrences and
> generate code  the same as reference modification.

And some compilers aren't.

> Given ALLOCATE and BASED in the 02 standard, don't we already have
> any-length elementary items?

No, not really, not in the sense I'm talking about.

> The language cannot guarantee there won't be two consecutive spaces;
> that has to come from the application designer.

From memory, from the "The Miner" in "Beyond the Fringe":  "... this is
known as the 'bad' method of getting coal out of the mine ..." ...
Substitute "defining a string" for "getting coal out of the mine" ...

> The language _could_
> make it easy to get rid of them by allowing variable length operands
> on INSPECT .. REPLACING.

The language *can* get rid of multiple embedded spaces now, with relatively
straightforward code.

> In the SQL language, it's trivially easy:
> REPLACE(foo, ' '+' ', ' '). I do it in a trigger so the programmer
> can't forget.

Well, goody.  Doing it in a trigger is really helpful to the COBOL
programmer.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:02sol09hpge843hvb7d8nf56r04pg64q2b@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 30 Sep 2004 11:31:25 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Triggers (and other stored procedures) can be written in Cobol.

Well, goody.  Point me to a definition of a "stored procedure", and the
rules differentiating a "trigger" from other forms of "stored procedures",
in any COBOL standard, please.

As it stands, "Blivets (and other instantiated thingamabobs) can be written
in COBOL" is a statement that, in the contexts of COBOL programming and
standard COBOL terminology, has neither more nor less precise meaning than
yours.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-28T14:55:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409281355.29f2e551@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <184il0hcvljo98k04t2gn1v57172blit6b@4ax.com> <uabil092fbefvedp9qmm36bjcgar6d52i6@4ax.com> <10lj5hjqgv90v89@corp.supernews.com> <j07jl0t0qd5pf1euvamk59ksdgjaf9gkta@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> In C, it is necessary to TRIM before STRCAT; 

It is only necessary to trim(), if it is not already trimmed, which is
is by most processes.

> in Cobol, it is not. That's what I meant when I said
> Cobol has better string handling.

> It works when strings were edited to insure two consecutive spaces do
> not apprear inside. 

So it _is_ necessary in Cobol to 'edit to insure ...' before
concatenating.

It seems to me that trimming excess trailing spaces is a reversable
process that has no effect on the data, while changing the spacing
between words is not reversable and changes the data in a way that the
user may not want.

I often find that, for example, descriptions and names and addresses
are entered by users deliberately with extra spacing.  Who gave you
permission to change that.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-28T18:08:38-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10ljo9p2sikrl26@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <184il0hcvljo98k04t2gn1v57172blit6b@4ax.com> <uabil092fbefvedp9qmm36bjcgar6d52i6@4ax.com> <10lj5hjqgv90v89@corp.supernews.com> <j07jl0t0qd5pf1euvamk59ksdgjaf9gkta@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:j07jl0t0qd5pf1euvamk59ksdgjaf9gkta@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 28 Sep 2004 12:48:17 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[121 more quoted lines elided]…
> Strings can have one space between words.

In C terminology, a string may have two or more
consecutive spaces.

> >It seems to me that the STRING *statement* allows COBOL
> >programmers to make any set of consecutive characters into
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> that location minus one?

A sending item in a STRING statement may be an elementary
numeric data item (integer, no 'P' in its PICTURE clause); as
such, it has no trailing spaces, nor can the OCCURS clause
apply to such an item. That is why one cannot 'just use Occurs
Depending On'.

> >Therefore, the questions of necessity and desirability must be
> >left to the individual programmer.
>
> Occam's Razor: the best solution is the simplest.

[Occam's razor. See Ockham's razor. -- A Dictionary
of Philosophy (ADOP)]

[Ockham's razor. The principle of ontological economy,
usually formulated as 'Entities are not to be multiplied
beyond necessity' (Entia non sunt multiplicanda praeter
necessitatem). These actual words are not in fact to be
found in the extant works of William of Ockham. -- ADOP]

Mr Wagner, if a programmer chooses, of necessity, to
'trim' consecutive characters by using a NUL character,
why should I disagree. To borrow from Kant, sometimes
a real NUL character is better than the possibility of one
(using ODO).

> >Now, Mr Wagner, the original poster wrote: "Is there any TRIM
> >type function to strip away the spaces of a Table Element to then
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> ODO is the standard COBOL way to delimit a string.

Unless that 'string' is an elementary numeric data item?

> > However, Mr Reymond provided
> >a means to do so by choosing to delimit all such strings by a
> >method used by the C progamming language.
>
> Correct. My point was that's not necessary in Cobol.

But, may be found necessary for a particular application.

> >Mr Wagner, how does stopping at the first occurrence of two
> >consecutive spaces guarantee that only the 'superfluous or
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> not apprear inside.

Such as, "replacing first ' ' [two spaces] by low-values"?
[Edited to delete one space.]

> >What if there is only one trailing space that is 'superfluous or
> >dispensable', how does your suggestion guarantee trimming of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> result is the same as doing it 'right'.

Mr Wagner, that may be true, in the particular case supplied
by the original poster; but not in the general case as suggested
by your use of 'Cobol strings'.

> >Finally, Mr Wagner, of what benefit is it to do something
> >'FASTER in Cobol' without assurance that it will be done
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> opposed to inferior  techniques imported from other languages.

Again, Mr Wagner, where is 'standard Cobol string handling'
defined?
```

```
On Tue, 28 Sep 2004 18:08:38 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>

…[147 more quoted lines elided]…
>Depending On'.

Where'd that come from? The question made no reference to numeric data
items. 

>> >Therefore, the questions of necessity and desirability must be
>> >left to the individual programmer.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>found in the extant works of William of Ockham. -- ADOP]

I too took high-school Latin. I'm not impressed by classics. 

>Mr Wagner, if a programmer chooses, of necessity, to
>'trim' consecutive characters by using a NUL character,
>why should I disagree. To borrow from Kant, sometimes
>a real NUL character is better than the possibility of one
>(using ODO).

The results of programming language comparison of string handling are
in. C finished last; Cobol finished near the top. 

>> >Now, Mr Wagner, the original poster wrote: "Is there any TRIM
>> >type function to strip away the spaces of a Table Element to then
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Unless that 'string' is an elementary numeric data item?

>> > However, Mr Reymond provided
>> >a means to do so by choosing to delimit all such strings by a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>But, may be found necessary for a particular application.

No. This is called Special Pleading. The burden of proof is on you to
show why your apocryphal particular application is special.

>> >Finally, Mr Wagner, of what benefit is it to do something
>> >'FASTER in Cobol' without assurance that it will be done
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>defined?

It's defined under the rules for STRING and UNSTRING in the standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-28T22:53:00-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10lk8va1rqv0b63@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <184il0hcvljo98k04t2gn1v57172blit6b@4ax.com> <uabil092fbefvedp9qmm36bjcgar6d52i6@4ax.com> <10lj5hjqgv90v89@corp.supernews.com> <j07jl0t0qd5pf1euvamk59ksdgjaf9gkta@4ax.com> <10ljo9p2sikrl26@corp.supernews.com> <mk2kl05rv052spjlrvcc4bj8vp2ulgc4uc@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:mk2kl05rv052spjlrvcc4bj8vp2ulgc4uc@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 28 Sep 2004 18:08:38 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >> >>Is there any TRIM type function to strip away the spaces of a
Table
> >> >Element
> >> >> >>to then be able to use the string command.
…[85 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >> >With the first version, we have been experimenting some problems
with
> >> >> >the length of the string. We had to reduce 32000 to a lower amount
due
> >> >> >to an error during execution.
> >> >>
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
> items.

Mr Wagner, you mentioned 'Cobol strings' and 'the string verb'.
One cannot discuss the STRING statement without the
possiblity of elementary numeric data items. [See below]

> >> >Therefore, the questions of necessity and desirability must be
> >> >left to the individual programmer.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> I too took high-school Latin. I'm not impressed by classics.

Mr Wagner, I never took Latin. I find it interesting that you
seem to find support in that by which you are not impressed;
else why mention Occam's razor.

> >Mr Wagner, if a programmer chooses, of necessity, to
> >'trim' consecutive characters by using a NUL character,
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> There weren't any numeric data items in the problem.

That, Mr Wagner, depends entirely upon what one sees as
the problem.

> >> > However, Mr Reymond provided
> >> >a means to do so by choosing to delimit all such strings by a
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> show why your apocryphal particular application is special.

Dr Wayne Dyer once said, "This is my way. What is your way?
There is no *the* way."

Mr Wagner, if there is, indeed, 'no *the* way', then how can
any 'way' be apocryphal. It seems to me that the important
criteria is correctness. Can you show that stopping at two
consecutive spaces always works and that using a C-style
string always fails? Or, is it that each works under some
conditions and fails in others?

If the use of C-style strings provides correct results, then
how could they not be sanctioned. Perhaps because it is
not 'your way'?

[apocryphal: of doubtful sanction; uncanonical. -- RHCD]

> >> >Finally, Mr Wagner, of what benefit is it to do something
> >> >'FASTER in Cobol' without assurance that it will be done
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> It's defined under the rules for STRING and UNSTRING in the standard.

Then, Mr Wagner, 'Cobol strings' must include elementary
numeric data items.

FDIS, page 536, STRING, 14.8.39.2 Syntax rules
"7) Where identifier-1 or identifier-2 is an elementary numeric
      data item, it shall be described as an integer without the
      symbol 'P' in its picture character-string."

Elementary numeric data items cannot be described with ODO.
Therefore, your claim that 'ODO is the standard COBOL way
to delimit a string.' is without support.
```

```
>> >> > However, Mr Reymond provided
>> >> >a means to do so by choosing to delimit all such strings by a
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>conditions and fails in others?

You did not show how this "particular application" warrants special
techniques. In fact, it's not special, it is plain vanilla string
concatenation. Examples of use: first/last name on an address label,
city/state/ZIP, formatting a CSV record. People program these things
routinely and I've never seen them insert delimiters in the inputs
before using STRING. It's not necessary in Cobol. 

>If the use of C-style strings provides correct results, then
>how could they not be sanctioned. Perhaps because it is
>not 'your way'?

Because it's not the Cobol way.

>> >Again, Mr Wagner, where is 'standard Cobol string handling'
>> >defined?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>numeric data items.

I can't think of a practical use for stringing integers. Maybe it was
included for people who erroneously use pic 9 for things like part
numbers and ZIP codes.
```

```
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…

only a question and an example from which any number
of 'applications' may be extrapolated. This includes the
possibility of an 'application' processing strings where
any such string may contain two consecutive spaces.
To deny this possibility is to risk an error of omission.

> >If the use of C-style strings provides correct results, then
> >how could they not be sanctioned. Perhaps because it is
> >not 'your way'?

work and ways that fail each of which may be implemented
in COBOL.

> >> >Again, Mr Wagner, where is 'standard Cobol string handling'
> >> >defined?
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> numbers and ZIP codes.

Practical use or not, my point in including elementary numeric
data items was to refute your claim that 'ODO is the standard
COBOL way to delimit a string.' A point which you did not
address.
```

```
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

W. Wayt Gibbs, "Software's Chronic Crisis," Scientific
American, September 1994, page 91, "Because they
mimic only the outward behavior of systems, prototypes
are of little help in spotting logical inconsistencies in a
system's design. 'The vast majority of errors in large-scale
software are errors of omission,' notes Lazlo A. Belady,
director of Mitsubishi Electric Reasearch Laboratory.
And models do not make it easier to detect bugs once
a design is committed to code."
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-28T15:14:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409281414.541e6218@posting.google.com>`

```
Alain Reymond <> wrote

>            perform varying i from ls-len by -1
>            until ((i < 1) or (lnk-texte(i:1) <> " "))
>                move x'00' to lnk-texte(i:1)
>            end-perform

I tend to use ' until ... > space ' so that any existing nul, tab,
newline etc are also stripped.  But I am using ASCII and this would be
different for EBCDIC.
```

#### ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-28T08:29:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0vydnXX80IOB-sTcRVn-uA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no>`

```
D wrote:
> Using Merant Micro Focus Net Express version 3.1
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any suggestions ??

Assuming you'll want even more:

Move 1 to Out-Pointer
Move space to Output-Table
Perform varying I from 1 by 1 until I > Num-Table
   Unstring AT-Entry(I) delimited space into Output-Table
      with pointer Out-Pointer
   End-Perform.

You could gussie-up the above code by adding a space after each unstring 
operation, viz:

Add 1 to Out-Pointer
  Move space to Output-Table(Out-pointer:1)

taking care that you don't imbed two consecutive spaces.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-28T12:36:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409281136.40b62bd4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <0vydnXX80IOB-sTcRVn-uA@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote 

> Assuming you'll want even more:
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>    End-Perform.

No, that won't work.  On an UNSTRING the WITH POINTER is applied to
the field being unstrung (AT-Entry(I)), not the output.  This means
that the first at-entry will be put into output-table and then part of
the 2nd will overwrite the start of output-table.

You probably meant to use STRING.
 
> You could gussie-up the above code by adding a space after each unstring 
> operation, viz:
> 
> Add 1 to Out-Pointer
>   Move space to Output-Table(Out-pointer:1)

You only need to increment the pointer, it is already a space.
 
> taking care that you don't imbed two consecutive spaces.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ErmdnYYH7rz3VMTcRVn-ig@giganews.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>> taking care that you don't imbed two consecutive spaces.

Slap. I would have figured that out after, at most, five compiles.
```

#### ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-28T19:32:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no>`

```
"D" <all4aide@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
news:4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no...
> Using Merant Micro Focus Net Express version 3.1
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any suggestions ??


I have gone back to the original post to respond.  I *know* that someone already 
provided this (or a similar) answer, but somehow it seems to have gotten lost - 
or I am misunderstanding something.  Although the original message mentions 
"TRIM" - it just is NOT needed in standard COBOL.  I have tried the following 
portion of code and I think it does EXACTLY what the original request was asking 
for.  Could someone tell me what is wrong with it (given the initial 
"specification")?

NOTE:
  I have some comments at the end indicating POTENTIAL problems - but not 
problems given the original request:

Code (snippet) follows:

       Data Division.
        Working-Storage Section.
       01  Text-Input.
           05  Pic X(10)    Value "This".
           05  Pic X(10) Value "and".
           05  Pic X(10) Value "That".
           05  Pic X(20) Value Spaces.
       01  Text-Table redefines Text-Input.
           05  Each-Elem
               Occurs 5 Times
               Indexed by Ind.
               10  Elem  Pic X(10).
       01  String-Output        Pic X(50).
       Procedure Division.
        Mainline.
           Move Spaces to String-Output
           String Elem (1)  Delimited by Spaces
                  Space    Delimited by Size
                  Elem (2) Delimited by Spaces
                  Space  Delimited by Size
                  Elem (3) Delimited by Spaces
                  Space  Delimited by Size
                  Elem (4) Delimited by Spaces
                  Space  Delimited by Size
                  Elem (5) Delimited by Spaces
               Into String-Output
               On Overflow
                   Display "Input too big"
           End-String


****

Comments (restrictions):
  the following would require additional coding - but don't seem (to me) to be 
relevant given the original request:

1) If any individual element has "internal" spaces (i.e. is "multiple" words), 
then this would not work
2) If one of the "middle" elements is blank (spaces) but a following item is 
not, then two - rather than one - space would appear in the output
3) If any element had leading spaces, then this would not work

Each of the above could be solved - but doesn't need to be as I read the 
original "request".
```

```
it myself.

That post was nearly 24-hours ago. All the remaining
discussion is usenet.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-29T09:02:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cjema0$sl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <wFp6d.199030$yk.32938@news.easynews.com>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:wFp6d.199030$yk.32938@news.easynews.com...
> "Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
> news:br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com...
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> If the goal was to do something with C facilities, then why not just tell
them
> to program in C?
>
> Answer - because the original poster was asking how to do something *IN
COBOL*.
>
> Again,  as I posted earlier today - given the limited information in the
> original post, the standard COBOL STRING statement does what was asked
for.  Why
> use anything else (anything more complex)?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

And given the possibility of embedded spaces within the individual fields,
INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE TALLYING can provide the number of trailing spaces
in each field, in which case the sources for the STRING become reference
modifications of the field.

With the advent of user-defined functions in '02 COBOL, and presuming the
availability of a compliant compiler, I would think the preferred
methodology would be to build such a function and use it in this case, and
to lobby the particular compiler vendor to make such a compliant compiler
available (or at least to make this feature available as an extension to
their '85 offering!).

Incidentally, the reason I prefer INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE TALLYING over a
PERFORM decrementing an index to find the last nonblank character in a data
item is that the INSPECT allows for the possibility that the implementation
might actually support a backwards scan for the construct.  The more general
 PERFORM case would present enormous if not insurmountable barriers to the
detection of all contexts in which that efficient code might be use.  Absent
such hardware or other implementation support, the two might well produce
the same code, but I'd still rather rely on the single INSPECT statement
rather than the PERFORM even if what's being PERFORMed is inline for the
purpose of detecting the location of the last nonblank character.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
On Wed, 29 Sep 2004 09:02:08 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>Incidentally, the reason I prefer INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE TALLYING over a
>PERFORM decrementing an index to find the last nonblank character in a data
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>purpose of detecting the location of the last nonblank character.

Intel (IA32) has such an instruction in 'repe scasb'. Realia uses it
as you described, also in SEARCH where the size is one byte and the
only WHEN compares equal or not equal to a single value.
```

```
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> (That's a link to Google Groups - just shortened it so it wouldn't
wrap...)

No, I didn't, but I would contend that's *one* case in *one* environment.
Being a compiler-type person, I still prefer the use of INSPECT.  I don't
much like the explicit double MOVE in the Rick and Judson cases, though I
grant INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE is likely to end up with a behind-the-scenes
MOVE unless the hardware and the implementation support some sort of reverse
scan.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
> news:PKG6d.1970$Za.87@fe40.usenetserver.com...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> scan.

I can't remember if I ran that on the 2200 or not.  We're fielding our 3 
1/2 year project to the first base this week, so spare time is, well, 
hard to come by.  :)  I'll try running it again.

What we used to have was an 80-character output line, defined something 
like...

01  Traf-OutBytes       Pic X(80).
01  Traf-OutBytes-X Redefines Traf-OutBytes.
     03  Traf-Out-Elt Occurs 80 Times
                        Indexed by Traf-Out-Ind
                          Pic X(01).

The application programs use it to do traffic.  The traffic handler had 
a nice go-to loop to start at 80 and look backwards to find the first 
space.  I changed that to use a Function Reverse - but, after seeing 
those timings, I'm considering changing it back!  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-28T23:20:07-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10lkahs4mc4qp82@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 28 Sep 2004 21:00:24 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> strcpy() and strcat().

Perhaps the original poster was seeking a BASIC-like
solution. Mr Wagner, I checked some of my sources and
find no trim() function in C. I did find a RTRIM$ Function
in QuickBasic 4.5 that 'Returns a string with the trailing
(right-hand) spaces removed.' The function may exist in
other BASIC implementations.

> Calling C functions from Cobol is trivial. Just do it. You don't need
> anything special.

Perhaps using a facility from BASIC is not so trivial.

Both C-style and Pascal-style strings are rather
straight-forward to implement in COBOL. BASIC-style
strings may be implemented in COBOL; but there are,
as I see it, problems with garbage collection and
compaction.
```

- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <10lkahs4mc4qp82@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Tue, 28 Sep 2004 23:20:07 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>find no trim() function in C. 

It's not in the ANSI standard, but is in most commercial class
libraries.

// I compiled and tested this string class on Linux (Redhat 7.1) and 
// MS Windows Borland C++ version 5.2 (win32). This should also work
// using MS Visual C++ compiler

String trim();
String rtrim();

http://oopweb.com/CPP/Documents/CPPHOWTO/Volume/C++Programming-HOWTO-6.html

http://msdn.microsoft.com/library/default.asp?url=/library/en-us/cpref/html/frlrfsystemstringclasstrimtopic1.asp

Another option is to do it in SQL:

exec SQL select rtrim(:foo)+char(0) into :foo from dual; end-exec.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-28T22:51:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409282151.11f3e79d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> Since the original poster was seeking a C-like solution, 

I don't see any indication that the poster wanted a 'C-like solution',
this seems to be another example of you starting with a conclusion and
then inventing facts to match that.

> why not
> advise him to call the C trim() function? Perhaps followed by calls to
> strcpy() and strcat(). 
 
Because he specifically wanted to use STRING - this _was_ in his
message.

> Calling C functions from Cobol is trivial. Just do it. You don't need
> anything special.

Actually that is very system specific and may in some cases simply not
be possible, in others it may require run times to be built and/or
libraries linked, it may also require extensions such as 'BY VALUE'
(depending on the routines) or special forms of the CALL statement,
all of which would qualify as 'something special'.

Of course on other Cobol systems it may be trivial.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-29T16:36:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e7nll0hbcf8ikful46u566vvhmtlak68up@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409282151.11f3e79d@posting.google.com>`

```
On 28 Sep 2004 22:51:17 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>then inventing facts to match that.

You're right, the original poster didn't ask for a C-like solution.
But the post to which I responded offered one.

>> why not
>> advise him to call the C trim() function? Perhaps followed by calls to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>message.

Which is why I said in my first posting to the thread: " It is not
necessary nor desirable to turn Cobol strings into C strings in order
to concatenate them. It can be done with one line of code:  the string
verb."

>> Calling C functions from Cobol is trivial. Just do it. You don't need
>> anything special.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>all of which would qualify as 'something special'.

That was in the Good Old Days. Now, most Cobol compilers use the C
calling convention by default (IBM, Micro Focus) or provide it as an
option. 

I misspoke when I said CALL. I should have said INVOKE, because
rtrim() is in the C++ String class library. Linkage conventions in the
OO world are compatible across languages that compile to machine code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-09-29T12:57:44-04:00

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> >> Calling C functions from Cobol is trivial. Just do it. You don't need
> >> anything special.

> I misspoke when I said CALL. I should have said INVOKE, because
> rtrim() is in the C++ String class library. Linkage conventions in the
> OO world are compatible across languages that compile to machine code.

In that case you also misspoke when you said 'trivial' and 'nothing
special', now he needs an OO Cobol compiler _and_ a C++ development
environment and needs to link them together.

I would also like to know how you would intend to deal with C++'s name
mangling to get the correct method name ?  Or have you stepped, once
again, well beyond where you have a clue what is going on ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-29T23:48:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8shml0t1kvmj8iumo7ude664o2d3dol1af@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409282151.11f3e79d@posting.google.com> <e7nll0hbcf8ikful46u566vvhmtlak68up@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409291432.7b45e7ba@posting.google.com>`

```
On 29 Sep 2004 15:32:02 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>environment and needs to link them together.

Micro Focus Server Express comes, by default, with OO. Solaris comes,
by default, with C++ libraries. 

>I would also like to know how you would intend to deal with C++'s name
>mangling to get the correct method name ?  Or have you stepped, once
>again, well beyond where you have a clue what is going on ?

Name mangling is used when you overload an existing method. No
standard  C++ classes have methods named TRIM or RTRIM, so no name
mangling is involved.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-29T23:13:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409292213.37ed218d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409282151.11f3e79d@posting.google.com> <e7nll0hbcf8ikful46u566vvhmtlak68up@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409291432.7b45e7ba@posting.google.com> <8shml0t1kvmj8iumo7ude664o2d3dol1af@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> >> >> Calling C functions from Cobol is trivial. Just do it. You don't need
> >> >> anything special.
> 
> Micro Focus Server Express comes, by default, with OO. Solaris comes,
> by default, with C++ libraries. 

Are you now saying that he needs Solaris ?
 
> >I would also like to know how you would intend to deal with C++'s name
> >mangling to get the correct method name ?  Or have you stepped, once
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> mangling is involved.

Clue stick number 2: name mangling is _always_ involved.

It seems that C++ and name mangling is another whole area that you are
completely clueless about, yet will probably pontificate at length.

Method names in one class are independent of method names in unrelated
classes, so your 'no standard .. methods named ..' is quite
irrelevant, name mangling will happen whether this is true or not.

In C++ name mangling happens with all methods and all versions
(overloads) of these methods even if the method is not actually
overloaded (in fact the compiler cannot tell whether a particular
method is or will be overloaded later by a derived class so your 'no
.. methods named' is entirely incompetent).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-30T18:22:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fnbol0tifjo4ihd13hsn0664hveqer97j6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409282151.11f3e79d@posting.google.com> <e7nll0hbcf8ikful46u566vvhmtlak68up@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409291432.7b45e7ba@posting.google.com> <8shml0t1kvmj8iumo7ude664o2d3dol1af@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409292213.37ed218d@posting.google.com>`

```
On 29 Sep 2004 23:13:18 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

>> >I would also like to know how you would intend to deal with C++'s name
>> >mangling to get the correct method name ?  Or have you stepped, once
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>completely clueless about, yet will probably pontificate at length.

You get around mangling by writing a wrapper:

extern "C" char* trim(char*);
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-30T16:10:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409301510.374f3961@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <Q_i6d.2276817$6p.384811@news.easynews.com> <10lk2btopr83m45@corp.supernews.com> <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409282151.11f3e79d@posting.google.com> <e7nll0hbcf8ikful46u566vvhmtlak68up@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409291432.7b45e7ba@posting.google.com> <8shml0t1kvmj8iumo7ude664o2d3dol1af@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409292213.37ed218d@posting.google.com> <fnbol0tifjo4ihd13hsn0664hveqer97j6@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> >Clue stick number 2: name mangling is _always_ involved.
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> extern "C" char* trim(char*);

cluestick number 3:  that is actually the complete inverse of what is
needed.

I was actually waiting for you to make that blunder and show further
your complete lack of understanding of the whole subject. You
obviously googled and found this without having a clue about what it
actually did or why.

The extern "C" declaration allows a C++ program to access a standard C
routine by not mangling the specified name.  That is, the C (not C++)
routine is in an external library and does not have a mangled name.

What you were requiring was the complete opposite: accessing a mangled
method name in a C++ class from a C or C compatible call (based on
your example).

You cannot put the 'extern "C"' in the compile of the class and get it
to not mangle the method name - all method names are mangled, and in
any case that is not what the extern "C" is saying to do.

What would be required is, in the _Cobol_ program a directive of the
form:

        extern "C++" char * trim(char*);

which would mangle the name trim() for you.  But there is isn't one.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Functions in MF

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-09-29T17:01:37+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<cjepph0ehl@news1.newsguy.com>`

```

In article <br4kl01ul70eofiuq2k91me7sqk2fsk5ba@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> writes:
> 
> Since the original poster was seeking a C-like solution, why not
> advise him to call the C trim() function?

Perhaps because there is no "trim" function in C?  If you believe
otherwise, feel free to cite the relevant portion of any version
of ISO 9899.

(There's no "trim" function in POSIX / SUS or in MSVC, either.)
```

### Re: String Functions in MF

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-28T12:05:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cjccl2$1gp3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <184il0hcvljo98k04t2gn1v57172blit6b@4ax.com> <3r8il0lavru86r2dlk53p2jors0bnddc7f@4ax.com> <osbjl0pch428mkfv2b01pdcgaju1srekrj@4ax.com>`

```
<Alain Reymond> wrote in message
news:osbjl0pch428mkfv2b01pdcgaju1srekrj@4ax.com...
> >>You can also write another code which will do the same
> >>trim.cbl
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
> Regards.

2002 COBOL added the LENGTH and BYTE-LENGTH functions, and on by-reference
CALLs the called program or user-defined function could avoid overwriting B
(or avoid running off the end of A, which has a rather different, and more
immediate, result on some systems).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

## Thread: COBOL, Oracle & XML — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### COBOL, Oracle & XML

- **From:** ctaliercio@yahoo.com (Chris)
- **Date:** 2004-09-28T12:20:34-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cc07162.0409281120.207d1262@posting.google.com>`

```
Does anyone have any sample code they could point me to that
demonstrates how to use the XML features of Oracle within COBOL?

I would like to select data from our database into an XML format that
will then be passed to a remote process. I would also like to take the
response from that process (again in XML format) and insert it back
into my database.

I was thinking that using Oracle's built in XML processing
(XML_SYSGEN, etc) would be the best way to accomplish this, but I
cannot find any "practical" examples of how you would accomplish this.

Here's the pertinent info:

OS - HP-UX 11i
Oracle - 9i (9.2.0.4.0)
Cobol - MF OC 4.2 or MF Server Express 2.2 (both available)

Thanks in advance for any input!

Chris
```

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message news:...
> "D" <all4aide@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
> news:4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>
 I have gone back to the original post to respond.  I *know* that someone 
already provided this (or a similar) answer, but somehow it seems to have gotten 
lost - or I am misunderstanding something.  Although the original message 
mentions  "TRIM" - it just is NOT needed in standard COBOL.  I have tried the 
following portion of code and I think it does EXACTLY what the original request 
was asking for.  Could someone tell me what is wrong with it (given the initial 
"specification")?

 NOTE:
  I have some comments at the end indicating POTENTIAL problems - but not 
problems given the original request:

 Code (snippet) follows:

       Data Division.
        Working-Storage Section.
       01  Text-Input.
           05  Pic X(10)    Value "This".
           05  Pic X(10) Value "and".
           05  Pic X(10) Value "That".
           05  Pic X(20) Value Spaces.
       01  Text-Table redefines Text-Input.
           05  Each-Elem
               Occurs 5 Times
               Indexed by Ind.
               10  Elem  Pic X(10).
       01  String-Output        Pic X(50).
       Procedure Division.
        Mainline.
           Move Spaces to String-Output
           String Elem (1)  Delimited by Spaces
                  Space    Delimited by Size
                  Elem (2) Delimited by Spaces
                  Space  Delimited by Size
                  Elem (3) Delimited by Spaces
                  Space  Delimited by Size
                  Elem (4) Delimited by Spaces
                  Space  Delimited by Size
                  Elem (5) Delimited by Spaces
               Into String-Output
               On Overflow
                   Display "Input too big"
           End-String


 ****

 Comments (restrictions):
  the following would require additional coding - but don't seem (to me) to be 
relevant given the original request:

 1) If any individual element has "internal" spaces (i.e. is "multiple" words), 
then this would not work
 2) If one of the "middle" elements is blank (spaces) but a following item is 
not, then two - rather than one - space would appear in the output
 3) If any element had leading spaces, then this would not work

 Each of the above could be solved - but doesn't need to be as I read the 
original "request".

 -- 
 Bill Klein
> wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
>
```

## Thread: String Functions in MF — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: String Functions in MF

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-29T00:23:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cqpjl0l2ddsd0p8g0dp5maiistukcvl94v@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4E_5d.124061$%S.29555@pd7tw2no> <184il0hcvljo98k04t2gn1v57172blit6b@4ax.com> <3r8il0lavru86r2dlk53p2jors0bnddc7f@4ax.com> <osbjl0pch428mkfv2b01pdcgaju1srekrj@4ax.com>`

```
On Tue, 28 Sep 2004 21:00:04 +0200, Alain Reymond <> wrote:

>>>You can also write another code which will do the same 
>>>trim.cbl
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

>No. What I need is a pointer to the string passed to the procedure.
>That is why I need only a pic x and not more in the called procedure.
>Using this system, you are not limited to a precise length. You can
>use the same procedure with a string of pic x(80), pic x(2500) or
>whatever you want.

By default, Cobol passes pointers to all parameter items. Call using
by reference address of AT-Entry (1) is unnecessarily complex. You
could have accomplished the same with call using AT-Entry (1).

>That is why it is necessary to precise the length of the string you
>want to trim. 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>you will override the 10 first chars of b, which is not the aim...

Bugs like that gave pointers a bad reputation, as being 'too
complicated'.

>But you can easely trim a or c without caring about their respective
>defined length - passing it with length of... If you change the length
>of c for any reason there is no impact on the code.

Correct. Another solution is prototypes. Did you know Cobol has
(optional) prototypes?
```

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

<a id="t-f43a2977ed"></a>
## Thread: DD DUMMY an output file? — 1 this month / 10 total

### DD DUMMY an output file?

- **From:** "Ron S" <NoSpam@SpamStopper.org>
- **Date:** 2004-09-30T22:28:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yMWdnbdoFIdQU8HcRVn_vA@giganews.com>`

```
In the Enterprise COBOL for z/OS 3.3 manual on page 368 there is a statement
regarding OPEN OUTPUT. It says:

Do not specify OUTPUT for files that:
Are defined with a DD dummy card. Unpredictable results can occur.

Huh? I've used DD DUMMY on output files for 25 years and I've never had
unpredictable results. The program always runs and writes to DUMMY, ie. no where.
What are they talking about?
```

---

<a id="t-d280019548"></a>
## Thread: CICS CEMT — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### CICS CEMT

- **From:** "ts" <tomstack@cox.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-30T22:03:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iy57d.4663$uY3.2995@fed1read03>`

```
We just created a new Version 5 tx-series region.  This new region seems to
hang up for all the users if someone uses CEMT or CEDF or uses the IBM
distributed debugger.   Usually CEMT was never a problem for hanging
everyone, and the debugger would only hang you if you got on the same task
in windows2000.  This region is different then all our others because it is
hooked to oracle and not DB2.

What did we miss in the intallation of the region?

-COBOL is still the future!
```
